metadata.source: Google Professional Cloud Architect Official Sample Questions
metadata.notes: Use these as (1) seed questions in the tutor and (2) style examples for LLM question generation

questions.question: Anonymous users from all over the world access a public health information website hosted in an on-premises EHR data center. The servers that host this website are older, and users are complaining about sluggish response times. There has also been a recent increase of distributed denial-of-service attacks toward the website. The attacks always come from the same IP address ranges. EHR management has identified the public health information website as an easy, low risk application to migrate to Google Cloud. You need to improve access latency and provide a security solution that will prevent the denial-of-service traffic from entering your Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network. What should you do?

choices.A: Containerize the application and move it into Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE). Create a GKE service to expose the pods within the cluster, and set up a GKE network policy.
choices.B: Deploy an external HTTP(S) load balancer, configure Google Cloud Armor, and move the application onto Compute Engine virtual machines.
choices.C: Containerize the application and move it into Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE). Create an internal load balancer to expose the pods outside the cluster, and configure Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) for access.
choices.D: Deploy an external HTTP(S) load balancer, configure VPC firewall rules, and move the applications onto Compute Engine virtual machines.

questions.explanation: B is right because an external HTTP(S) load balancer is the standard GCP product for serving a public website globally, and Cloud Armor adds DDoS and WAF protection. The GKE options are wrong because GKE is unnecessary for a simple public website, C uses an internal load balancer/IAP which are for private access, and D lacks Cloud Armor and still relies only on VPC firewall rules.

questions.question: EHR needs a private, high-bandwidth connection between their on-premises data center and Google Cloud for their primary patient data systems. Their data center is in a colocation facility. What type of interconnect should they provision?

choices.A: Provision a Dedicated Interconnect connection.
choices.B: Provision a Partner Interconnect connection.
choices.C: Provision Carrier Peering.
choices.D: Provision a new Internet connection.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Partner Interconnect is designed for private, high-bandwidth connectivity when your data center is in a colocation facility and you want to connect through a service provider. Dedicated Interconnect requires a direct physical cross-connect, Carrier Peering is for public Google services rather than private VPC access, and the internet is not private or reliable enough for this requirement.

questions.question: One of EHR's healthcare customers is an internationally renowned research and hospital facility. Many of their patients are well-known public personalities. The hospital requires that patient information stored in Cloud Storage buckets not leave the geographic areas in which the buckets are hosted. You need to ensure that information stored in Cloud Storage buckets in the 'europe-west2' region does not leave that area. What should you do?

choices.A: Enable Virtual Private Cloud Service Controls, and create a service perimeter around the Cloud Storage resources.
choices.B: Create an access control list (ACL) that limits access to the bucket to authorized users only, and apply it to the buckets in the 'europe-west2' region.
choices.C: Assign the Identity and Access Management (IAM) 'storage.objectViewer' role only to users and service accounts that need to use the data.
choices.D: Encrypt the data in the application on-premises before the data is stored in the 'europe-west2' region.

questions.explanation: A is right because VPC Service Controls creates a service perimeter around Cloud Storage to reduce data exfiltration risk for highly sensitive data. ACLs and IAM in B and C only manage authorization, and D encrypts data but does not prevent authorized insiders or services from moving data out.

questions.question: The EHR sales employees are a remote-based workforce that travels to different locations to do their jobs. Regardless of their location, the sales employees need to access web-based sales tools located in the EHR data center. EHR is retiring their current Virtual Private Network (VPN) infrastructure, and you need to move the web-based sales tools to a BeyondCorp access model. Each sales employee has a Google Workspace account and uses that account for single sign-on (SSO). What should you do?

choices.A: Create a Google group for the sales tool application, and upgrade that group to a security group.
choices.B: Deploy an external HTTP(S) load balancer and create a custom Cloud Armor policy for the sales tool application.
choices.C: Create an Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) connector that points to the sales tool application.
choices.D: For every sales employee who needs access to the sales tool application, give their Google Workspace user account the predefined AppEngine Viewer role.

questions.explanation: C is correct because Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) lets remote users securely access web applications from anywhere without needing a VPN or location-based setup. A is just group management, B is about protecting public endpoints rather than user access control, and D is unrelated because App Engine Viewer is not the right permission model for arbitrary web tools.

questions.question: You are the data compliance officer for Mountkirk Games and must protect customers' personally identifiable information (PII). Mountkirk Games wants to make sure they can generate anonymized usage reports about their new game and delete PII data after a specific period of time. The solution should have minimal cost. You need to ensure compliance while meeting business and technical requirements. What should you do?

choices.A: Write a Cloud Logging filter to export specific date ranges to Pub/Sub.
choices.B: Archive audit logs in BigQuery, and generate reports using Google Data Studio.
choices.C: Archive user logs on a locally attached persistent disk, and cat them to a text file for auditing.
choices.D: Archive audit logs in Cloud Storage, and manually generate reports.

questions.explanation: B is right because storing audit logs in BigQuery makes it easy to run queries and generate anonymized usage reports at scale using reporting tools such as Looker Studio. The other options are wrong because Pub/Sub and persistent disks are not suitable long-term analytics stores, and Cloud Storage with manual reporting is too cumbersome for compliance reporting.

questions.question: You need to implement Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) Service Controls for Mountkirk Games. Mountkirk Games wants to allow Cloud Shell usage by its developers. Developers should not have full access to managed services. You need to balance these conflicting goals with Mountkirk Games' business requirements. What should you do?

choices.A: Prioritize VPC Service Controls implementation over Cloud Shell usage for the entire platform.
choices.B: Create a service perimeter around only the projects that handle sensitive data, and do not grant your developers access to it.
choices.C: Include all developers in an access level associated with the service perimeter, and allow them to use Cloud Shell.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls for the entire platform.

questions.explanation: B is correct because a VPC Service Controls perimeter should wrap only the sensitive projects, while developers can still use Cloud Shell outside the restricted perimeter. The other choices are wrong because they either over-restrict the whole platform or give developers direct access to the protected perimeter, defeating the security goal.

questions.question: Your new game running on Google Cloud is in public beta, and you want to design meaningful service level objectives (SLOs) before the game becomes generally available. What should you do?

choices.A: Define one SLO as 99% HTTP requests return the 2xx status code. Define the other SLO as 99% requests return within 100 ms.
choices.B: Define one SLO as service availability that is the same as Google Cloud's availability. Define the other SLO as 100-ms latency.
choices.C: Define one SLO as 99.9% game server availability. Define the other SLO as less than 100-ms latency.
choices.D: Define one SLO as total uptime of the game server within a week. Define the other SLO as the mean response time of all HTTP requests that are less than 100 ms.

questions.explanation: A is right because good SLOs should be measurable user outcomes, and HTTP success rate plus request latency are clear, concrete metrics for a game service. B incorrectly ties the SLO to Google Cloud’s own availability, C is too vague and unrealistic without a defined service baseline, and D mixes imprecise wording with the wrong metric definition.

questions.question: HRL wants you to help them bring existing recorded video content to new fans in emerging regions. Considering the HRL business and technical requirements, what should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud CDN to cache the video content from HRL's existing public cloud provider.
choices.B: Replicate the video content in Google Kubernetes Engine clusters in regions close to the fans.
choices.C: Serve the video content directly from a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket.
choices.D: Use Apigee Edge to cache the video content from HRL's existing public cloud provider.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Cloud CDN can cache video content close to users around the world, improving delivery performance for existing public-origin content. B adds unnecessary GKE complexity, C serves content directly from Cloud Storage rather than accelerating an existing provider, and D is an API management product, not a video CDN.

questions.question: You are the data compliance officer for TerramEarth and must protect customers' personally identifiable information (PII), like credit card information. TerramEarth wants to personalize product recommendations for its large industrial customers. You need to respect data privacy and deliver a solution. What should you do?

choices.A: Use AutoML to provide data to the recommendation service.
choices.B: Use the Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API to provide data to the recommendation service.
choices.C: Process PII data on-premises to keep the private information more secure.
choices.D: Manually build, train, and test machine learning models to provide product recommendations anonymously.

questions.explanation: B is right because the Cloud DLP API is the GCP product for detecting and de-identifying sensitive data such as PII before it is used for recommendations. AutoML and manual ML in A and D do not protect sensitive fields by themselves, and C is not the right scalable GCP-based solution for controlled de-identification.

questions.question: You are designing a future-proof hybrid environment that will require network connectivity between Google Cloud and your on-premises environment. You want to ensure that the Google Cloud environment you are designing is compatible with your on-premises networking environment. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a network plan for your VPC in Google Cloud that uses CIDR ranges that overlap with your on-premises environment. Use a Cloud Interconnect connection between your on-premises environment and Google Cloud.
choices.B: Create a network plan for your VPC in Google Cloud that uses non-overlapping CIDR ranges with your on-premises environment. Use a Cloud Interconnect connection between your on-premises environment and Google Cloud.
choices.C: Use the default VPC in your Google Cloud project. Use a Cloud VPN connection between your on-premises environment and Google Cloud.
choices.D: Create a custom VPC in Google Cloud in auto mode. Use a Cloud VPN connection between your on-premises environment and Google Cloud.

questions.explanation: B is correct because non-overlapping CIDR ranges are required for hybrid connectivity, and Cloud Interconnect provides the future-proof, high-performance connection between on-premises and Google Cloud. Overlapping ranges in A break routing, while C and D use VPN, which is less scalable and not the best long-term hybrid design.

questions.question: Your company wants to track whether someone is present in a meeting room reserved for a scheduled meeting. There are 1000 meeting rooms across 5 offices on 3 continents. Each room is equipped with a motion sensor that reports its status every second. You want to support the data ingestion needs of this sensor network. The receiving infrastructure needs to account for the possibility that the devices may have inconsistent connectivity. Which solution should you design?

choices.A: Have devices poll for connectivity to Pub/Sub and publish the latest messages on a regular interval to a shared topic for all devices.
choices.B: Have devices poll for connectivity to Cloud SQL and insert the latest messages on a regular interval to a device specific table.
choices.C: Have devices create a persistent connection to an App Engine application fronted by Cloud Endpoints, which ingest messages and write them to Datastore.
choices.D: Have each device create a persistent connection to a Compute Engine instance and write messages to a custom application.

questions.explanation: A is right because Pub/Sub is the best GCP product for many distributed devices to publish messages reliably at scale through a shared topic. Cloud SQL in B is not a messaging system, and both C and D rely on direct persistent connections to compute/web services, which do not scale well for thousands of devices.

questions.question: You set up an autoscaling managed instance group to serve web traffic for an upcoming launch. After configuring the instance group as a backend service to an HTTP(S) load balancer, you notice that virtual machine (VM) instances are being terminated and re-launched every minute. The instances do not have a public IP address. You have verified that the appropriate web response is coming from each instance using the curl command. You want to ensure that the backend is configured correctly. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a tag on each instance with the name of the load balancer. Configure a firewall rule with the name of the load balancer as the source and the instance tag as the destination.
choices.B: Ensure that a firewall rule exists to allow load balancer health checks to reach the instances in the instance group.
choices.C: Assign a public IP to each instance, and configure a firewall rule to allow the load balancer to reach the instance public IP.
choices.D: Ensure that a firewall rule exists to allow source traffic on HTTP/HTTPS to reach the load balancer.

questions.explanation: B is correct because HTTP(S) load balancers need firewall rules that allow Google health check IPs to reach the backend VMs, otherwise the instances appear unhealthy. The other options are wrong because tags and public IPs do not solve health check reachability, and opening HTTP/HTTPS to the load balancer does not address backend firewall access.

questions.question: Your organization has a 3-tier web application deployed in the same Google Cloud Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). Each tier (web, API, and database) scales independently of the others. Network traffic should flow through the web to the API tier, and then on to the database tier. Traffic should not flow between the web and the database tier. How should you configure the network with minimal steps?

choices.A: Add tags to each tier and set up firewall rules to allow the desired traffic flow.
choices.B: Set up software-based firewalls on individual VMs.
choices.C: Add each tier to a different subnetwork.
choices.D: Add tags to each tier and set up routes to allow the desired traffic flow.

questions.explanation: A is right because instance tags combined with VPC firewall rules are the standard GCP way to control traffic between independently scaling tiers in the same VPC. Separate subnetworks do not enforce tier-to-tier access by themselves, software firewalls are harder to manage at scale, and routes control path selection rather than access control.

questions.question: You are designing a large distributed application with 30 microservices. Each of your distributed microservices needs to connect to a database backend. You want to store the credentials securely. Where should you store the credentials?

choices.A: In a secret management system
choices.B: In a config file that has restricted access through ACLs
choices.C: In an environment variable
choices.D: In the source code

questions.explanation: A is correct because a secret management system such as Secret Manager is designed to store database credentials securely and centrally. Config files, environment variables, and source code all expose secrets too broadly and are harder to rotate safely.

questions.question: Your customer is moving their corporate applications to Google Cloud. The security team wants detailed visibility of all resources in the organization. You use Resource Manager to set yourself up as the Organization Administrator. Which Identity and Access Management (IAM) roles should you give to the security team while following Google recommended practices?

choices.A: Organization viewer, Project owner
choices.B: Organization viewer, Project viewer
choices.C: Project owner, Network administrator
choices.D: Organization administrator, Project browser

questions.explanation: B is right because an Organization Viewer role gives visibility across the organization, while Project Viewer lets you inspect project resources without granting administrative control. Project Owner in A is too powerful, C focuses on networking rather than visibility, and D includes Project Browser but lacks the broader project read access needed.

questions.question: The database administration team has asked you to help them improve the performance of their new database server running on Compute Engine. The database is used for importing and normalizing the company's performance statistics. It is built with MySQL running on Debian Linux. They have an n1-standard-8 virtual machine with 80 GB of SSD zonal persistent disk which they can't restart until the next maintenance event. What should they change to get better performance from this system as soon as possible and in a cost-effective manner?

choices.A: Migrate their performance metrics warehouse to BigQuery.
choices.B: Increase the virtual machine's memory to 64 GB.
choices.C: Dynamically resize the SSD persistent disk to 500 GB.
choices.D: Create a new virtual machine running PostgreSQL.

questions.explanation: C is correct because resizing the SSD persistent disk in Google Compute Engine can improve I/O performance when the database is bottlenecked on storage throughput or capacity. A, B, and D are wrong because BigQuery is unrelated to the VM’s disk performance, adding memory only helps if RAM is the constraint, and creating a new PostgreSQL VM does not address the existing server’s storage issue.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare is migrating customer-facing workloads from colocation facilities to Google Cloud, but several legacy insurance provider integrations must remain on-premises for the next few years. The team needs a secure, high-performance connection between on-premises systems and Google Cloud, and they want predictable network behavior as they onboard new providers. They also need to reduce latency for customers and maintain regulatory compliance while the migration happens in phases. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Partner Interconnect with Cloud Router to connect the data center to Google Cloud and keep legacy integrations reachable over private IPs.
choices.B: Place the legacy systems behind Cloud CDN and route all provider traffic through a global external Application Load Balancer.
choices.C: Move all integrations into Cloud Run and use API Gateway to replace the remaining on-premises insurance provider systems.
choices.D: Deploy Cloud VPN with Cloud Router and expose the on-premises integrations through an external HTTPS load balancer.

questions.explanation: Partner Interconnect with Cloud Router provides a secure, high-performance, private connection suitable for hybrid connectivity during a phased migration while keeping legacy on-premises integrations accessible.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare runs several customer-facing applications in containers and wants a consistent way to manage multiple environments while dynamically scaling new environments as demand changes. The platform must support rapid rollout of updates, reduce infrastructure administration costs, and provide a stable operating model for the team. The applications will be deployed across development, test, and production with standardized controls. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Standardize deployment on Google Kubernetes Engine with separate namespaces and node pools for each environment.
choices.B: Deploy the applications on Compute Engine managed instance groups and install Kubernetes manually on each environment.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run for all workloads and move stateful services into Cloud SQL to eliminate container orchestration.
choices.D: Use App Engine flexible environment with a separate service for every provider integration.

questions.explanation: Google Kubernetes Engine provides a consistent container platform with strong support for multiple environments, autoscaling, and operational standardization, which fits EHR Healthcare's container-based workloads.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare must provide at least 99.9% availability for customer-facing systems and wants a disaster recovery approach that can adapt as the business grows. The company runs web-based applications with containerized services and cannot afford long outages during a regional incident. The architecture should support future expansion into more regions without forcing a major redesign. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy all services in a single Google Cloud region and use Cloud Load Balancing across multiple zones.
choices.B: Run a multi-region architecture behind global external Application Load Balancing and store durable data in regional Cloud SQL instances with cross-region replicas where needed.
choices.C: Use active-passive deployments across two regions with Cloud DNS failover and manual instance promotion.
choices.D: Place the applications on Bare Metal Solution and rely on snapshot exports to Cloud Storage for recovery.

questions.explanation: A multi-region design behind global load balancing improves availability and supports regional failover while allowing the architecture to scale with future growth.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare currently uses several open source monitoring tools, and alerts are often ignored because they arrive by email without enough context. The company wants centralized visibility into system performance and usage across all customer-facing environments. It also needs consistent logging, log retention, monitoring, and alerting as it expands its Google Cloud footprint. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, and Cloud Monitoring alerting policies with log sinks to BigQuery for long-term analysis.
choices.B: Use Operations Suite agents on Compute Engine only and keep Kubernetes logs in local cluster storage.
choices.C: Use Cloud Trace alone for performance visibility and export all logs directly to Cloud Storage buckets.
choices.D: Use Security Command Center with Event Threat Detection as the primary source of application health alerts.

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring provide centralized logs, metrics, dashboards, and alerting, while BigQuery sinks support retention and deeper analysis across environments.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants to ingest data from new insurance providers quickly and generate insights into healthcare trends from provider data. The solution must support future analytics workloads, scale as provider volume grows, and help the company build predictions and reports. The data will arrive continuously from multiple sources and should be prepared for downstream analysis. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Filestore to stage incoming data and Data Fusion only for scheduled batch exports to Cloud Storage.
choices.B: Use Cloud CDN to cache provider payloads and Cloud SQL to store the raw event stream for reporting.
choices.C: Use Cloud Pub/Sub to ingest provider events, Dataflow to transform the data, and BigQuery to store it for analytics and machine learning.
choices.D: Use Memorystore for Redis as the primary analytics warehouse and Cloud Functions to run periodic reports.

questions.explanation: Cloud Pub/Sub, Dataflow, and BigQuery form a scalable ingestion and analytics pipeline that supports continuous data processing and downstream reporting or prediction use cases.

questions.question: A retail company is rearchitecting a monolithic order system on Google Cloud. The checkout flow must stay highly available, while the catalog and recommendation functions can scale independently and fail without blocking purchases. The team also wants to reduce resource waste, keep service interfaces clear, and allow separate updates to each component without redeploying the whole application. What should you do?

choices.A: Split the system into loosely coupled services on Cloud Run that communicate through Pub/Sub and standard APIs, while keeping the checkout service stateless and independently scalable.
choices.B: Deploy the entire application on a single managed instance group in Compute Engine and use Cloud Load Balancing to distribute traffic across identical VMs.
choices.C: Break the application into separate services on GKE and connect them directly with shared in-memory state to minimize network calls.
choices.D: Move the application to App Engine standard environment and use Cloud SQL as the shared backend for all functions to simplify deployment.

questions.explanation: A modular architecture with loosely coupled, stateless services allows independent scaling and updates, and Pub/Sub plus APIs provide clear interfaces between components. Cloud Run supports per-service scaling and helps isolate less critical functions from checkout.

questions.question: A media analytics startup receives large daily uploads that must be processed into thumbnails, metadata, and usage summaries. The ingest pipeline must handle spikes in parallel work, and each stage should scale separately based on demand. The company wants to avoid long-running synchronous requests and keep processing resilient if one stage slows down. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run jobs for all processing steps and store intermediate results in Memorystore for Redis to reduce latency.
choices.B: Use Pub/Sub to decouple ingestion from processing, then run separate autoscaled Cloud Run services for thumbnail generation, metadata extraction, and reporting.
choices.C: Use Dataflow for the entire workflow and store all intermediate artifacts directly in Cloud SQL.
choices.D: Use a single GKE deployment with a large horizontal pod autoscaler and process each file synchronously inside one container.

questions.explanation: Pub/Sub enables asynchronous fan-out and decoupling, while separate autoscaled Cloud Run services allow each processing stage to scale independently and handle bursts in parallel.

questions.question: A logistics company is designing a shipment tracking platform with pricing, route optimization, and notification components. Pricing changes frequently and must be updated without affecting the other services, and notifications must continue operating even if route optimization is unavailable. The platform needs minimal dependencies between components and clear service boundaries. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the components as separate Cloud Run services and use API Gateway for synchronous calls, while using Pub/Sub for notifications that can tolerate delays.
choices.B: Implement the application on Anthos with a shared Redis cache and direct container-to-container communication for all requests.
choices.C: Place all components behind an External Application Load Balancer and share a single Cloud SQL database schema across them.
choices.D: Build the system as a single Cloud Functions application and use Firestore documents as the shared interface for all business logic.

questions.explanation: Separate Cloud Run services with API Gateway and Pub/Sub support clear boundaries and loose coupling. Pub/Sub also allows less critical notification flows to continue independently when other components are degraded.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is building a patient intake web application on Google Cloud. The application must support rapid scaling during flu season, allow instances to be replaced without losing active session data, and avoid dependence on any single server. The team also wants to simplify recovery and reduce the risk of state corruption during deployment updates. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Deploy the front end on Cloud Run and store session and patient intake state in Firestore so each request can be handled independently.
choices.B: Run the application on Compute Engine VMs with local SSDs and store session state on each instance for low-latency access.
choices.C: Use GKE with a StatefulSet and attach Persistent Disks to preserve session data across pod restarts.
choices.D: Use App Engine flexible environment and keep user session data in the application process memory for faster response times.

questions.explanation: A stateless Cloud Run front end with Firestore for persisted session data supports horizontal scaling, simpler recovery, and safe replacement of instances without losing state.

questions.question: A subscription billing platform runs a heavy batch invoicing engine and a lightweight customer portal. The business wants to avoid overprovisioning resources for the portal, keep the invoicing workload isolated, and allow each component to scale based on its own usage patterns. The architecture should also make it easy to reduce spending during quiet periods. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy both workloads in the same GKE node pool and use a single autoscaler for the entire cluster to simplify operations.
choices.B: Run the portal on Cloud Run and the invoicing engine on Cloud Run jobs or Dataflow, so each component receives only the resources it needs.
choices.C: Move the portal and invoicing engine to one Cloud Run service and increase the memory allocation to cover peak invoicing demand.
choices.D: Place both workloads in a single Dataflow pipeline so all resources are shared dynamically across portal and billing tasks.

questions.explanation: Separating the lightweight portal from the batch invoicing workload lets each component use the appropriate Google Cloud service and scale or run only when needed, reducing waste and improving cost efficiency.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to automate product catalog enrichment by generating attributes, descriptions, and images from supplier-provided data. The solution must reduce manual catalog work, keep outputs consistent across channels, and support future integration with the company’s existing web and mobile commerce experiences. The team also wants to minimize operational overhead while using Google Cloud managed services for the AI workflow. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Dataflow to transform supplier feeds into product descriptions and images, then load the results into Memorystore for Redis for application access.
choices.B: Use BigQuery ML to generate product attributes directly from the catalog tables and store enriched results in Cloud SQL for the website to query.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Agent Builder to crawl supplier files in Cloud Storage and publish catalog updates directly to Firebase Hosting.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI with Cloud Run and Pub/Sub to orchestrate catalog enrichment jobs, store source files in Cloud Storage, and write approved results to BigQuery.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI provides the generative AI capabilities, Cloud Run and Pub/Sub support a managed, event-driven workflow, Cloud Storage holds supplier inputs, and BigQuery can store curated enriched catalog data for downstream analytics and applications.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to add a conversational shopping experience to its website and mobile app so customers can ask for products in natural language and get relevant results. The solution must improve product discoverability, support personalized responses, and use Google Cloud search and AI capabilities rather than the current database-only browsing model. The company also wants a design that can scale with traffic spikes during promotions. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Data Catalog and Pub/Sub to tag catalog records and trigger batch queries against MongoDB for each customer message.
choices.B: Use Cloud SQL and Memorystore for Redis to build keyword search indexes and return results from the application tier.
choices.C: Use Apigee and Cloud Functions to expose the product catalog API and let the mobile app call the relational databases directly.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI Search and Conversation with Cloud Run to serve the chat experience and connect it to product data stored in BigQuery.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Search and Conversation is the best fit for natural-language product discovery, and Cloud Run provides a scalable serverless frontend for the chat interface. BigQuery can be used as a governed source of product data for search grounding and analytics.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail has legacy SFTP file transfers and ETL batch processing between on-premises systems and cloud applications. The company wants to modernize these integrations, reduce data-transfer errors, and keep the existing systems connected during migration. The solution must support scheduled file movement, transformation, and secure connectivity to Google Cloud. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run Jobs and Firestore to store incoming batch files and trigger custom cron scripts from the application layer.
choices.B: Use BigQuery federated queries over the on-premises file server and replace ETL jobs with Looker dashboards.
choices.C: Use Cloud Load Balancing and GKE Ingress to receive SFTP files and transform them in Kubernetes pods.
choices.D: Use Storage Transfer Service with Cloud VPN and Dataflow to move, transform, and load files into Cloud Storage and BigQuery.

questions.explanation: Storage Transfer Service is suitable for managed file movement, Cloud VPN provides hybrid connectivity, and Dataflow can perform scalable transformations before loading data into Cloud Storage and BigQuery.

questions.topic: Modernizing the web application database layer
questions.question: Cymbal Retail’s custom web application currently queries relational databases directly for product names and categories, which limits scaling and makes modernization difficult. The company wants to reduce database maintenance, improve availability, and support a more cloud-native application design without changing the customer-facing functionality. The architecture should also allow the team to scale independently as traffic grows. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Bigtable for relational catalog queries and connect the web app through Cloud CDN for lower latency.
choices.B: Migrate the application to Cloud Run and use Cloud SQL for the transactional product catalog database with automated backups and high availability.
choices.C: Move the application to GKE and keep the databases on-premises behind a VPN for all catalog queries.
choices.D: Use Memorystore for Redis as the primary source of truth and sync product records with Pub/Sub.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run provides a managed application platform with independent scaling, and Cloud SQL offers a managed relational database with backups and high availability for workloads that still require SQL access.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail currently uses a mix of Grafana, Nagios, and Elastic for monitoring, but the tools are fragmented and make it hard to detect issues across the customer journey. The company wants a unified monitoring strategy for cloud-hosted workloads, faster incident detection, and better insight into application and infrastructure health as it modernizes. The solution should be aligned with Google Cloud managed operations tools and support both application metrics and logs. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Composer to aggregate logs from the services and store them in Firestore for manual dashboarding.
choices.B: Use Cloud DNS and Cloud NAT to capture service health events and send them to Looker dashboards.
choices.C: Use Security Command Center and Cloud Armor to replace the existing monitoring stack with threat findings.
choices.D: Use Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, and Error Reporting to centralize telemetry and create alerting policies for the retail services.

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, and Error Reporting provide a unified, managed observability stack for logs, metrics, and application errors across modernized workloads.

questions.question: A retail company wants to modernize its shopping application while keeping its customer database on-premises for regulatory reasons. The frontend must run in Google Cloud for elasticity, the backend services must access the on-premises system with low latency, and the solution must support gradual migration of application components. The team also wants a reusable pattern that clearly separates tiers and can be expanded later. What should you do?

choices.A: Use a tiered hybrid architecture with the frontend and stateless services on Google Kubernetes Engine or Compute Engine in Google Cloud and the stateful database remaining on-premises, connected by Cloud Interconnect.
choices.B: Move the customer database to Cloud SQL and host the frontend on Cloud Run, using Cloud Storage as the integration layer between tiers.
choices.C: Duplicate the full application stack in two Google Cloud regions with global load balancing to replace the on-premises environment.
choices.D: Deploy the entire application on Google Kubernetes Engine in one region and use Cloud VPN only for administrative access to the on-premises database.

questions.explanation: A tiered hybrid pattern places different application tiers in the environments that best fit them. This is appropriate when the frontend or stateless services move to Google Cloud while the database remains on-premises and must be reached with low-latency private connectivity such as Cloud Interconnect.

questions.question: A media company operates workloads in Google Cloud and another public cloud. Its recommendation engine uses data science services in Google Cloud, while its video processing pipeline must remain in the other cloud because of existing licensing contracts. The company needs a design that keeps each workload where it runs best, but still allows both environments to exchange data through defined interfaces. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Replicate the video processing pipeline into Compute Engine in Google Cloud and use Cloud Storage Transfer Service to phase out the other cloud.
choices.B: Use a partitioned multicloud architecture with each workload deployed in the most suitable cloud and integrate the services through APIs and messaging.
choices.C: Consolidate all workloads onto Google Kubernetes Engine in Google Cloud and connect to the other cloud only through Cloud VPN for backup traffic.
choices.D: Deploy identical stacks in both clouds using Traffic Director and Cloud Load Balancing to actively split every request between them.

questions.explanation: A partitioned multicloud pattern is designed for distributing different application components across multiple clouds based on fit, with communication through well-defined interfaces. That matches a scenario where one workload remains in another cloud and another workload uses Google Cloud services.

questions.question: A financial services company stores transaction data in an on-premises warehouse and in a separate cloud provider, but wants to centralize analytics in Google Cloud without moving all operational systems. Analysts need to query data from multiple environments, and the company wants a pattern that supports distributed data sources and a common analytics layer. The architecture must minimize disruption to source systems and allow expansion to new sources later. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a redundant environment in Google Cloud by copying all source databases into Cloud SQL and running analytics only against the replicas.
choices.B: Host the analytics tools on Compute Engine in each environment and connect them with Cloud Load Balancing to distribute queries evenly.
choices.C: Move the operational transaction systems into BigQuery and use Dataproc to simulate the on-premises warehouse for all analytics workloads.
choices.D: Use an analytics hybrid and multicloud architecture with BigQuery as the central analytics layer and data ingested or federated from the on-premises and other-cloud sources.

questions.explanation: An analytics hybrid and multicloud pattern is intended for centralized analytics across distributed data sources. BigQuery is the natural Google Cloud analytics layer for querying and analyzing data from multiple environments without requiring all operational systems to be moved first.

questions.question: A manufacturer has factories with intermittent connectivity to Google Cloud, but local systems must continue to process sensor data and control equipment even when the network is unavailable. The company also wants to send aggregated data to Google Cloud for central monitoring and longer-term analysis when connectivity returns. The design should support local responsiveness, limited bandwidth usage, and eventual integration with cloud services. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run for every factory application and require all sensor data to be sent directly to BigQuery in real time.
choices.B: Replicate all factory workloads across multiple Google Cloud regions with global HTTP(S) Load Balancing to eliminate the need for local control.
choices.C: Deploy the local processing layer on Google Distributed Cloud at the edge and synchronize aggregated data to Google Cloud services such as Pub/Sub and BigQuery.
choices.D: Move the factory control systems to a single Google Cloud region and use Cloud VPN so the sites can tolerate brief outages.

questions.explanation: An edge hybrid pattern is suited to sites that must keep working locally with limited or unreliable connectivity while still integrating with Google Cloud. Google Distributed Cloud at the edge can host local processing, and summarized data can later flow to services such as Pub/Sub and BigQuery.

questions.question: A software company runs a baseline workload on-premises but experiences predictable traffic spikes at the end of each month. It wants to keep normal operating costs low, preserve its existing environment, and temporarily extend capacity into Google Cloud only during peak demand. The application is stateless and can scale horizontally when extra workers are added. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy a redundant multi-region environment in Google Cloud and route all traffic there with Cloud Load Balancing during the peak periods.
choices.B: Use Cloud Storage and BigQuery as the temporary burst tier while leaving compute tasks on-premises.
choices.C: Build a cloud bursting architecture that keeps the core workload on-premises and uses Google Cloud Compute Engine or Google Kubernetes Engine to add capacity during spikes.
choices.D: Migrate the entire application to Cloud Run and use Firestore so that all peak capacity is always handled natively in Google Cloud.

questions.explanation: Cloud bursting is designed for extending an existing environment into the cloud only when demand exceeds local capacity. Keeping the baseline on-premises and adding Compute Engine or Google Kubernetes Engine capacity in Google Cloud is the best fit for a stateless burstable workload.

questions.question: A retail company is moving several applications to Google Cloud and wants a VPC design that will support future growth without forcing a major redesign later. The network team must integrate with an existing on-premises IP address plan, avoid overlapping address ranges, and use clear subnet names for operations. They also want a design that is easier to understand and maintain across multiple environments. What should you do?

choices.A: Use multiple auto mode VPC networks and connect them with VPC Network Peering to keep application environments isolated.
choices.B: Design a custom mode VPC network with manually defined subnets that align to the enterprise IP plan and naming standards.
choices.C: Create an auto mode VPC network so Google Cloud automatically provisions subnets in every region and simplifies initial workload placement.
choices.D: Deploy a Shared VPC architecture immediately so all future projects inherit the same network and subnet layout.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks are the recommended enterprise choice because they let you control address allocation, avoid overlap with on-premises ranges, and apply consistent naming conventions.

questions.question: A global manufacturer is standardizing its Google Cloud landing zone and needs a consistent way to name VPC resources across business units, regions, and environments. The architecture team wants names that are intuitive for operators, easy to search, and consistent across projects. They also want the naming pattern to work for VPC networks, subnets, firewall rules, and routes. What should you do?

choices.A: Adopt separate naming conventions for each project so teams can optimize names for their own applications and regions.
choices.B: Define a single standardized naming convention for VPC networks, subnets, firewall rules, and routes across Google Cloud.
choices.C: Use short random identifiers for each resource and store the mapping in Cloud Asset Inventory to avoid naming collisions.
choices.D: Use labels only for all networking resources and leave resource names to be generated automatically by Google Cloud.

questions.explanation: A single, clear, and consistent naming convention across networking resources improves readability, operations, and maintainability, which is a best practice for VPC design.

questions.question: A financial services company is designing its Google Cloud network for multiple application teams. Security and networking stakeholders want to minimize future disruption, keep routing simple, and reduce the number of subnets that must be managed. The environment will include development, test, and production workloads in several regions. What should you do?

choices.A: Create one subnet per VM instance so each workload has a dedicated IP range and isolated routing domain.
choices.B: Use custom mode VPC networks with fewer, larger subnets grouped by environment and region where appropriate.
choices.C: Use one shared auto mode VPC network per environment and allow all teams to add regional subnets as needed.
choices.D: Create a separate auto mode VPC network for each application team and let each region create its own default subnet.

questions.explanation: Grouping applications into fewer, larger subnets in custom mode helps keep the design simpler and easier to manage while supporting enterprise IP planning.

questions.question: A healthcare company is establishing a new Google Cloud foundation and wants to avoid disruptive network changes after workloads are deployed. The architecture team must decide how to approach VPC design alongside organizational setup, security planning, and future workload growth. They need a recommendation that reduces rework and supports incremental adoption. What should you do?

choices.A: Delay VPC design until the first production application is ready so the network can be optimized around actual traffic patterns.
choices.B: Use separate unmanaged networks for each project and connect them only when application dependencies appear.
choices.C: Treat VPC design as an early architecture decision and plan it before workloads and organizational structure are finalized.
choices.D: Start with auto mode VPC networks and convert them to custom mode later once the landing zone is complete.

questions.explanation: VPC design should be considered early because late changes to segmentation, routing, and workload placement can be disruptive and expensive.

questions.question: A media company is planning Google Cloud connectivity for multiple business units and expects several future migrations from on-premises systems. The network architects want a simple, reliable, and well-understood topology that can support growth without unnecessary complexity. They also want to prepare the design for stakeholders from security, operations, and application teams. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Build a complex mesh of VPC Network Peering connections between application-specific VPC networks to maximize isolation.
choices.B: Use auto mode VPC networks in each region so Google Cloud expands the topology automatically as workloads are deployed.
choices.C: Design a simple custom mode VPC-based landing zone and align stakeholders early on requirements, timelines, and network ownership.
choices.D: Place all workloads in a single shared subnet and use firewall tags to separate business units after deployment.

questions.explanation: The best practice is to keep the VPC topology simple and to identify decision makers and pre-work early so the design can meet stakeholder requirements.

questions.question: A media analytics company is migrating a batch processing platform to Google Cloud. The workloads ingest variable daily data volumes, must finish processing within a fixed overnight window, and are expected to grow 3x over the next year. The team needs to define performance requirements for compute, storage throughput, and scaling before implementation, and they want a design that avoids both over-allocation and processing delays. They also need a way to validate the design against real workload behavior before going live. What should you do?

choices.A: Use GKE Autopilot for the batch application, place all data in Cloud SQL, and rely on horizontal pod autoscaling to absorb the growth.
choices.B: Define granular performance targets for each layer, select Compute Engine and Cloud Storage settings based on required CPU, memory, IOPS, and throughput, then load test the system and monitor results with Cloud Monitoring.
choices.C: Move the workload to App Engine Standard, rely on automatic scaling for all components, and use Memorystore for Redis to store batch inputs and outputs.
choices.D: Deploy the workload on a single Compute Engine machine type with oversized vCPU and memory reservations, and use Cloud Storage Standard for all intermediate data.

questions.explanation: The best approach is to define detailed performance requirements for each layer first, then choose resources such as machine type and storage performance characteristics that match the workload. Load testing and Cloud Monitoring help validate whether the allocation is sufficient and efficient.

questions.question: A financial services company is designing a customer portal on Google Cloud. The application must keep data in a specific country for regulatory reasons, serve users with low latency, and use managed services that are available in the chosen location. The architecture team also expects traffic to increase during market events and wants to avoid redesigning the platform later. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Choose a single Google Cloud region that satisfies data-locality requirements, verify that required services are available there, and design the application to scale within that region.
choices.B: Deploy the application in a multi-region architecture across several continents and use Cloud CDN to mask latency differences.
choices.C: Host the portal on Compute Engine in a region closest to the development team and use Cloud Load Balancing to route users globally.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run in any available region and replicate data asynchronously to Cloud Storage buckets in multiple countries for resilience.

questions.explanation: The correct design is to select a region that meets data-locality constraints, offers the needed Google Cloud services, and supports low-latency access for users. Planning for in-region scalability aligns with the stated growth requirement.

questions.question: A software company is modernizing two applications at the same time. One is a latency-sensitive interactive service with highly variable traffic, and the other is a nightly batch job that processes large datasets with predictable runtime. The architects want to choose hosting platforms that match each workload's performance characteristics without overprovisioning infrastructure. They also want to keep the design simple enough to support future growth. What should you do?

choices.A: Place both applications on GKE Standard so they can share clusters, and use Persistent Disk for all batch and interactive storage needs.
choices.B: Migrate both applications to App Engine Standard because automatic scaling will handle all workload differences equally well.
choices.C: Run both applications on the same Compute Engine managed instance group with identical machine types to simplify operations.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run for the interactive service and Compute Engine or GKE Standard for the batch workload, selecting each platform based on its performance profile.

questions.explanation: Different workload types have different performance characteristics, so the hosting platform should be chosen per application. Cloud Run is well suited for variable interactive traffic, while Compute Engine or GKE Standard can be better for batch processing and tighter control over resource allocation.

questions.question: An ecommerce company is redesigning its order-processing system on Google Cloud. The application writes transaction records frequently, reads customer history during checkout, and also stores large product images for later retrieval. The team must ensure that the storage design meets throughput and IOPS requirements for the transactional layer while remaining cost-effective for the image archive. They need a plan that considers performance early in the architecture phase. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Persistent Disk or Hyperdisk for the transactional database layer and Cloud Storage for the image archive, sizing each based on expected throughput and IOPS.
choices.B: Use Memorystore for Redis for both orders and images so the application can read and write all data from memory.
choices.C: Store all application data in Cloud Storage Archive to minimize cost and then use Cloud CDN to accelerate transactional reads.
choices.D: Use Filestore for product images and BigQuery for transaction records so both data types are handled by fully managed services.

questions.explanation: Transactional workloads require storage with appropriate IOPS and throughput, while image archives are better suited to object storage. Separating the storage strategy by workload type is the best way to meet performance and cost goals.

questions.question: A retail company is planning a migration of its seasonal online storefront to Google Cloud. Traffic can spike suddenly during promotions, and the team wants to avoid unexpected deployment failures caused by exhausted quotas. They need a design process that helps them align resource allocation with expected demand, track capacity over time, and identify scaling constraints before peak season. What should you do?

choices.A: Set up Cloud Monitoring dashboards for quota usage, review Active Assist recommendations, and adjust Compute Engine and other resource quotas based on forecasted demand.
choices.B: Move all storefront traffic to Cloud CDN and Firebase Hosting so quota limits on compute resources become irrelevant.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor to block excess traffic during peak events, and rely on Cloud Load Balancing to keep quota consumption low.
choices.D: Provision a fixed number of Compute Engine instances for the peak season and avoid monitoring quotas to prevent unnecessary changes.

questions.explanation: The right approach is to monitor quota usage, review recommendations from Active Assist, and plan capacity based on forecast demand so the environment can scale without hitting quota limits unexpectedly.

questions.question: A media company is moving a customer-facing application from on-premises to Google Cloud and expects frequent feature releases after the migration. The platform team needs an architecture that supports small, low-risk changes, rapid feedback, and clear separation between development and production environments. They also want to track delivery performance so they can prove whether changes are improving release speed and stability. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Google Kubernetes Engine with Artifact Registry, Cloud Deploy, and Cloud Build to implement progressive delivery across development, staging, and production environments.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run with Cloud Storage and Pub/Sub, then add manual approval checkpoints in each environment instead of using a delivery pipeline.
choices.C: Use Cloud Load Balancing with Compute Engine managed instance groups and Cloud SQL, then release changes by updating instances in place after testing in a staging subnet.
choices.D: Use App Engine standard environment with Firestore and Cloud Tasks, then deploy only quarterly to reduce the number of architectural changes.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy and Cloud Build support repeatable CI/CD and progressive delivery, while GKE and Artifact Registry provide a strong foundation for controlled changes across multiple environments. This aligns with the Well-Architected principle of designing for change and enables tracking delivery performance.

questions.question: A retailer is re-architecting several legacy applications on Google Cloud, and multiple teams are making design decisions in parallel. The chief architect needs a way to document the current architecture, capture decision history, and make the design understandable to new team members. The solution must also help teams align on standards and reduce duplicated work as the design evolves. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a Terraform-only implementation in Cloud Deployment Manager and use Cloud Logging to record architectural rationale automatically.
choices.B: Store architecture diagrams in Cloud Storage, use Cloud Source Repositories for notes, and rely on email threads to preserve decision history.
choices.C: Use Firebase Hosting for architecture documents, Cloud Functions for change notes, and BigQuery for storing design decisions.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Architecture Center templates, maintain diagrams and decision logs in Cloud Build artifacts, and publish the approved documents in a shared Cloud Storage location.

questions.explanation: Architecture Center templates help teams document cloud designs consistently, and maintaining decision logs in a shared, accessible location supports change history and alignment across teams. This directly reflects the Well-Architected guidance to document architecture and maintain change history.

questions.question: A financial services company is designing a new loan-processing platform on Google Cloud. The application must remain available during a regional outage, and the business has strict recovery time and recovery point objectives that influence where failover can occur. The architecture team also needs to minimize application changes because the current system will be migrated gradually. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run with Firestore in native mode and rely on automatic multi-zone scaling within one region for failover.
choices.B: Deploy the application across two regions with global external HTTP(S) Load Balancing, Cloud SQL cross-region replicas, and Cloud Storage dual-region buckets for shared data.
choices.C: Use GKE Autopilot in one region with regional persistent disks and configure instance templates to recreate nodes after an outage.
choices.D: Deploy the application on Compute Engine in a single region behind Cloud Load Balancing and replicate backups to Cloud Storage in a second region.

questions.explanation: A multi-region design with global load balancing and cross-region data replication best supports recovery objectives when a regional outage is a concern. It also limits application changes while improving resilience, which aligns with reliability-focused planning.

questions.question: A manufacturing company is migrating an ERP system from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud. The system will run in a hybrid model for at least 18 months, must connect securely to on-premises databases, and requires predictable network performance for batch processing. The network team wants an architecture that is scalable and can later support additional applications without redesign. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud VPN, a single VPC, and Cloud NAT to connect the ERP system to on-premises resources over the internet.
choices.B: Use External Application Load Balancing with Cloud Armor and connect to on-premises databases through Serverless VPC Access.
choices.C: Use Dedicated Interconnect, Cloud Router, and Shared VPC to establish private connectivity and centrally manage network expansion.
choices.D: Use VPC Peering between the on-premises network and Google Cloud, then expose the ERP application through Identity-Aware Proxy.

questions.explanation: Dedicated Interconnect provides private, predictable connectivity for hybrid workloads, Cloud Router automates route exchange, and Shared VPC gives a scalable model for multiple applications and teams. This is a strong planning choice for a long-term hybrid architecture.

questions.question: A startup is designing a new analytics platform on Google Cloud, but traffic is expected to vary widely by month and the team wants to avoid overprovisioning. The workload consists of batch jobs, ephemeral services, and a few always-on components that must remain available. Leadership wants the architecture to maximize business value while keeping costs predictable. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use GKE Standard with preemptible VMs for all services and keep the analytics data in Cloud SQL to simplify pricing.
choices.B: Use App Engine flexible environment for every component and enable autoscaling based only on CPU utilization.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run for ephemeral services, BigQuery for analytics, and committed use discounts only for the always-on Compute Engine components.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine sole-tenant nodes for all workloads, reserve capacity in every region, and store data in standard persistent disks.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run and BigQuery fit variable and usage-based demand, while committed use discounts can reduce cost for steady workloads. This combination supports cost optimization by matching platform choices to workload patterns.

questions.question: A retail company is starting its first production deployment on Google Cloud and wants a secure foundation before moving application teams. The platform team must quickly create projects, billing, IAM, networking, monitoring, and security controls with consistent defaults. They want a recommended starting point that can be expanded later and prefer a guided setup rather than assembling every component from scratch. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud VPN and Cloud Router first so the network foundation is established before any identity or governance configuration.
choices.B: Deploy the enterprise foundations blueprint with Terraform so the team can build the entire environment from code before any projects are created.
choices.C: Use Google Cloud Setup to create the organization structure, baseline networking, billing, monitoring, and security settings for a recommended landing zone.
choices.D: Create a Shared VPC host project and attach all workloads to it before defining folders, billing, and organization policies.

questions.explanation: Google Cloud Setup is the fastest recommended way to create a well-crafted landing zone with organization, billing, networking, monitoring, and security starting points.

questions.question: A financial services company is planning a full Google Cloud deployment and wants to review the architecture thoroughly before implementation. The design must follow security best practices, support repeatable deployment, and be easy to customize for internal standards. The cloud platform team prefers an infrastructure-as-code approach that can be reviewed and version controlled. What should you do?

choices.A: Build the environment with the Landing zone design in Google Cloud documents and then manually recreate the configuration in each region.
choices.B: Use Google Cloud Setup to create the environment interactively, then export the configuration to Terraform after the projects are live.
choices.C: Start with Cloud Deployment Manager templates and later migrate the resources to Terraform once the network is in production.
choices.D: Adopt the enterprise foundations blueprint and deploy it with the provided Terraform configuration, modifying it only where needed.

questions.explanation: The enterprise foundations blueprint is designed for end-to-end deployments based on security best practices and is available as Terraform, making it the best fit here.

questions.question: A media company is migrating several application tiers from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud over the next six months. The current environment has overlapping IP ranges with a partner network, and some workloads must keep private connectivity during the transition. The team needs guidance on architectural approaches that fit enterprise migration scenarios rather than a greenfield design. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the enterprise foundations blueprint to standardize the target environment before any migration planning is done.
choices.B: Use Cloud Load Balancing and Cloud DNS to resolve the overlapping address ranges during the migration window.
choices.C: Use the Designing networks for migrating enterprise workloads: Architectural approaches guidance to select a migration-friendly network architecture.
choices.D: Create a new network using Google Cloud Setup and move all workloads immediately into a single Shared VPC.

questions.explanation: The migration-focused networking guidance is specifically intended for enterprises moving workloads from existing environments and covers architectural approaches for that scenario.
products: Designing networks for migrating enterprise workloads: Architectural approaches

questions.question: An e-commerce company is designing a new global application on Google Cloud that must serve customers with low latency and high availability. The application will receive unpredictable traffic spikes during promotions, and the architecture must support global traffic distribution and name resolution at scale. The network team wants a design that emphasizes delivery to end users rather than only private connectivity. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Armor and VPC Service Controls to protect the application traffic and eliminate the need for global delivery services.
choices.B: Use Cloud VPN and Cloud Router to create resilient private connectivity and rely on regional routes for customer traffic.
choices.C: Use Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud CDN, and Cloud DNS to distribute traffic globally and serve content efficiently at scale.
choices.D: Use Cloud NAT and Private Service Connect so the application can scale globally without public-facing endpoints.

questions.explanation: For global customer-facing scale, Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud CDN, and Cloud DNS are the core services for traffic distribution, caching, and name resolution.

questions.question: A startup has only a few engineers and wants to begin using Google Cloud immediately, but the security team still requires a recommended structure for organization, billing, networking, monitoring, and security. The team does not want to spend weeks reading architecture documents before creating the first environment. They need a practical way to start that still follows Google's recommended patterns. What should you do?

choices.A: Build the environment from the enterprise foundations blueprint in Terraform so the startup can start with a fully customized code base.
choices.B: Create Cloud Router and Cloud Interconnect connections first, then add projects and security policies after application deployment.
choices.C: Use the Landing zone design in Google Cloud document set and decide all network details manually before creating any resources.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Setup to create a recommended baseline environment, then refine the landing zone as requirements evolve.

questions.explanation: Google Cloud Setup is intended for quickly creating a recommended baseline environment and is the best fit when speed and guidance are priorities.

questions.question: A retail company wants to move 120 virtual machines from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud within 9 months. The workloads have mixed requirements: some must be moved quickly with minimal code changes, some can be modernized later, and the team wants to reduce risk by validating the environment before cutover. They also need a structured approach that helps separate discovery, foundation building, workload transfer, and optimization. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run for all workloads and redesign the applications first so the migration can skip any intermediate landing zone work.
choices.B: Use Migrate to Virtual Machines for a rapid lift-and-shift of eligible servers, then use the Migration Center and Google Cloud Architecture Framework to plan modernization phases.
choices.C: Use Transfer Appliance to move the virtual machines directly into Cloud Storage and then import them into Compute Engine after the data transfer completes.
choices.D: Use Google Kubernetes Engine to replatform all applications immediately and migrate the virtual machines by rebuilding each service as containers.

questions.explanation: Migrate to Virtual Machines supports lift-and-shift of VM workloads, while Migration Center and the Architecture Framework help assess, plan, and sequence a broader migration program. This fits a phased migration with low-risk validation and later modernization.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is preparing to migrate several applications from a private hosting environment to Google Cloud. The security team requires separate environments for development, testing, and production, centralized identity management, and a network foundation that can scale as more applications are moved. The platform team also wants a consistent way to organize projects and apply guardrails before workloads are deployed. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use VPC Service Controls as the primary landing zone mechanism and place all workloads in one project behind a secure perimeter.
choices.B: Use Shared VPC with a dedicated host project and separate service projects, then establish IAM, folders, and organization policies as the migration foundation.
choices.C: Create a single project for all workloads, connect it to the on-premises network with Cloud VPN, and defer organization structure until after migration.
choices.D: Deploy the workloads first in individual projects, then use Cloud Run and Artifact Registry to standardize governance later.

questions.explanation: Shared VPC, folders, IAM, and organization policies are core landing zone components for scalable governance and network design. This creates a foundation that supports multiple environments and controlled growth.

questions.question: A manufacturer is migrating an ERP system from on-premises to Google Cloud while keeping several legacy databases on-site for the first year. The application needs low-latency connectivity, predictable bandwidth, and a design that can grow if more sites are added later. The network team wants a primary private connection and a backup path for resilience. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud VPN only, and place all migrated systems in a single VPC without any routing redundancy.
choices.B: Use Cloud Interconnect with Cloud Router for dynamic routing, and add HA VPN as an encrypted backup path.
choices.C: Use VPC Peering between the on-premises network and Google Cloud, and rely on static routes for failover.
choices.D: Use Cloud NAT for the on-premises servers and expose the ERP system through External HTTP(S) Load Balancing.

questions.explanation: Cloud Interconnect with Cloud Router provides high-bandwidth private connectivity and dynamic routing, while HA VPN is a common resilient backup. This meets low-latency and scalability requirements for hybrid migration.

questions.question: A media company must migrate 1.5 PB of archival data from an on-premises storage system to Google Cloud within two weeks. The WAN link is too slow to finish the transfer in time, and the company wants to avoid building temporary infrastructure just for the migration. The data will be used later in Cloud Storage and BigQuery for analytics. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Transfer Appliance to ship the data to Google Cloud, then load it into Cloud Storage for downstream analytics.
choices.B: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to load the archival files directly into BigQuery, then export them to Cloud Storage afterward.
choices.C: Use Filestore as an intermediate landing zone and copy the data to Cloud Storage after the migration window closes.
choices.D: Use Storage Transfer Service over the existing WAN link and compress files into a single archive before copying them.

questions.explanation: Transfer Appliance is designed for very large offline data migrations when network transfer is too slow or impractical. The data can then be ingested into Cloud Storage for analytics workloads.

questions.question: A software company is migrating a monolithic Java application from a private hosting environment to Google Cloud. The CTO wants a plan that gets the application running quickly, but the engineering team also wants to reduce future deployment complexity and improve scalability after the first release. The company needs a target path that supports both an initial move and later modernization without starting over. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move the monolith to Compute Engine first, then later use App Engine standard only for the database tier.
choices.B: Rewrite the monolith as a serverless application using Firebase Hosting and Firestore before any migration planning begins.
choices.C: Rebuild the application immediately for Cloud Run and delay the migration until every component can be redesigned for containers.
choices.D: Migrate the application to Compute Engine initially, then modernize selected components to Google Kubernetes Engine or Cloud Run after stabilization.

questions.explanation: A staged approach that first lifts and shifts to Compute Engine and later modernizes to GKE or Cloud Run is a common migration strategy. It balances speed, risk reduction, and future optimization.

questions.question: A retail company is moving a customer-facing ordering system to Google Cloud. The current application is a monolith, but the team wants to reduce release risk by separating payment processing from catalog browsing, and they need to scale the payment path independently during flash sales. They also require clear service boundaries so that future teams can update inventory and promotions without affecting checkout. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Deploy the entire application on a single managed instance group in Compute Engine behind an External Application Load Balancer and scale the group vertically during peak traffic.
choices.B: Migrate the application to App Engine standard and use Memorystore for Redis to centralize session and application data across all modules.
choices.C: Refactor the application into separate services on GKE and use Cloud SQL for all shared state, while keeping synchronous calls between every component.
choices.D: Decompose the system into independent microservices on GKE, expose them through Cloud Load Balancing, and use Pub/Sub for asynchronous communication between noncritical components.

questions.explanation: A modular architecture with independent services and asynchronous messaging supports loose coupling, targeted scaling, and clearer interfaces. GKE and Pub/Sub are appropriate Google Cloud services for implementing this design.

questions.question: A media startup runs a video upload workflow on Google Cloud. Upload processing, thumbnail generation, and email notifications currently share the same application tier, and failures in notifications sometimes delay the main upload response. The business wants the upload service to stay fast even if noncritical downstream tasks fail, and it wants each function to evolve independently. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move the workflow into one GKE deployment with multiple containers and share a common in-memory cache using Memorystore for Redis.
choices.B: Place all workflow steps behind one Cloud Run service and share the same Cloud SQL instance so each request completes in a single transaction.
choices.C: Use Cloud Functions for each workflow step and connect them with Pub/Sub, so upload processing can continue even if thumbnail generation or email delivery is delayed.
choices.D: Implement the workflow as one Dataflow pipeline that performs upload handling, image creation, and notifications in a single streaming job.

questions.explanation: Pub/Sub decouples the workflow steps and Cloud Functions lets each step be deployed and scaled independently. This fits a loosely coupled design and protects the primary upload path from less-important failures.

questions.question: An analytics team needs to process millions of image records each night on Google Cloud. The workload must finish within a four-hour window, and the team wants to improve throughput without overprovisioning always-on servers. The data can be split into independent chunks, and each chunk can be processed without waiting on the others. Which solution should you recommend?
