choices.A: Use Cloud Run with a single service instance and increase request timeout so each image is processed sequentially in one process.
choices.B: Use BigQuery scheduled queries to process the images directly in SQL and store the results in Cloud SQL.
choices.C: Use a GKE Autopilot cluster with parallel worker pods reading from Cloud Storage and writing results to BigQuery.
choices.D: Run the batch jobs on a single Compute Engine VM with a larger machine type and use Cloud Storage for input and output data.

questions.explanation: The workload is naturally parallel, so using multiple worker pods in GKE Autopilot lets the team scale out processing and finish faster without maintaining fixed capacity. BigQuery is a suitable destination for analytical results.

questions.question: A financial services company is redesigning an API tier for loan applications on Google Cloud. The current servers store user session data locally, which makes rolling updates risky and causes problems when instances restart. The architects want the service to be easy to scale horizontally and recover quickly from failures while keeping request-specific state outside the compute layer. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use GKE with stateful sets and attach a separate Persistent Disk to each pod to preserve user sessions across restarts.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run for the API tier and store session and application state in Firestore so each request is handled independently.
choices.C: Use App Engine flexible environment and store session state in local SSD on each serving instance for faster access.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine virtual machines with attached Persistent Disks and keep session state on the same instance that handled the request.

questions.explanation: A stateless API tier can scale and recover more easily when request-specific state is stored externally. Cloud Run and Firestore provide a managed, scalable approach that avoids coupling state to individual instances.

questions.question: A logistics company is splitting a shipment tracking platform into more services on Google Cloud. The architects want to improve maintainability and independent deployment, but they are concerned that too many service-to-service calls could add latency to the customer search experience. The team needs a design that preserves modularity while reducing unnecessary synchronous communication. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Place the services on GKE and use gRPC for every interaction, including notifications and reporting, to maximize uniformity.
choices.B: Collapse the application into one Compute Engine managed instance group and use Cloud Load Balancing to scale the whole stack together.
choices.C: Keep the search path synchronous on Cloud Run, but move background updates to Pub/Sub and Cloud Tasks so noncritical work is handled asynchronously.
choices.D: Use Cloud SQL as a shared database for every service and allow each service to query the same schema directly.

questions.explanation: The scenario calls for balancing modularity with performance. Keeping the user-facing search path synchronous while offloading noncritical work to Pub/Sub and Cloud Tasks reduces latency from excessive service chatter and preserves independent components.

questions.question: A retail company is moving multiple application teams into Google Cloud and wants a network foundation that can support future growth without frequent redesign. The company has existing on-premises RFC 1918 ranges, a security team that wants clear resource ownership, and an operations team that needs predictable routing and naming. The cloud architects must minimize the risk of IP overlap, keep the topology easy to understand, and make it easier to connect additional environments later. What should you do?

choices.A: Design custom mode VPC networks with clear naming conventions and planned subnet ranges based on the company IP address management scheme.
choices.B: Use Shared VPC with multiple auto mode host projects so each team can manage its own network ranges while still sharing common services.
choices.C: Deploy a single default VPC network and add Cloud Router with Network Connectivity Center to expand routing as new teams onboard.
choices.D: Create auto mode VPC networks for each application so Google Cloud automatically provisions subnets in every region and reduces early design effort.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks are recommended for most enterprise production environments because they better fit existing IP management, avoid overlapping ranges, and allow clear, descriptive subnet naming.

questions.question: A software company is planning a new Google Cloud landing zone for several business units that will deploy workloads in multiple regions over the next two years. The networking team wants to reduce operational overhead, avoid an explosion of subnet objects, and keep address management predictable for security review. The architects are also required to leave room for future environments and avoid reworking the VPC design later. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use a few larger custom mode subnets per region and group related applications into fewer address ranges.
choices.B: Use separate default VPC networks for each project and rely on firewall rules to separate business units.
choices.C: Create many small subnets per application and per environment in each region so that each workload has its own isolated address space.
choices.D: Use auto mode VPC networks so Google Cloud can automatically add region subnets whenever workloads are created in new locations.

questions.explanation: The best practice is to group applications into fewer subnets with larger address ranges to simplify planning, reduce complexity, and support future growth.

questions.question: An enterprise is designing its first Google Cloud network for a set of shared services and multiple application projects. The architecture team wants a simple topology that is easy to operate, supports future expansion, and provides a clear place to centralize shared networking controls. The company also wants to avoid designing something overly complex before the workload needs are fully known. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Shared VPC with a single host project and service projects for application workloads and shared services.
choices.B: Deploy multiple default VPC networks and use Cloud NAT in each project to keep the environment simple.
choices.C: Create separate auto mode VPC networks for each region so routing stays localized and no centralized network structure is needed.
choices.D: Build a mesh of VPC Network Peering connections between every application project so each team can own its own isolated network.

questions.explanation: Shared VPC is a common enterprise architecture for centralizing network control in a host project while allowing workloads to run in service projects, keeping the design simpler and more governable.

questions.question: A global manufacturing company is standardizing Google Cloud network resources across several regions and environments. The platform team needs names that clearly show the company, business unit, environment, and location so administrators can quickly identify resources in a console or audit report. They also want a convention that can be extended consistently to VPC networks, subnets, firewall rules, and routes. What should you do?

choices.A: Use unique random identifiers for each resource name so that collisions are impossible across projects and regions.
choices.B: Adopt a consistent naming convention that includes company, business unit or application, region, environment, and resource type.
choices.C: Encode only the project ID in each resource name and rely on labels for the rest of the operational context.
choices.D: Name all VPC resources after the owning team and use identical subnet names in every region for consistency.

questions.explanation: A simple, intuitive, and consistent naming convention helps administrators understand the purpose, location, and differentiation of resources across the network.

questions.question: A healthcare startup is planning its Google Cloud network and expects to connect to an existing on-premises environment later in the year. The company has carefully managed private IP ranges already in use on-premises and wants to avoid conflicts when the cloud network is connected. The architects need a design that supports future hybrid connectivity and makes the network easier to maintain as more workloads are added. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use custom mode VPC networks with address ranges aligned to the existing enterprise IP address management plan.
choices.B: Use a single default VPC network and add Cloud VPN later when hybrid connectivity is ready.
choices.C: Create multiple peered auto mode VPC networks so each application can independently choose its own subnet range.
choices.D: Start with auto mode VPC networks so Google Cloud can manage IP allocation automatically and reduce initial planning time.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks are better suited for enterprises with existing IPAM because they help avoid overlap with on-premises address space and support hybrid expansion.

questions.question: A retail company is starting its first production deployment on Google Cloud and wants a network foundation that follows recommended practices. The team has 30 applications, needs a fast path to establish organization structure, billing, networking, monitoring, and security, and wants to avoid designing every component from scratch. They also want guidance that can be expanded later as workloads grow. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy only a VPC network and Cloud Router first, then add organization, billing, and security controls later after application migration.
choices.B: Create a shared VPC manually and connect applications directly to it without using any landing zone guidance or setup tooling.
choices.C: Use the enterprise foundations blueprint and modify the Terraform configuration to build the entire landing zone before creating any projects.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Setup in the Google Cloud console to create an initial landing zone with organization, billing, networking, monitoring, and security configured.

questions.explanation: Google Cloud Setup is the fastest recommended way to establish a well-crafted initial landing zone and includes guidance for organization, billing, networking, monitoring, and security.

questions.question: A financial services firm is planning a new Google Cloud environment and must meet strict security requirements from day one. The architecture team wants a full end-to-end deployment pattern that is based on security best practices, can be reviewed before implementation, and can be customized with Infrastructure as Code. They prefer a repeatable approach over a guided console setup. What should you do?

choices.A: Use the enterprise foundations blueprint and deploy it as a Terraform configuration that can be used as is or modified.
choices.B: Use Google Cloud Setup to provision the organization and then manually rebuild the network later with Terraform modules.
choices.C: Use Cloud NAT and Cloud DNS as the primary foundation, then add project and folder structure after the first application release.
choices.D: Adopt a single shared VPC design and postpone security baseline decisions until workloads are already deployed.

questions.explanation: The enterprise foundations blueprint is intended for an end-to-end deployment based on security best practices and is delivered as a Terraform configuration.

questions.question: A manufacturing company is migrating several enterprise applications from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud over six months. Some workloads need hybrid connectivity during the transition, and the network team wants architectural guidance specifically for migration scenarios. They need to compare design options before choosing a final topology. What should you do?

choices.A: Review Designing networks for migrating enterprise workloads: Architectural approaches to evaluate migration network architectures.
choices.B: Use the enterprise foundations blueprint and treat it as the only network design reference for the migration.
choices.C: Use Google Cloud Setup to create a new landing zone and rely on its default connectivity settings for the migration.
choices.D: Use Cloud VPN for all applications and defer network architecture planning until after the first workloads are moved.

questions.explanation: That document is the specific Google Cloud reference for designing networks when migrating enterprise workloads.

questions.question: An e-commerce company already has its core network in Google Cloud and now wants to improve global application delivery for seasonal traffic spikes. The team needs a design focused on scale and wants to use managed services for traffic distribution, DNS, and content delivery. They are not looking to redesign the organization or landing zone. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Cloud Setup to rebuild the network so load balancing and DNS can be configured from scratch.
choices.B: Use Cloud Interconnect only, because it provides application scaling, content delivery, and DNS routing features.
choices.C: Use the networking resources under Scale to design with Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud CDN, and Cloud DNS.
choices.D: Use the enterprise foundations blueprint to re-architect the environment before adding any traffic management services.

questions.explanation: The Scale category in the networking resources covers load balancing, content delivery, and DNS for delivering applications at any scale.

questions.question: A healthcare provider has already moved several applications to Google Cloud and now wants to strengthen the network design without changing the core landing zone. The architecture team needs guidance for protecting applications and network traffic while keeping the current platform operational. They want a reference that focuses on security-specific network enhancements. What should you do?

choices.A: Use the enterprise foundations blueprint and replace the existing network with a new Terraform deployment.
choices.B: Use Google Cloud Setup to recreate the organization so security controls can be added more easily later.
choices.C: Use Cloud DNS and Cloud CDN as the main security controls because they also provide traffic protection.
choices.D: Use the networking resources under Secure to design protections for applications and network traffic.

questions.explanation: The Secure category in the networking resources is specifically for protecting applications and network traffic in an existing Google Cloud deployment.

questions.question: A retail company is designing its first Google Cloud landing zone for multiple business units and expects to connect the environment to an existing on-premises network later. The network team wants to avoid future IP overlap, keep the design easy to operate, and use descriptive names that clearly identify environment and region. They also want the architecture to scale as new projects are added without having to redesign the foundational network. What should you do?

choices.A: Use VPC Network Peering between multiple auto mode VPC networks so each business unit can keep its own address space.
choices.B: Create an auto mode VPC network so Google Cloud automatically adds subnets in each new region and reduces the need for early planning.
choices.C: Create separate Shared VPC networks per application and use Cloud Router to dynamically create new subnets as business units expand.
choices.D: Create custom mode VPC networks with clearly named subnets and establish an IP addressing plan that avoids overlap with on-premises ranges.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks are recommended for enterprise environments because they support deliberate IP planning, avoid the fixed auto mode ranges, and allow clear subnet naming. This approach is better for future on-premises connectivity and long-term maintainability.

questions.question: A global manufacturing company is migrating workloads into Google Cloud and must integrate with several existing data centers that already use carefully managed RFC 1918 ranges. The network architects need to reduce the chance of future route conflicts, keep regional expansion simple, and ensure subnets are understandable to operations teams. The company expects multiple application teams to build on the same foundation over time. What should you do?

choices.A: Use auto mode VPC networks in each project so Google Cloud assigns standard subnet ranges consistently across the organization.
choices.B: Use custom mode VPC networks with a centrally managed IP address plan and descriptive regional subnet names.
choices.C: Create one VPC network per zone and expand it later with VPC Network Peering when the address space becomes constrained.
choices.D: Deploy all workloads into a single subnet and rely on Cloud NAT for separation between applications and business units.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks fit enterprise IP management because they let you choose subnet ranges that avoid overlaps with on-premises networks. Central planning and descriptive subnet naming also improve scalability and operations.

questions.question: A software company has a central platform team that manages networking and security, while multiple application teams need to deploy workloads in separate projects. The company wants consistent network controls, simpler governance, and a design that avoids duplicating VPC configuration in every project. The platform team also wants to use a predictable naming pattern for networks, subnets, and firewall rules. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a Shared VPC host project with custom mode subnets and attach the application projects as service projects.
choices.B: Place all applications in the same project and use IAM conditions to separate network administration by team.
choices.C: Use VPC Network Peering between project-specific VPC networks so each application team can maintain separate routing policies.
choices.D: Create one auto mode VPC network in each application project and let each team manage its own firewall rules independently.

questions.explanation: Shared VPC is the right design when a central team should control networking while multiple application projects consume shared subnets. It provides consistent governance and avoids duplicating network configuration across projects.

questions.question: An enterprise architecture group is standardizing Google Cloud networking for several regions and environments. Operations teams report that current resource names make it hard to identify which VPC, subnet, or firewall rule belongs to which application and environment. The architects want a naming scheme that is consistent, concise, and easy to understand for both administrators and developers. What should you do?

choices.A: Use different naming patterns for each project so teams can choose names that best match their local conventions.
choices.B: Rely on labels only for all VPC resources because labels automatically replace the need for readable resource names.
choices.C: Adopt a single, consistent naming convention for VPCs, subnets, firewall rules, and routes that includes company, environment, and region identifiers.
choices.D: Use randomly generated resource names and store the mapping in a separate Cloud Storage bucket for reference.

questions.explanation: Consistent, intuitive naming is a best practice for VPC design because it makes resources easier to manage, audit, and troubleshoot. Including company, environment, and region identifiers improves clarity across teams and projects.

questions.question: A financial services company is planning its Google Cloud network architecture and expects strict oversight from security, operations, and application owners. The architects want to reduce complexity, avoid later disruptive redesigns, and create a foundation that can support incremental workload migration. The solution must also be understandable to new administrators joining the team. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy each workload in a separate project with its own isolated routing and postpone network architecture decisions until go-live.
choices.B: Use auto mode VPC networks so the platform can grow quickly without requiring early stakeholder alignment.
choices.C: Start VPC network design early, involve key stakeholders, and keep the topology as simple as possible.
choices.D: Design multiple overlapping VPC networks now and consolidate them later with Cloud VPN after workloads are migrated.

questions.explanation: Google recommends considering VPC design early, involving the right decision makers, and keeping the topology simple. This reduces the risk of disruptive redesign and creates a stable architecture for future growth.

questions.question: A retail company is moving its first enterprise workloads to Google Cloud and wants a secure foundation before any applications are deployed. The solution must support multiple environments, centralize identity, and allow shared networking across application projects. The company also needs outbound internet access for private workloads without assigning external IP addresses, and it wants a structure that can grow as more teams adopt Google Cloud. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy all workloads in one Shared VPC project, use Cloud VPN for outbound internet access, and isolate environments with firewall rules only.
choices.B: Create a single project with separate VPC networks for each application team, use Cloud NAT for all internet access, and manage users directly with Cloud IAM in each project.
choices.C: Build a landing zone with an organization resource, Cloud Identity, and a Shared VPC per environment, then attach service projects and use Cloud NAT for private egress.
choices.D: Create separate standalone projects for each workload, use VPC peering between them, and rely on Cloud DNS private zones for network segmentation.

questions.explanation: A landing zone should be established before enterprise workloads, with an organization resource, centralized identity, and a scalable hierarchy. Shared VPC supports multiple service projects across environments, and Cloud NAT provides outbound internet access without external IP addresses.

questions.question: A manufacturing company is designing a Google Cloud landing zone for workloads that will integrate with on-premises systems and a partner cloud environment. The architecture must support private access to Google Cloud services, central logging and monitoring, and DNS resolution for internal workloads. The company wants a modular design that can expand to production, development, and testing environments without redesigning the network. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Design a landing zone with Shared VPC networks for each environment, Cloud Interconnect for on-premises connectivity, Cloud DNS private zones, and Google Cloud Observability.
choices.B: Create a flat network with one project per application, use Cloud Interconnect only for internet egress, and centralize logging in BigQuery.
choices.C: Deploy workloads in isolated projects, use VPC Network Peering between environments, and enable VPC Service Controls instead of hybrid connectivity.
choices.D: Use a separate VPC network in every project, connect on-premises systems with Cloud VPN, and publish internal records in Cloud DNS public zones.

questions.explanation: A modular landing zone commonly uses Shared VPCs per environment, hybrid connectivity with Cloud Interconnect, private DNS for internal resolution, and central observability with Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging.

questions.topic: IAM roles and hierarchical access control
questions.question: A retail company is moving multiple application teams to Google Cloud. Each team needs access to its own project resources, but the security team wants a single way to manage access across all current and future projects in a folder. The company must avoid giving broad permissions, and it wants permissions to inherit automatically as new projects are created. What should you do?

choices.A: Use service accounts for each team and assign them Owner permissions on the projects they need to manage.
choices.B: Create custom roles for each team and attach them directly to individual Compute Engine instances and Cloud Storage buckets.
choices.C: Create Google Groups for each team and grant predefined IAM roles on the folder so that access is inherited by all projects in that folder.
choices.D: Grant basic roles such as Editor to each team at the organization level, and use Cloud Identity groups to separate users by department.

questions.explanation: Folder-level IAM policy inheritance is the best fit because it lets access apply consistently to all current and future projects in the folder while using predefined roles for least privilege. Google Groups are appropriate human principals for team-based access.

questions.question: A platform team runs a containerized application on Google Kubernetes Engine that needs to call Cloud Storage and Pub/Sub without storing service account keys. The security team requires short-lived credentials, and the developers want each workload to have its own identity. The solution must avoid sharing a single identity across all clusters. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a service account key file and mount it into each pod as a Kubernetes secret.
choices.B: Create a workload identity pool and use Workload Identity Federation for the GKE pods to access Google Cloud services.
choices.C: Grant the node service account broad Editor access so all pods can use the node identity.
choices.D: Create a Google Group for the developers and grant the group roles/storage.objectAdmin on the bucket.

questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation provides short-lived credentials and a per-workload identity model without service account keys. It is the recommended approach for GKE workloads that need to access Google Cloud services securely.

questions.question: A finance application team needs permission to publish messages to an existing Pub/Sub topic, but they must not be able to create topics, delete subscriptions, or access unrelated Google Cloud resources. The team will be managed by a central platform group that wants the simplest option with low maintenance. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Grant the team the Pub/Sub Publisher predefined role on the topic or project that contains the topic.
choices.B: Create a custom IAM role with all Pub/Sub permissions and attach it to the project for future flexibility.
choices.C: Assign the team the Storage Object Admin role on a Cloud Storage bucket and route messages through files.
choices.D: Grant the team the Pub/Sub Subscriber role on the project so they can consume and manage messages.

questions.explanation: The Pub/Sub Publisher predefined role is the least-privilege choice for publishing messages. It is easier to maintain than a custom role and avoids granting unnecessary administrative permissions.

questions.question: A healthcare organization has one organization, several folders for business units, and many projects under each folder. The compliance team wants auditors to have read-only access to all projects in one business unit without granting access to projects in other units. The access should remain effective as new projects are added under that business unit. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant the auditors the Viewer role on the folder that contains the business unit projects.
choices.B: Grant the auditors the Viewer role on the organization so they can inspect all projects in every folder.
choices.C: Create a custom role for auditors and apply it to each Compute Engine instance in the business unit.
choices.D: Grant the auditors the Viewer role only on individual projects and update the policy each time a new project is created.

questions.explanation: Granting the role at the folder level scopes access to the business unit while ensuring that all projects in that folder inherit the policy, including future projects. This matches the compliance team's requirement for consistent read-only access.

questions.question: A software company has 200 engineers who need access to a shared development project, but only a subset should be able to deploy Cloud Run services. The company wants to minimize IAM policy changes when people join or leave the team. Access must be managed using the team's corporate directory. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant the entire project the basic Owner role and rely on Cloud Audit Logs for accountability.
choices.B: Create a Google Group for the deployers and another Google Group for all engineers, then bind predefined IAM roles to those groups.
choices.C: Grant each engineer individual IAM bindings on the project and update them whenever staffing changes occur.
choices.D: Use service accounts for each engineer and distribute keys through Cloud Secret Manager.

questions.explanation: Google Groups are designed for human principals and reduce policy churn when team membership changes. Binding predefined IAM roles to groups is the cleanest way to separate general project access from deployment permissions.

questions.question: A retail company runs a web application on a Compute Engine managed instance group behind an external HTTP(S) load balancer. Traffic is steady most of the month, but it spikes sharply during a quarterly promotion, and the engineering team wants the platform to add capacity automatically when CPU demand rises and remove it when demand falls. They need to keep costs low during quiet periods, avoid manual intervention during sudden traffic surges, and maintain the same application image across all instances. What should you do?

choices.A: Migrate the application to a Cloud Run service and configure request concurrency so the service scales only during peak hours.
choices.B: Create additional regional persistent disks for each instance and use Cloud Storage buckets to absorb the extra traffic during the promotion.
choices.C: Use GKE Standard with manual node pool resizing and deploy the application as a DaemonSet to add capacity during the promotion.
choices.D: Configure a Compute Engine managed instance group autoscaler based on CPU utilization and set the minimum and maximum instance counts for the expected load.

questions.explanation: A managed instance group autoscaler is the right Compute Engine feature for elastic horizontal scaling based on real-time metrics such as CPU utilization. It adds and removes VM instances automatically within configured bounds, which matches the need for cost efficiency and rapid response to traffic spikes.

questions.question: A media company runs a Compute Engine managed instance group for a streaming recommendation service. Historical metrics show a reliable traffic increase every weekday morning, and the team wants new VMs available before the spike occurs so users do not experience slow responses while instances start. They also want the system to continue reacting to unexpected bursts outside the normal pattern. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Spanner autoscaling to forecast future CPU load for the recommendation service and increase instance capacity ahead of time.
choices.B: Configure a GKE Horizontal Pod Autoscaler with custom metrics and disable all CPU-based scaling for the Compute Engine layer.
choices.C: Enable predictive autoscaling on the managed instance group and keep CPU-based autoscaling as the reactive signal.
choices.D: Move the workload to Cloud Run functions so each request creates a new execution environment from historical load patterns.

questions.explanation: Predictive autoscaling is designed for workloads with repeatable patterns and works with historical metrics to scale before the spike arrives, while still allowing reactive scaling for unexpected demand. This directly matches the requirement for both forecasted and sudden load changes on Compute Engine VMs.

questions.question: A startup is building an API that processes bursts of image uploads from mobile devices. The workload is unpredictable, traffic can drop to zero at night, and the developers want to avoid managing virtual machines or node pools. They need the service to scale out quickly when uploads surge and scale down automatically when requests stop. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Run the API on GKE Standard with a manually sized node pool and use the Vertical Pod Autoscaler for request spikes.
choices.B: Deploy the API on a Compute Engine managed instance group with a fixed minimum of three VMs and attach regional persistent disks.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run to deploy the API as a container service and let it autoscale based on incoming requests.
choices.D: Host the API on BigQuery and use scheduled queries to process upload events during periods of high demand.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run is an inherently elastic serverless service that scales automatically with incoming requests and can scale down to zero when idle. That makes it the best fit for unpredictable bursty traffic without VM or cluster management.

questions.question: A development team is modernizing a microservices application that currently runs on virtual machines. They want Kubernetes features for service discovery and rolling updates, but they do not want to manage node pools, cluster autoscaling settings, or node provisioning. The application traffic varies throughout the day, and the team wants the platform to adjust compute capacity automatically while minimizing operational overhead. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Run the microservices on Compute Engine instance templates with a managed instance group and deploy Kubernetes on top of each VM.
choices.B: Create a GKE Standard cluster and manage node pools manually while using Cloud Build triggers for each deployment.
choices.C: Deploy the services on Cloud Run and use VPC Access connectors to mimic Kubernetes pod networking.
choices.D: Use GKE Autopilot so Google manages node provisioning and pod-driven scaling for the workloads.

questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot is the best choice when the team needs Kubernetes capabilities but wants Google to handle node management and autoscaling behavior. It reduces operational overhead while still supporting elastic workloads.

questions.question: An e-commerce company must process order events from a Pub/Sub topic and trigger downstream inventory updates, email notifications, and billing workflows. The event volume is highly variable, sometimes reaching zero for hours and then surging suddenly during marketing campaigns. The platform team wants a solution that scales independently for each service and requires minimal capacity planning. What should you do?

choices.A: Use GKE Standard with a fixed node pool and manually scale pods based on message backlog in Cloud Monitoring.
choices.B: Deploy the event processors on Compute Engine managed instance groups and use Cloud Load Balancing to distribute Pub/Sub messages.
choices.C: Create a Cloud SQL instance for the event processors and use scheduled exports to handle the burst periods.
choices.D: Use Pub/Sub with Cloud Run, Eventarc, and Workflows so each service scales automatically as events arrive.

questions.explanation: Pub/Sub, Cloud Run, Eventarc, and Workflows are all serverless or event-driven services that provide elastic scaling without capacity planning. They are well suited for independently scaling event processing components and can handle bursts with minimal operational effort.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is standardizing new Google Cloud environments for its growing commerce and catalog workloads. The platform team needs a secure landing zone that includes organization, billing, networking, monitoring, and security foundations, and they want to move quickly without designing everything from scratch. They also need the setup to support future connectivity to on-premises systems and to be based on Google-recommended practices. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Anthos Config Management to create all projects and networks, then connect the environment to on-premises systems with Identity-Aware Proxy.
choices.B: Deploy the enterprise foundations blueprint with Terraform to provision the landing zone, then extend it with Shared VPC and Cloud Interconnect as needed.
choices.C: Build the network with Cloud VPN first, then add Compute Engine bastion hosts and manually create each project, folder, and IAM binding as the environment expands.
choices.D: Create the foundation with Google Cloud Setup in the Cloud Console and use its guided workflow to configure organization structure, billing, networking, monitoring, and security.

questions.explanation: The enterprise foundations blueprint provides a secure, best-practice landing zone as Terraform. It fits Cymbal Retail's need for a repeatable foundation that can be extended for future connectivity and scaling.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is migrating several workloads that still depend on on-premises databases and SFTP-based batch transfers. The company needs private, high-throughput connectivity between its data center and Google Cloud, and it wants predictable network performance for steady traffic. The solution must support both current hybrid applications and future expansion to additional Google Cloud services. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Create a VPC peering connection to the on-premises network and use Network Connectivity Center to route traffic through it.
choices.B: Move all file transfers to Cloud Storage and use Identity-Aware Proxy to replace the need for hybrid network connectivity.
choices.C: Use Cloud VPN with HA VPN gateways and Cloud Router to provide encrypted connectivity over the public internet.
choices.D: Deploy Cloud Interconnect with Cloud Router to establish private connectivity and dynamic routing between on-premises and Google Cloud.

questions.explanation: Cloud Interconnect is the best choice for private, high-throughput hybrid connectivity with predictable performance. Cloud Router provides dynamic route exchange for scalable routing between on-premises and Google Cloud.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail runs containerized applications that support product discovery and catalog services. The platform team wants a managed Kubernetes environment that reduces operational overhead, supports autoscaling, and integrates cleanly with Google Cloud networking and observability. They also want to keep the option to use existing container deployment patterns with minimal application changes. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Migrate the containers to App Engine standard environment and use Cloud Tasks to manage all network ingress.
choices.B: Deploy the workloads on Google Kubernetes Engine and use node pools, Cluster Autoscaler, and Cloud Monitoring for operations.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run for all services, including the stateful catalog services, so that Kubernetes operations are completely eliminated.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine Managed Instance Groups with startup scripts to run the containers on VM-based nodes.

questions.explanation: Google Kubernetes Engine provides a managed Kubernetes platform with autoscaling and strong integration with Google Cloud networking and observability. It aligns well with Cymbal Retail's existing containerized workloads.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is modernizing a MySQL-based product catalog service that currently runs on-premises. The team wants to reduce database administration effort, preserve high availability, and minimize application changes during migration. They need a managed relational database service that can support production traffic and simplify backups and maintenance. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Deploy MySQL on Compute Engine VMs and use Persistent Disk snapshots to handle backups and patching.
choices.B: Migrate the database to BigQuery and update the application to use SQL queries directly against analytic tables.
choices.C: Migrate the workload to Cloud SQL for MySQL and use Database Migration Service to move data with minimal downtime.
choices.D: Use Cloud Spanner because it provides a distributed relational database with automatic scaling for every MySQL workload.

questions.explanation: Cloud SQL for MySQL is the managed relational database option that minimizes operational overhead while preserving compatibility. Database Migration Service helps move the existing MySQL data with minimal downtime.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants its applications in a Shared VPC to access Google-managed services and APIs without sending traffic over the public internet. The security team requires private access from internal subnets, and the platform team wants to minimize the number of public endpoints exposed. The solution should work well for managed Google services used by catalog enrichment and data pipelines. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Configure Private Google Access on the subnets and use Cloud DNS forwarding for all outbound traffic.
choices.B: Enable Private Service Connect endpoints for the required Google services and direct application traffic to those private endpoints.
choices.C: Deploy a Cloud Load Balancing external application load balancer and route internal requests through it to Google APIs.
choices.D: Use Cloud NAT and public IP addresses so instances can reach Google APIs through egress-only internet access.

questions.explanation: Private Service Connect lets Cymbal Retail consume supported Google services through private endpoints, reducing exposure to the public internet. It is a strong fit for private access from applications in a Shared VPC.

questions.question: A retail company runs dozens of daily BigQuery reports for its analytics team, but several queries have become slow and unpredictable during peak hours. The team wants to identify whether the issue is caused by excessive bytes read, shuffle-heavy stages, or slot contention, and they want to use a Google Cloud-native tool to investigate. They also want a way to review query behavior over time without changing the SQL first. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Monitoring and the BigQuery administrative resource charts to inspect query resource usage over time, and then analyze the query plan for the slow jobs.
choices.B: Use Cloud SQL Insights and Database Center to profile the queries and determine which reports are consuming the most compute resources.
choices.C: Use Dataflow monitoring and Cloud Logging to trace the SQL execution path and identify which report jobs are reading the most data.
choices.D: Use BigQuery BI Engine to accelerate all reporting queries and rely on its in-memory cache to diagnose the slowdown automatically.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring and the BigQuery administrative resource charts let the team evaluate job behavior over time, and the query plan helps identify bytes read, slot time, and expensive stages.

questions.question: A media analytics team uses BigQuery as the backend for an executive dashboard that repeatedly queries a small set of frequently accessed tables. The dashboard must return results quickly during business hours, and the team wants to improve latency without rewriting the SQL or adding a separate caching layer. The workload is mostly interactive BI traffic with repeated filters and aggregations. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud SQL with read replicas and migrate the dashboard SQL to standard PostgreSQL queries.
choices.B: Enable BigQuery BI Engine for the project so frequently accessed data can be cached in memory and accelerate the dashboard queries.
choices.C: Create a Memorystore for Redis cache and have the dashboard application query Redis instead of BigQuery.
choices.D: Move the dashboard data into Cloud Spanner and use the BigQuery connector for Spanner to serve all interactive queries.

questions.explanation: BigQuery BI Engine is designed to accelerate repeated BI-style SQL queries by using in-memory caching, often without query changes.

questions.question: A SaaS company has a nightly BigQuery transformation pipeline and an analyst team that runs ad hoc queries throughout the day. The company needs predictable monthly spending for query processing and wants to reduce performance variation during peak usage. They also expect workload spikes during the end-of-month close and want extra capacity only when needed. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery capacity-based pricing with slot commitments and enable autoscaling slots for periods of higher demand.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run to execute the SQL jobs in containers so you can control compute capacity directly.
choices.C: Move the workloads to Bigtable and use autoscaling nodes to provide predictable query performance.
choices.D: Keep all queries on on-demand pricing and increase table partitioning to guarantee more query slots during peak hours.

questions.explanation: Capacity-based pricing with committed slots provides predictable spend, and autoscaling slots add temporary capacity for spikes and concurrency.

questions.question: An e-commerce company has a BigQuery query that joins several large tables and occasionally runs much slower than expected. The data engineering team needs to understand which stage of the query is consuming the most resources and whether the bottleneck is caused by scanning, shuffling, or a complex join. They want a built-in Google Cloud feature that shows execution statistics for each stage. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Dataplex data profiling to identify the most expensive table columns and reduce query latency.
choices.B: Use Cloud Logging to filter BigQuery audit logs and estimate the time spent in each SQL clause.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Workbench to run the query in a notebook and compare the runtime across multiple executions.
choices.D: Use the BigQuery query plan and query execution graph to inspect stage-level bytes read and slot time consumed.

questions.explanation: The query plan and execution graph provide stage-level execution details, including bytes read and slot time, which are needed to diagnose the bottleneck.

questions.question: A financial services company runs a mix of scheduled ETL jobs and analyst queries in BigQuery, and several interactive queries are getting queued during busy periods. The platform team wants to improve concurrency for the highest-priority workloads while keeping the rest of the environment stable. They also want a solution that works with BigQuery's built-in capacity model rather than moving the data elsewhere. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Place the datasets behind Cloud CDN so queued queries can read cached results from the edge.
choices.B: Use BigQuery materialized views for every query so that all queued jobs are guaranteed to run immediately.
choices.C: Adjust BigQuery slot reservations to assign more dedicated capacity to the interactive workload and use priority-aware scheduling where appropriate.
choices.D: Increase the number of GKE nodes running batch jobs so BigQuery queries can borrow compute from Kubernetes during peak periods.

questions.explanation: BigQuery slot reservations are the native way to allocate dedicated capacity to important workloads and improve concurrency and consistency.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail plans to migrate several customer-facing and internal services from on-premises systems into Google Cloud over the next year. The network team must avoid IP range overlap with existing on-premises ranges, keep the design easy to operate, and support future expansion into additional regions. They also want separate environments for development, test, and production without creating unnecessary network complexity. What should you do?

choices.A: Create custom mode VPC networks with planned subnet CIDR ranges for each environment and use Shared VPC to centralize network administration.
choices.B: Create multiple VPC Network Peering connections between auto mode VPC networks so each team can manage its own IP ranges independently.
choices.C: Create auto mode VPC networks for each environment and use Shared VPC later if overlapping subnets become a problem.
choices.D: Create a single default VPC network, then use firewall rules and tags to logically separate development, test, and production workloads.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks let Cymbal choose non-overlapping IP ranges that align with on-premises addressing, and Shared VPC supports centralized administration while keeping environments separated and scalable.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is moving catalog data processing to Google Cloud, but its order system and SFTP-based integrations must continue to exchange data with on-premises applications during the migration. The network must provide private connectivity, predictable routing, and support growth as more workloads move to Google Cloud. The team wants a managed option that can connect multiple VPC networks and on-premises sites without depending on public internet paths. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud VPN with Cloud NAT for all communication and place the applications in separate VPC networks.
choices.B: Use Transfer Appliance for continuous connectivity and route application traffic through Cloud Storage buckets.
choices.C: Provision Cloud Interconnect and Cloud Router to provide private, dynamic connectivity between on-premises systems and Google Cloud.
choices.D: Use VPC Network Peering between the cloud VPC and the on-premises network, then route traffic through firewall rules.

questions.explanation: Cloud Interconnect provides private connectivity, and Cloud Router exchanges routes dynamically so Cymbal can support hybrid traffic as the migration progresses.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is containerizing a product-discovery service that has steady baseline traffic with occasional spikes during promotions. The platform team needs a managed Kubernetes environment that reduces operational overhead, supports autoscaling, and integrates with Google Cloud networking and load balancing. The service must also remain highly available across failures in a zone. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the service on Compute Engine managed instance groups and install Kubernetes manually on each VM.
choices.B: Create a GKE Standard regional cluster and enable Cluster Autoscaler and node auto-provisioning for the workload.
choices.C: Deploy the service on Cloud Run and expose it through an internal TCP/UDP load balancer.
choices.D: Use App Engine standard environment and connect it to the existing container registry for all runtime needs.

questions.explanation: A regional GKE Standard cluster provides managed Kubernetes with high availability across zones, while autoscaling features help handle variable promotion traffic.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail still receives large supplier product feeds through legacy SFTP deliveries, and those files must be ingested into Google Cloud for downstream processing. The solution should be managed, support secure file landing, and integrate with existing ETL pipelines that run after each batch arrives. The team wants to minimize custom server management while keeping the files durable and available for processing. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to ingest the SFTP files directly into BigQuery tables.
choices.B: Use Filestore as the landing zone for supplier files and trigger batch jobs with Cloud Scheduler.
choices.C: Provision a Compute Engine VM with an SFTP server and store the incoming files on Persistent Disk.
choices.D: Use Storage Transfer Service with an SFTP source to move files into Cloud Storage for downstream ETL processing.

questions.explanation: Storage Transfer Service supports managed transfers from SFTP to Cloud Storage, which gives Cymbal a durable landing zone for batch processing without operating an SFTP server.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is modernizing its product catalog platform and wants to reduce the operational burden of managing MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server databases. The company needs a managed database service with automated backups, maintenance, and high availability for transactional workloads. It also wants to preserve compatibility with existing relational application logic while simplifying administration. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the databases on Compute Engine VMs and configure custom backup scripts and replication jobs.
choices.B: Migrate the transactional databases to Cloud SQL for MySQL and SQL Server with high availability enabled.
choices.C: Move the workloads to Memorystore for Redis and rewrite the application to use key-value access patterns.
choices.D: Migrate the databases to BigQuery and use scheduled queries to emulate transactional behavior.

questions.explanation: Cloud SQL provides fully managed relational databases with automated backups, maintenance, and HA, which aligns with Cymbal's goal of reducing operational overhead while keeping relational compatibility.

questions.question: A retail company runs its checkout API on Compute Engine instances in a managed instance group behind a global external HTTP(S) load balancer. The team knows that traffic spikes every year during a planned holiday sale, and the application must stay responsive when demand increases by about 4x for several hours. They want to avoid users waiting for instances to be created during the sale, and they also want to reduce capacity again after the event ends. What should you do?

choices.A: Manually scale out the Compute Engine managed instance group before the known peak period and set an autoscaling policy to scale based on CPU utilization during the event.
choices.B: Increase the machine type of the Compute Engine instances in the managed instance group and keep the same fixed instance count throughout the sale period.
choices.C: Move the checkout API to Cloud Run and use Cloud Tasks to buffer all requests until the sale ends.
choices.D: Use Google Kubernetes Engine Standard with a fixed node pool size and rely on the load balancer to distribute traffic evenly.

questions.explanation: For a known peak period, the recommended approach is to plan ahead and scale up or scale out before the surge so capacity is ready immediately. An autoscaling policy can still reduce resources after the event and react to additional demand.

questions.question: A media company runs a user-upload service on Compute Engine in a managed instance group. Traffic is unpredictable, and the service becomes slow when average CPU utilization stays above 75% for several minutes. The team wants the system to add capacity automatically during sudden spikes and remove instances when load drops. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Deploy the service on Cloud Run and configure minimum instances to keep capacity available at all times.
choices.B: Place the service behind Cloud CDN and increase the backend service timeout to absorb the spike.
choices.C: Configure a Compute Engine managed instance group autoscaler with CPU-based metrics and set the target utilization to 75%.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine sole-tenant nodes and increase the vCPU count on each VM whenever CPU exceeds 75%.

questions.explanation: For unexpected load, a MIG autoscaler using real-time metrics is the appropriate elastic scaling option. CPU-based autoscaling adds and removes instances automatically as demand changes.

questions.question: A subscription platform runs a web tier on Compute Engine managed instance groups. Usage patterns are highly seasonal, and historical metrics show that traffic rises every weekday morning before the support center opens. The operations team wants the platform to add instances earlier than reactive autoscaling would, so users do not experience slow page loads during the morning ramp-up. What should you do?

choices.A: Enable predictive autoscaling on the Compute Engine managed instance group and base scaling on historical CPU utilization trends.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run with always-on minimum instances to guarantee morning capacity.
choices.C: Replace the web tier with Google Kubernetes Engine Autopilot and use Horizontal Pod Autoscaling only.
choices.D: Increase the maximum size of the managed instance group and keep manual scaling disabled.

questions.explanation: Predictive autoscaling is designed for workloads with recurring patterns because it forecasts future load from historical metrics and scales earlier than reactive policies can.

questions.question: A startup is building an event-processing pipeline that receives irregular bursts of messages from Pub/Sub. The team wants the processing layer to scale out instantly during spikes and scale down to zero when no events are arriving. They want to minimize operational overhead and avoid managing servers or node pools. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud SQL with read replicas to absorb the burst of incoming messages.
choices.B: Run the processors on Compute Engine virtual machines in a managed instance group and configure custom autoscaling policies.
choices.C: Use Google Kubernetes Engine Standard with cluster autoscaling and a fixed Deployment replica count.
choices.D: Deploy the processors on Cloud Run and trigger them from Pub/Sub through Eventarc.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run is inherently elastic and can scale quickly, including down to zero. Pairing Pub/Sub with Eventarc is a serverless way to process event bursts without managing servers.

questions.question: A software company runs a microservices platform on Google Kubernetes Engine and wants Google Cloud to manage the underlying node provisioning as demand changes. The platform has varying traffic across services, and the team wants each service to consume only the compute resources it needs without overprovisioning the cluster. They also want to reduce the operational work required to maintain node pools. What should you do?

choices.A: Move the microservices to Cloud Run jobs and schedule them with Cloud Scheduler.
choices.B: Use GKE Autopilot and let Google manage node provisioning while workloads scale based on pod resource requests.
choices.C: Deploy the platform on GKE Standard with manually managed node pools and disable cluster autoscaling.
choices.D: Run the services on Compute Engine in a single managed instance group and use load balancer capacity recommendations.

questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot is the best fit when the goal is to reduce node management while keeping Kubernetes control. It helps with elastic resource consumption by managing the underlying infrastructure more automatically than Standard mode.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is building a new analytics pipeline to support product discovery dashboards and expects unpredictable spikes in usage during promotions. The data team needs consistent query performance for repeated BI queries, a predictable monthly cost, and enough capacity for multiple analysts running dashboards at the same time. They also want to avoid changing existing SQL used by their reporting tools. What should you do?

choices.A: Export the data to Cloud Storage and query it with Dataproc Serverless Spark to improve concurrency for BI users.
choices.B: Create a BigQuery reservation with autoscaling slots and assign the reporting projects to that reservation.
choices.C: Use BigQuery on-demand pricing and rely on query result caching to absorb peak demand for all dashboard traffic.
choices.D: Move the dashboards to Cloud SQL and use read replicas to distribute analytical queries across the reporting workload.

questions.explanation: BigQuery reservations with autoscaling slots provide dedicated, predictable capacity for concurrent BI workloads without changing SQL, which fits Cymbal Retail's need for stable performance and cost control.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is migrating its product catalog reporting to BigQuery, and the merchandising team uses the same dashboard filters throughout the day. They need faster interactive performance for repeated analytical queries, want minimal application changes, and prefer a managed service rather than adding custom caching logic. What should you do?

choices.A: Move the dashboard queries to AlloyDB and use columnar indexes to speed up aggregations.
choices.B: Use Pub/Sub and Dataflow to cache dashboard results in Firestore for low-latency access.
choices.C: Create Cloud SQL read replicas and connect the dashboarding tool directly to the replicas.
choices.D: Enable BigQuery BI Engine for the datasets used by the merchandising dashboards.

questions.explanation: BigQuery BI Engine accelerates repeated in-memory analytics queries with minimal or no SQL changes, which is well suited to Cymbal Retail's interactive merchandising dashboards.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is moving its transactional order-management database from on-premises MySQL to Google Cloud. The application requires minimal downtime during maintenance, automatic failover if a zone becomes unavailable, and a fully managed database service. The team also wants to keep using standard MySQL clients and avoid redesigning the schema. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy MySQL on Compute Engine instances across two regions and manage failover with a custom script.
choices.B: Use Cloud Spanner for the order database and refactor the schema to fit global horizontal scaling.
choices.C: Create a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance with high availability enabled and use Database Migration Service for the cutover.
choices.D: Store the order data in Firestore in Datastore mode and use Cloud Functions to emulate relational transactions.

questions.explanation: Cloud SQL for MySQL with high availability provides managed MySQL compatibility and automatic failover, while Database Migration Service supports a lower-risk migration from on-premises MySQL.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail's product search service frequently reads the same inventory and pricing data, and repeated database queries are causing latency during traffic spikes. The engineering team wants a managed in-memory cache with low operational overhead, support for application-side cache-aside patterns, and better performance for the existing web application. What should you do?

choices.A: Store the hot product data in Cloud Storage and serve it through Cloud CDN for database offload.
choices.B: Deploy Redis on Compute Engine and manage the cluster topology, persistence, and patching yourself.
choices.C: Use Cloud Memorystore for Redis and integrate the application with a cache-aside design.
choices.D: Use BigQuery materialized views for the inventory data and point the web application to those views.

questions.explanation: Cloud Memorystore for Redis is the managed Redis service on Google Cloud and fits Cymbal Retail's need for a low-ops, in-memory cache to reduce latency and database load.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is modernizing its containerized customer-facing services by moving them to Google Cloud. The team wants Google-managed load balancing for external traffic, automatic scaling for the backend pods, and a deployment model that integrates cleanly with existing Kubernetes manifests. The solution must also support rolling updates with minimal disruption to the shopping experience. What should you do?

choices.A: Run the services on Compute Engine managed instance groups and install NGINX manually for load balancing.
choices.B: Create a GKE cluster, expose the service with a GKE Ingress, and use the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for workloads.
choices.C: Use App Engine standard environment and route all container traffic through Cloud Load Balancing.
choices.D: Deploy the services on Cloud Run and rewrite the manifests into Cloud Run jobs for traffic handling.

questions.explanation: GKE with Ingress and Horizontal Pod Autoscaler gives Cymbal Retail managed load balancing, autoscaling, and standard Kubernetes-based deployment patterns with rolling updates.

questions.question: A financial services company is creating its first Google Cloud landing zone for production workloads and expects to connect to an existing on-premises network later. The network team needs a design that avoids overlapping IP ranges, allows clear subnet naming, and supports future expansion across multiple regions. Security architects also want the VPC structure to be easy to understand and manage as new applications are added. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Shared VPC with all subnets created in the host project after workloads are deployed to keep the initial setup minimal.
choices.B: Create a custom mode VPC network with intentionally sized subnets and a consistent naming convention for networks, subnets, and firewall rules.
choices.C: Create an auto mode VPC network and add Cloud VPN later so Google Cloud can automatically manage new regional subnets.
choices.D: Create separate default VPC networks for each application team and connect them with VPC Network Peering when integration is needed.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks are recommended for production because they avoid auto mode IP range constraints, support better IP planning, and let you use clear, consistent naming conventions.

questions.question: A retail company is migrating multiple application tiers into Google Cloud and wants to simplify administration while keeping the network scalable. The architects expect several workloads in each region, but they want to minimize subnet sprawl and make future routing and firewall management easier. They also need to avoid creating too many small address blocks that would be hard to reuse later. What should you do?

choices.A: Create many small subnets, one per application instance, so each workload has a dedicated IP range.
choices.B: Use auto mode VPC subnets in every region so Google Cloud can allocate address space automatically as the company expands.
choices.C: Use a shared VPC network with Cloud NAT and put all workloads into a single subnet per region regardless of workload type.
choices.D: Group applications into fewer larger custom mode subnets and plan address ranges to accommodate growth within each region.

questions.explanation: The best practice is to group applications into fewer subnets with larger address ranges to reduce complexity and make planning and operations easier.

questions.question: A manufacturing company is standardizing Google Cloud resources across several business units and regions. The network operations team wants names that clearly identify the company, business unit, environment, and region so they can quickly troubleshoot and delegate ownership. They also want firewall rules and subnet names to be consistent with the same pattern across projects. What should you do?

choices.A: Adopt a consistent VPC naming convention for networks, subnets, firewall rules, and routes using company, business unit, region, and environment identifiers.
choices.B: Place all network resources in the same region and use identical names for subnets and firewall rules to simplify administration.
choices.C: Name resources with random suffixes so unique names are guaranteed across environments and regions.
choices.D: Use project IDs as the only naming standard and rely on labels to distinguish all VPC resources.

questions.explanation: A clear, consistent naming convention improves understandability and manageability across VPC networks, subnets, firewall rules, and routes.

questions.topic: Custom Mode vs Auto Mode VPC
questions.question: An engineering organization is evaluating how to set up networking for a new production platform in Google Cloud. The team plans to connect the environment to an existing corporate network and expects to peer with another VPC later. They want to avoid the limitations of automatically created subnets and keep IP planning aligned with the enterprise address strategy. What should you do?

choices.A: Use an auto mode VPC network because it automatically creates subnets in every new region and minimizes initial design work.
choices.B: Create a separate legacy network and use Cloud Router to automatically fix overlapping subnets later.
choices.C: Use default VPC networks for each environment because they provide a stable baseline for enterprise address management.
choices.D: Create a custom mode VPC network so you can control subnet creation, naming, and IP ranges from the beginning.

questions.explanation: Custom mode VPC networks are preferred for production because they give you explicit control over subnet creation and IP address planning, which is important for enterprise connectivity and peering.

questions.question: A media company is moving several departments to Google Cloud and wants the network architecture to remain understandable as more workloads are added. The networking team has been asked to balance future growth, operational simplicity, and consistent administration across regions. They also want to avoid a design that becomes disruptive if the company later needs to change how workloads are segmented. What should you do?

choices.A: Plan the VPC network early, keep the topology simple, and use custom mode VPC networks with clearly defined subnet boundaries.
choices.B: Delay VPC design until after the first production workloads are deployed so the segmentation model can follow application usage patterns.
choices.C: Use separate auto mode VPC networks for each department so each team can independently expand into new regions.
choices.D: Implement a hub-and-spoke design with Shared VPC and create many small subnets for each workload to maximize isolation.

questions.explanation: Google recommends considering VPC network design early and keeping the topology simple; custom mode networks support deliberate planning and simpler long-term management.

questions.question: A media company runs a GKE-based API that serves highly variable traffic during live events. The platform team wants the application to scale quickly when demand spikes, keep costs low during quiet periods, and avoid overprovisioning nodes. They also want scaling behavior to work well for both pod replicas and the underlying node pool. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the application on Cloud SQL replicas and use Memorystore for Redis to absorb traffic spikes instead of scaling GKE.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run for Anthos to replace the GKE workload and rely on manual instance resizing for capacity changes.
choices.C: Enable GKE Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for the workload and Cluster Autoscaler for the node pool so both pods and nodes scale with demand.
choices.D: Configure a fixed-size GKE Standard node pool and use Compute Engine instance groups outside Kubernetes to handle traffic spikes.

questions.explanation: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales pods based on demand, and Cluster Autoscaler adds or removes GKE nodes to match pending pod capacity. Together they provide responsive scaling and better cost efficiency.

questions.question: A financial services team runs a production application on GKE Standard and must keep the service available during upgrades. The team requires a controlled rollout, minimal disruption to users, and a way to verify each new version before moving all traffic. They also want to use native GKE capabilities rather than building a custom deployment system. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move the workload to Cloud Run and redeploy a new revision only after the entire cluster upgrade is complete.
choices.B: Use GKE release channels and perform a rolling node pool upgrade with surge capacity to keep workloads available during the upgrade.
choices.C: Create a new Compute Engine managed instance group for the application and migrate traffic after upgrading the old group.
choices.D: Back up the cluster with Backup for GKE and recreate the entire environment in a new region before each version upgrade.

questions.explanation: GKE release channels and rolling upgrades with surge capacity are designed for smooth, low-disruption cluster maintenance. They keep workloads available while nodes are upgraded.

questions.question: A retailer is deploying a new GKE cluster for customer-facing services. The cluster must use private nodes, expose only selected applications publicly, and support reliable network connectivity to on-premises systems over Cloud VPN. The team also wants consistent IP planning for Pods and Services to avoid overlap with corporate address space. What should you do?

choices.A: Place the cluster behind Cloud Armor only and use VPC peering to connect directly to the on-premises data center.
choices.B: Create a private GKE cluster in a VPC with alias IP ranges, use Cloud Load Balancing for public services, and connect to on-premises through Cloud VPN.
choices.C: Use Anthos Service Mesh to allocate all Pod and Service IP ranges and rely on Cloud Interconnect for public exposure.
choices.D: Deploy the cluster on a shared VPC subnet with external IP addresses on every node and route on-premises traffic through Cloud NAT.

questions.explanation: A private GKE cluster with alias IP ranges supports clean Pod and Service IP management, while Cloud Load Balancing exposes only required services and Cloud VPN provides private connectivity to on-premises.

questions.question: An online education company is standardizing new GKE clusters for multiple development teams. The security team wants to reduce the attack surface, prevent privileged container access where possible, and apply a consistent baseline across all clusters. They also want to keep cluster management simple for platform engineers. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use GKE Autopilot with Workload Identity and Kubernetes Network Policies to enforce a secure default posture for workloads.
choices.B: Run the workloads on Cloud Run jobs and use Secret Manager to replace all Kubernetes access controls.
choices.C: Create a standard GKE cluster with unrestricted node access and use Cloud Logging alerts to detect insecure pods after deployment.
choices.D: Deploy the clusters in Compute Engine instance groups and enforce security by using OS Login and Shielded VMs only.

questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot provides a strongly managed cluster baseline, Workload Identity reduces reliance on node service account keys, and Network Policies help restrict pod traffic. This combination aligns well with secure-by-default operations.

questions.question: A software company hosts several internal development teams on a shared GKE platform. Each team needs isolated workloads, different access permissions, and the ability to share cluster infrastructure without seeing each other's resources. The platform team wants a design that scales to many teams while minimizing cluster sprawl. What should you do?

choices.A: Create one separate GKE cluster per namespace and use Cloud DNS to isolate team workloads.
choices.B: Use GKE Enterprise multi-tenancy with namespaces, RBAC, and policy controls to isolate teams on a shared cluster.
choices.C: Move each team to a separate Cloud Run service and use Artifact Registry repositories to enforce namespace isolation.
choices.D: Use GKE node pools with one node pool per team and rely on Cloud IAM to isolate Kubernetes objects.

questions.explanation: GKE Enterprise multi-tenancy is intended for securely running multiple teams on a shared cluster. Namespaces, RBAC, and policy controls provide the required isolation without excessive cluster sprawl.

questions.question: Altostrat Media runs containerized content services on GKE in Google Cloud and also maintains on-premises systems that still process media ingestion. The platform team wants a single way to manage both Kubernetes fleets, enforce consistent policies, and simplify application rollout across environments. They need scalable Kubernetes operations, centralized visibility, and minimal change to existing cluster runtimes. What should you do?

choices.A: Migrate the on-premises workloads to Cloud Run and use Cloud Scheduler to replace the Kubernetes-based deployment model.
choices.B: Deploy Anthos Config Management and register the on-premises Kubernetes clusters and GKE clusters to a single fleet in Google Cloud.
choices.C: Use Cloud Build triggers and Artifact Registry to deploy directly to each cluster without a centralized Kubernetes management layer.
choices.D: Create separate GKE clusters in each environment and manage application configuration with Secret Manager only.

questions.explanation: Anthos Config Management with fleet registration provides centralized policy and configuration management across both on-premises Kubernetes and GKE, which fits Altostrat Media's hybrid Kubernetes requirement.

questions.question: Altostrat Media needs secure, high-performance connectivity from on-premises media ingestion systems to Google Cloud for transferring large video files and metadata. The network team wants a solution with predictable throughput, private connectivity, and support for ongoing hybrid operations. Internet-based transfer is not acceptable for the ingestion pipeline. What should you do?

choices.A: Provision Cloud Interconnect with Cloud Router to establish private, high-throughput connectivity between on-premises and Google Cloud.
choices.B: Use VPC peering between the on-premises network and a VPC to route media ingestion traffic privately.
choices.C: Deploy Transfer Appliance for every ingestion job and replace the live hybrid pipeline with batch shipping only.
choices.D: Use Cloud VPN with Cloud Router and send all ingestion traffic over the public internet through Cloud NAT.

questions.explanation: Cloud Interconnect with Cloud Router provides the private, high-performance, and predictable connectivity needed for large hybrid ingestion workloads.

questions.question: Altostrat Media stores a rapidly growing library of audio and video assets in Cloud Storage. Most newly uploaded media is accessed frequently for a short period, but older assets are rarely read and must still remain highly available and scalable. The storage team wants to reduce costs without changing application access patterns. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Create Cloud Storage lifecycle policies to transition older objects from Standard to Nearline or Coldline storage.
choices.B: Enable Cloud CDN for the bucket and keep all media in Standard storage permanently.
choices.C: Use Filestore for the media repository and configure snapshots to lower long-term storage expense.
choices.D: Move all objects to Cloud SQL and use read replicas to reduce object storage costs.

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage lifecycle policies can automatically transition infrequently accessed media to cheaper storage classes while preserving availability and object-based access.

questions.question: Altostrat Media wants to automate video transcoding, metadata extraction, and personalized recommendation updates whenever a new object is uploaded to Cloud Storage.
