The team wants a serverless approach that scales automatically, supports event-driven execution, and minimizes infrastructure management. They already use containerized services and want to keep the processing logic lightweight. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Workflows to query BigQuery directly on a schedule and write results back to Cloud Storage.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run services behind an HTTPS load balancer and poll Cloud Storage for new uploads every minute.
choices.C: Use GKE Autopilot and deploy a cron-based sidecar to watch the bucket for new files.
choices.D: Use Eventarc to trigger Cloud Run functions when Cloud Storage object-finalize events occur.

questions.explanation: Eventarc with Cloud Run functions is a serverless, event-driven pattern that fits object-finalize triggers for transcoding and metadata workflows.

questions.question: Altostrat Media wants to enrich its content platform with natural language interactions, automatic summaries, content moderation, and rich metadata extraction from audio, video, and documents. The architecture must use managed Google AI services so the team can launch quickly without training custom models from scratch. They also want support for video analysis and text-based conversational experiences. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Vertex AI, the Speech-to-Text API, the Video Intelligence API, the Natural Language API, and the Gemini API to power the media workflows.
choices.B: Use Apigee and Cloud Functions to proxy requests to the public internet for third-party AI services.
choices.C: Use Cloud Dataflow and Pub/Sub to build custom NLP and computer vision models inside streaming pipelines.
choices.D: Use Cloud SQL, Bigtable, and Memorystore to infer summaries and recommendations from stored media metadata.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI and Google prebuilt AI APIs provide managed generative AI, speech, vision, and NLP capabilities that match Altostrat Media's summarization, moderation, and conversational requirements.

questions.question: A retail company uses Google Cloud projects for separate development, testing, and production environments. The security team wants application operators to manage Compute Engine instances only in the production project, while auditors must be able to view resource configuration across all projects in the folder. The company also wants to avoid granting overly broad permissions and prefers a design that leverages Google Cloud's resource hierarchy. What should you do?

choices.A: Create custom roles for all users, then bind the roles at the organization level so access automatically applies to every current and future resource.
choices.B: Use Cloud Identity groups to authenticate users, then rely on project labels to control access to Compute Engine instances and audit data.
choices.C: Grant the operators the basic Editor role on the organization and grant the auditors the basic Viewer role on each individual project.
choices.D: Grant the operators a predefined Compute Engine role on the production project and grant the auditors a predefined Viewer role on the folder that contains all projects.

questions.explanation: IAM roles should be granted on the narrowest resource that meets the requirement. A Compute Engine predefined role on the production project limits operator access to that project, while a Viewer role on the folder lets auditors inherit read access across all projects in the folder.

questions.question: A media company is building an internal automation tool that needs to list Cloud Storage buckets and read object metadata, but it must not be able to delete buckets or change IAM policies. The platform team wants to follow least privilege and avoid granting broad access that would also affect unrelated services. The same permission set will be used by several service accounts across multiple projects. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant the service accounts the basic Viewer role on each project so they can read all resources needed by the tool.
choices.B: Grant the service accounts roles/storage.admin so they can access buckets and metadata without additional configuration.
choices.C: Bind roles/storage.objectViewer at the organization level and use Cloud Storage bucket labels to restrict access to the intended objects.
choices.D: Create a custom IAM role with only the required Cloud Storage permissions and grant it to the service accounts on the needed projects.

questions.explanation: A custom IAM role is appropriate when the required permissions are narrow and must exclude destructive or administrative actions. Granting that role to the service accounts on the needed projects provides least-privilege access across multiple projects without overexposing other services.

questions.question: A fintech company deploys application changes several times per day to Google Cloud using Terraform and Cloud Build. Security reviews have found repeated misconfigurations because teams can create resources in any region and share them broadly across domains. The company wants to prevent these issues before deployment, while still allowing developers to use CI/CD for fast releases. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Organization Policy Service constraints to centrally restrict allowed regions and domain sharing, and enforce the policy in your landing zone.
choices.B: Use IAM roles and service account impersonation to limit who can apply Terraform changes from Cloud Build.
choices.C: Use Cloud Asset Inventory to detect noncompliant resources after deployment and remediate them with Security Command Center findings.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls to create perimeters around all projects and rely on them to block region and sharing misconfigurations.

questions.explanation: Organization Policy Service is the best preventive control for centrally enforcing configuration guardrails such as allowed regions and domain-based sharing before resources are created.

questions.question: A healthcare team manages GKE clusters and Google Cloud resources with Kubernetes manifests and Terraform. They want to stop workloads from being deployed if images come from unapproved registries or if Kubernetes resources request public IPs. The controls must run automatically in the CI/CD pipeline before any apply step, and the team wants a policy-as-code approach that developers can review in Git. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Security Command Center to scan the GKE cluster after deployment and then patch any violations with Cloud Deploy.
choices.B: Use Binary Authorization alone to validate Terraform resources and reject any cluster configuration that exposes services publicly.
choices.C: Use Policy Controller with Open Policy Agent constraints to validate Terraform and Kubernetes manifests in the CI/CD pipeline before deployment.
choices.D: Use Cloud Armor security policies to block unapproved container images and public IPs during deployment.

questions.explanation: Policy Controller with OPA is designed for policy-as-code enforcement in the pipeline, which helps prevent misconfigurations before deployment.

questions.question: A research organization stores sensitive datasets in BigQuery and Cloud Storage. The team wants to reduce the risk of data exfiltration from compromised identities or accidental sharing, and it must apply to multiple projects that use the same data analytics services. The organization already uses IAM, but leadership wants an additional preventive layer around supported Google Cloud services. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Private Service Connect endpoints for BigQuery and Cloud Storage to keep all access inside the VPC.
choices.B: Use Organization Policy Service to block all API access to BigQuery and Cloud Storage from outside the organization.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor to create rules that restrict BigQuery queries and Cloud Storage object access.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls to create a service perimeter around the supported analytics services and projects.

questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls adds a preventive data exfiltration boundary around supported Google Cloud services and is appropriate when IAM alone is not enough.

questions.question: An enterprise has multiple development, test, and production projects across folders. Security wants access to be granted based on resource attributes so that only approved engineers can administer production databases during business hours, and the policy must scale without attaching unique permissions to every resource. The company also wants to minimize administrative overhead as new projects are created. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Identity groups only and assign the same predefined roles to all engineers across every project.
choices.B: Use IAM Conditions together with Tags to apply attribute-based access control to the production database resources.
choices.C: Use Organization Policy Service to schedule access windows and automatically grant database administrator permissions.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls and Private Google Access to limit who can reach the production databases.

questions.explanation: IAM Conditions with Tags let you express scalable, attribute-based access control for specific resources and contexts without managing per-resource permissions manually.

questions.question: A software company runs containerized workloads on GKE and wants to ensure only trusted images are deployed. Images are built in Cloud Build, signed during the release process, and stored in Artifact Registry. The security team needs a preventive control that blocks unapproved images before they reach production clusters, while still fitting into a CI/CD workflow. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Binary Authorization to require trusted attestations for GKE container images before deployment.
choices.B: Use Cloud Deploy to promote releases only after manual approval from the production operations team.
choices.C: Use Security Command Center to detect vulnerable container images after the GKE deployment completes.
choices.D: Use Artifact Analysis to scan images and let GKE admit any image with no critical findings.

questions.explanation: Binary Authorization is the preventive control for ensuring only trusted, attested container images are admitted to GKE before deployment.

questions.question: A global company is moving employees from multiple legacy directories to Google Cloud. The security team wants a single place to manage corporate identities, enforce multi-factor authentication, and provide single sign-on across Google Cloud and SaaS applications. The company also needs to support a hybrid environment where some users still authenticate through Active Directory during the migration. What should you do?

choices.A: Create Google Cloud service accounts for each employee, grant them IAM roles directly on projects, and use Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy for application sign-in.
choices.B: Store user credentials in Secret Manager, rotate them automatically, and use Workload Identity Federation for employee access to Google Cloud resources.
choices.C: Adopt Cloud Identity or Google Workspace as the central identity platform, federate it with Active Directory, and enforce MFA and SSO policies from the Google admin console.
choices.D: Use Cloud Directory Sync only, keep identities in Active Directory, and assign Google Cloud IAM roles to groups synchronized from the on-premises directory.

questions.explanation: Cloud Identity or Google Workspace is the recommended central place to manage corporate identities, policies, MFA, and SSO. Federation with Active Directory supports the hybrid migration requirement while keeping identity management centralized.

questions.question: A development team is running a microservices application on Google Kubernetes Engine. The services must authenticate to Cloud Storage and Pub/Sub without embedding long-lived credentials in container images or configuration files. The solution must also support separate identities for each workload and follow Google-recommended practices for Kubernetes-based authentication. What should you do?

choices.A: Configure Workload Identity on Google Kubernetes Engine and map each Kubernetes service account to a dedicated Google Cloud service account.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run service accounts for the containers, export the credentials as environment variables, and share them across all namespaces.
choices.C: Store OAuth client secrets in Secret Manager, let each pod retrieve them at startup, and use Cloud Scheduler to rotate them weekly.
choices.D: Create a single Google Cloud service account for the cluster, mount its key file into every pod, and grant it broad project-level IAM roles.

questions.explanation: Workload Identity lets GKE workloads authenticate to Google Cloud using short-lived credentials without service account keys. Mapping Kubernetes service accounts to Google Cloud service accounts provides workload-level isolation and follows recommended practice.

questions.question: A media company runs part of its application on Google Cloud and part on another cloud provider. The operations team wants employees to use their existing corporate identity provider for accessing Google Cloud resources, and it wants to avoid managing separate passwords in Google Cloud. The company also needs centralized control over access policies and must minimize the number of local user accounts. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Identity to create local users for everyone and synchronize passwords from the external identity provider daily.
choices.B: Federate Google Cloud access with the external identity provider by using Cloud Identity or Google Workspace and configure SSO for corporate users.
choices.C: Create individual Google accounts for all employees, then use IAM Conditions to approximate access control across clouds.
choices.D: Grant access with service account keys shared by the identity provider and rotate the keys with Secret Manager.

questions.explanation: Federating with Cloud Identity or Google Workspace enables SSO from an external identity provider while avoiding separate Google Cloud passwords and minimizing local account management.

questions.topic: Migrating Consumer Accounts to Corporate Identities
questions.question: A healthcare provider discovers that many employees are using personal Gmail accounts to access internal applications and some Google Cloud resources. Auditors require that all workforce access use managed corporate identities, and the company wants to preserve access continuity during the transition. The identity team needs a plan that supports staged migration and centralized policy enforcement. What should you do?

choices.A: Keep the Gmail accounts, assign them fewer IAM roles, and rely on Cloud Audit Logs to prove compliance.
choices.B: Use Secret Manager to store the users' existing passwords and connect the applications directly to Cloud Identity later.
choices.C: Create service accounts for the employees, grant them membership in Google Groups, and use those accounts for browser-based sign-in.
choices.D: Migrate users from consumer accounts to Cloud Identity or Google Workspace, then apply centralized authentication and policy controls.

questions.explanation: Managed corporate identities in Cloud Identity or Google Workspace provide centralized control, policy enforcement, and a proper migration path away from consumer accounts.

questions.question: A financial services company has several batch jobs running on Compute Engine and Cloud Run that call third-party APIs and Google Cloud APIs. Security policy prohibits storing long-lived service account keys on disk or in source control, and the company wants to reduce the risk of credential leakage. The solution must still allow the workloads to authenticate reliably across environments. What should you do?

choices.A: Use attached Google Cloud service accounts for the workloads and rely on short-lived authentication tokens instead of keys.
choices.B: Use Secret Manager to hold the service account keys and attach the same secret to every workload at runtime.
choices.C: Use service account keys on Compute Engine metadata server and Cloud Run environment variables to distribute credentials.
choices.D: Create downloadable service account keys, store them in Cloud Storage, and restrict access with IAM policy bindings.

questions.explanation: Attaching service accounts directly to Compute Engine and Cloud Run workloads avoids long-lived keys and uses short-lived credentials, which reduces leakage risk and supports reliable authentication.

questions.question: A financial services company is moving an internal web application to Google Cloud. Employees need secure access from managed laptops and any location, but the app must not be exposed to the public internet. The security team wants access decisions to be based on user identity and device context, and they want to avoid deploying a VPN for every user. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Host the application on Google Kubernetes Engine and expose it through Cloud NAT with firewall rules for employee networks.
choices.B: Deploy the application on Compute Engine and require all users to connect through Cloud VPN before reaching the app.
choices.C: Use Identity-Aware Proxy with Cloud Load Balancing and Chrome Enterprise Premium to enforce context-aware access.
choices.D: Place the application behind Cloud Load Balancing and use Cloud Armor to restrict access to approved IP ranges.

questions.explanation: Identity-Aware Proxy provides zero-trust access based on identity and context, and Chrome Enterprise Premium adds device-based access controls. Together with Cloud Load Balancing, this enables secure access without exposing the application publicly or requiring a VPN for every user.

questions.question: A platform team runs a containerized application in Google Kubernetes Engine and wants to prevent unapproved images from reaching production. They need to scan images during the build process, store trusted artifacts centrally, and block deployments unless images are signed by the release pipeline. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy to release containers directly to GKE, and store images in Cloud Storage to simplify approvals.
choices.B: Use GKE Autopilot with VPC Service Controls to ensure that only approved pods can be scheduled.
choices.C: Use Artifact Registry for image storage, Cloud Build for CI, and Binary Authorization to enforce signed-image deployment policies.
choices.D: Use Secret Manager to store container images and Cloud Run Jobs to validate them before production deployment.

questions.explanation: Artifact Registry stores the trusted container images, Cloud Build integrates security checks into the build pipeline, and Binary Authorization blocks deployment unless images meet the signing policy. This is a classic shift-left security approach.

questions.question: A healthcare organization must run workloads that handle regulated patient data and demonstrate strong controls for data residency, restricted service access, and audit readiness. The organization wants Google Cloud to provide a framework that simplifies compliance scoping while limiting which Google Cloud services can be used. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Organization Policy Service alone to deny external IP addresses and let each project team choose its own data controls.
choices.B: Use Cloud KMS with customer-managed encryption keys and place all workloads behind Cloud Armor policies.
choices.C: Use Security Command Center to centralize findings and rely on Shared VPC to isolate the regulated workloads.
choices.D: Use Assured Workloads to create compliant folders and apply VPC Service Controls around the regulated data.

questions.explanation: Assured Workloads is designed to help organizations meet regulatory and compliance requirements by providing compliant environments and controls, while VPC Service Controls reduces data exfiltration risk by creating service perimeters around sensitive resources.

questions.question: A media company has several development teams creating projects in Google Cloud. The security group wants to enforce organization-wide controls so that new resources cannot be created in unsupported regions, public IP addresses are blocked by default, and users cannot disable key security settings. The controls must apply consistently to all current and future projects. How should you configure this?

choices.A: Use Cloud Asset Inventory to track violations and manually update IAM roles in each project.
choices.B: Use Cloud Armor security policies to block unsupported regions and prevent public IP assignment to VM instances.
choices.C: Use VPC Service Controls to define project-level constraints for regions, IP addresses, and security settings.
choices.D: Use Organization Policy Service at the organization and folder levels to enforce allowed locations, restrict external IPs, and constrain resource settings.

questions.explanation: Organization Policy Service is the correct mechanism for centrally enforcing resource-creation constraints across an organization and its folders or projects. It can restrict regions, external IP usage, and other configurable security constraints consistently.

questions.question: A retail company is building an internal generative AI assistant on Google Cloud. The assistant will use sensitive company documents, and the compliance team requires that access to the model environment be tightly controlled, data exfiltration be minimized, and encryption keys remain under company control. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Vertex AI with customer-managed encryption keys from Cloud KMS and place the data sources inside VPC Service Controls.
choices.B: Use Dataflow to preprocess documents and grant the assistant direct access through Cloud NAT and firewall rules.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI with Gemini and protect the data with Cloud SQL IAM database authentication.
choices.D: Use BigQuery Omni for the document store and secure the assistant with Cloud Armor and load balancer authentication.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI is the right platform for building the AI assistant, Cloud KMS CMEK keeps encryption keys under company control, and VPC Service Controls helps reduce exfiltration risk around sensitive data sources and model workflows.

questions.question: A retail company is moving its application platform to Google Cloud and wants to catch security misconfigurations before they reach production. The platform team uses Terraform to provision resources, and the security team wants centralized guardrails that developers cannot bypass in merge requests. The solution must enforce policy checks in the CI/CD pipeline, prevent noncompliant infrastructure from being deployed, and scale across multiple teams. What should you do?

choices.A: Use VPC Service Controls to block noncompliant infrastructure resources from being created by developers.
choices.B: Use IAM Conditions and principal access boundary policies to control who can deploy resources from the CI/CD system.
choices.C: Use Policy Controller with Open Policy Agent to validate Terraform or Kubernetes manifests in the CI/CD pipeline before deployment.
choices.D: Use Organization Policy Service constraints only and require the platform team to review all Terraform plans before deployment.

questions.explanation: Policy Controller with OPA provides policy-as-code checks in the pipeline, which is the shift-left approach for catching configuration issues before deployment.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is standardizing its Google Cloud landing zone for new projects. The security team must ensure that teams can only create resources in approved regions, that external domain sharing is limited, and that service account use is tightly controlled. These restrictions need to be enforced centrally so that every project inherits them by default. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use IAM roles at the project level and Cloud Audit Logs to detect when teams violate the approved configuration.
choices.B: Use VPC Service Controls perimeters to restrict which regions resources can be created in across the organization.
choices.C: Use Policy Controller constraints in each application repository to validate the project settings during code review.
choices.D: Use Organization Policy Service constraints at the organization and folder levels to centrally enforce the required restrictions.

questions.explanation: Organization Policy Service is designed for centrally enforced guardrails such as location, domain sharing, and service account restrictions across resource hierarchies.

questions.question: A media company stores sensitive customer data in Cloud Storage and BigQuery. The security team wants to reduce the risk of data exfiltration from managed Google Cloud services, even if an account is compromised. The solution must create a service perimeter around the data services and be compatible with the company’s existing identity and access controls. What should you do?

choices.A: Use VPC Service Controls to create perimeters around Cloud Storage and BigQuery and restrict access to trusted identities and networks.
choices.B: Use Cloud Armor policies to block requests to Cloud Storage and BigQuery from public IP addresses.
choices.C: Use Private Service Connect endpoints for Cloud Storage and BigQuery to keep all access inside the VPC.
choices.D: Use Secret Manager and Cloud KMS to encrypt the data and prevent unauthorized API access.

questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls is specifically intended to reduce data exfiltration risk by creating perimeters around supported Google Cloud services.

questions.topic: Secure access with IAM and PAB
questions.question: A software company has a centralized security team that performs emergency production fixes in Google Cloud. The company wants to ensure that those engineers can only access resources in approved projects, even if they temporarily receive elevated permissions. Access should be time-bound and limited to the organization’s defined resource boundaries. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Grant broad project-level IAM roles and rely on Cloud Logging alerts when engineers access sensitive resources.
choices.B: Use IAM Conditions to require MFA and IP-based restrictions for all production access.
choices.C: Use Privileged Access Manager together with principal access boundary policies to grant just-in-time access within approved boundaries.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls to ensure engineers can only access the approved production projects.

questions.explanation: Privileged Access Manager supports just-in-time elevation, and principal access boundary policies constrain what resources those elevated principals can access.

questions.question: A fintech company builds containerized microservices on Google Cloud and wants to stop vulnerable application images from being deployed. The security team requires automated scanning before release, and the DevOps team wants the results to appear in the existing CI/CD pipeline. The solution must focus on known vulnerabilities in application artifacts rather than network perimeter controls. How should you configure this?

choices.A: Use Organization Policy Service to block the creation of all container images that contain known CVEs.
choices.B: Use VPC Service Controls around Artifact Registry to prevent the upload of vulnerable container images.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor security policies and load balancer logging to block images with known vulnerabilities.
choices.D: Enable Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning and make Cloud Build fail the pipeline when critical findings are present.

questions.explanation: Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning with Cloud Build enforcement is a shift-left approach that prevents vulnerable artifacts from being promoted through the pipeline.

questions.question: A healthcare company is moving an internal patient-support portal to Google Cloud. The portal must be accessible only to employees using the corporate identity provider, require MFA, and avoid exposing the application directly to the internet. The team also wants to keep access checks at the application layer without building a custom authentication system. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Publish the portal through Apigee API Management and require OAuth tokens for all browser sessions.
choices.B: Place the portal behind Cloud Load Balancing and use Cloud Armor to block all external traffic except approved IP ranges.
choices.C: Deploy the portal on Compute Engine and enforce access with VPC firewall rules and Cloud VPN for remote users.
choices.D: Place the portal behind an external HTTP(S) Load Balancer and use Identity-Aware Proxy with Cloud Identity or a federated identity provider.

questions.explanation: IAP provides zero-trust access control at the application layer, integrates with Google identity or federation, and avoids exposing the application directly to users without requiring a custom auth solution.

questions.question: A financial services company needs to store regulated customer data in Google Cloud. The workload must meet strict data residency requirements, use approved Google Cloud services only, and provide guardrails for policy compliance across the project. The compliance team wants a managed way to enforce these restrictions during deployment and operation. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Enable VPC Service Controls around the project and route all access through a Private Service Connect endpoint.
choices.B: Create a dedicated folder and use Organization Policy Service constraints with Cloud KMS key rings in each project.
choices.C: Use Security Command Center to continuously scan resources and alert when noncompliant services are detected.
choices.D: Deploy the workload in an Assured Workloads folder and apply Organization Policy Service controls to restrict supported services and locations.

questions.explanation: Assured Workloads is designed for regulatory and residency requirements by providing a governed environment with compliance controls, and Organization Policy Service helps enforce the required restrictions.

questions.question: A media company stores proprietary content metadata in BigQuery and Cloud Storage. Security requires that data exfiltration risks be reduced even if credentials are stolen, and only trusted internal networks and approved Google Cloud services should access the data. The team wants a boundary-based control that protects managed services without rewriting applications. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Access Context Manager with Identity-Aware Proxy to gate access to BigQuery and Cloud Storage.
choices.B: Create a VPC Service Controls perimeter around the projects and configure access levels for approved identities and networks.
choices.C: Place the projects in a Shared VPC and restrict all egress with hierarchical firewall policies.
choices.D: Use Cloud Armor policies on the public endpoints and require all users to connect through Cloud VPN.

questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls creates a service perimeter around supported Google Cloud services and helps reduce data exfiltration risk by restricting access to approved identities, networks, and service paths.

questions.question: A software company runs a CI/CD pipeline for containerized applications on Google Cloud. They want to prevent untrusted images from being deployed to GKE, verify that images are built only from approved sources, and catch security issues before release. The platform team needs a managed approach that integrates into the build and deploy workflow. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy to promote releases and rely on Container Analysis to scan images after production rollout.
choices.B: Use Compute Engine instance templates and OS Config to validate container images at startup.
choices.C: Use Cloud Build to build the images, store them in Artifact Registry, and enforce Binary Authorization policies on GKE deployments.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Armor with Artifact Registry to block any container image that is not signed by a developer.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, and Binary Authorization support shift-left security by building, storing, and policy-checking container images before they are deployed to GKE.

questions.question: A global retail company wants to prevent engineers from accidentally creating resources in unsupported regions, using public IP addresses on certain services, or allowing service account key creation. The security team needs a centralized control mechanism that applies consistently across folders and projects. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Configure Organization Policy Service constraints at the folder level to restrict locations, external IP usage, and service account key creation.
choices.B: Apply VPC firewall rules and Cloud NAT to keep all resources private and regionally constrained.
choices.C: Use Cloud Asset Inventory reports and Cloud Logging alerts to notify administrators after resources are created.
choices.D: Use Security Command Center to detect unsupported regions and quarantine the affected projects.

questions.explanation: Organization Policy Service provides centrally managed guardrails that can be enforced across folders and projects to restrict allowed regions, public IP use, and other risky configurations.

questions.question: A healthcare company is moving an internal web application to Google Cloud. The app must be accessible only to employees, must not expose a public login page, and must require per-user authentication before any request reaches the backend. The security team wants access decisions based on user identity and context rather than the network location. They also want to minimize changes to the application and avoid deploying a separate VPN for remote users. What should you do?

choices.A: Place the application behind a public HTTPS load balancer and protect it with Cloud Armor and reCAPTCHA Enterprise policies.
choices.B: Front the application with External Application Load Balancing and store user credentials in Secret Manager for backend authentication.
choices.C: Publish the application through Identity-Aware Proxy and use Cloud Load Balancing with Cloud IAM to grant access only to authorized users.
choices.D: Deploy the application on GKE and require access through Cloud VPN plus VPC firewall rules tied to corporate IP ranges.

questions.explanation: Identity-Aware Proxy enforces user-level authentication and authorization before a request reaches the app, which aligns with zero trust and avoids relying on network location or a VPN.

questions.question: A financial services company stores sensitive customer files in Cloud Storage and processes them with BigQuery. The security team must reduce the risk of data exfiltration from compromised credentials, limit access to approved Google Cloud services, and keep analysts productive from corporate and remote locations. They want a control that protects data even if IAM permissions are misused. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Armor policies on the public endpoints for Cloud Storage and BigQuery, and require all users to connect through Cloud VPN.
choices.B: Store the files in Cloud SQL, enable Cloud KMS encryption keys, and access the data through Private Service Connect.
choices.C: Create VPC Service Controls perimeters around Cloud Storage and BigQuery, and grant users access with Cloud IAM roles inside the perimeter.
choices.D: Place the data in Filestore and protect it with organization policies and firewall rules applied to the VPC network.

questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls helps prevent data exfiltration by creating service perimeters around supported Google Cloud services such as Cloud Storage and BigQuery, adding an important layer beyond IAM.

questions.question: A media company runs workloads in multiple VPC networks and wants private access to Google APIs and managed services without sending traffic over the public internet. The company must keep service traffic within Google’s network, simplify egress controls, and reduce the need for broad public IP exposure. The platform team also wants a design that supports centralized networking across projects. What should you do?

choices.A: Connect the VPCs with Cloud VPN and route Google API traffic through a shared external HTTPS load balancer.
choices.B: Use Cloud NAT for outbound traffic and allow Google API access through public endpoints secured with Cloud Armor.
choices.C: Configure Private Service Connect for access to Google APIs and use Shared VPC to centralize network management across projects.
choices.D: Deploy a Shared VPC and use Cloud Router with Dynamic Routing to advertise Google API routes to all projects.

questions.explanation: Private Service Connect provides private access to Google APIs and services, and Shared VPC supports centralized network administration across projects, which fits the requirements.

questions.question: A software company is replacing perimeter-based access for its admin tools with a zero-trust model. Administrators work from managed devices and need access only when the device posture and user identity are validated. The company wants to reduce reliance on source IP addresses and enforce least privilege across internal tools hosted on Google Cloud. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Place the admin tools behind an external application load balancer and protect them with Cloud Armor geo-based rules.
choices.B: Expose the tools through Private Service Connect and rely on Cloud IDS to validate each administrator session.
choices.C: Use Identity-Aware Proxy with Chrome Enterprise Premium device trust signals and Cloud IAM for least-privilege access.
choices.D: Use Cloud VPN for all administrators and restrict access with VPC firewall rules based on office IP ranges.

questions.explanation: A zero-trust approach should verify each access attempt using identity and device context. IAP and Chrome Enterprise Premium support context-aware access and least privilege better than IP-based controls.

questions.question: A multinational retailer is creating a new Google Cloud landing zone for multiple application teams. The security team wants to avoid accidental exposure from default networks, keep network segmentation manageable, and enforce consistent firewall controls at multiple levels. They also want to reduce the chance that teams create inconsistent network layouts across projects. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud DNS policies to isolate the projects and rely on Cloud NAT for all east-west traffic between workloads.
choices.B: Keep the default network in each project but restrict it with hierarchical firewall policies and Cloud Armor.
choices.C: Create a separate VPC network in every project and let each team manage firewall rules independently with VPC firewall rules.
choices.D: Delete default networks, disable default network creation, and use Shared VPC with hierarchical firewall policies for centralized segmentation.

questions.explanation: Deleting default networks and disabling default network creation reduces accidental exposure, while Shared VPC and hierarchical firewall policies provide centralized segmentation and consistent control.

questions.question: A global manufacturing company uses Microsoft Active Directory for employee identities and is moving several internal applications to Google Cloud. The security team needs a single place to manage users and groups, enforce multi-factor authentication, and provide single sign-on for Google Cloud and other corporate applications. The company also wants to keep its existing on-premises directory as the source of truth during the transition. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a separate Cloud Identity directory for each business unit and use Google Cloud IAM roles directly on individual users in every project.
choices.B: Use Cloud Identity or Google Workspace as the central identity system and integrate it with Active Directory for federated single sign-on and policy enforcement.
choices.C: Move users to Cloud Storage buckets that contain application credentials and use VPC Service Controls to authenticate employee access.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud service accounts for all employees and grant access to Google Cloud resources through instance metadata and API keys.

questions.explanation: Cloud Identity or Google Workspace provides centralized corporate identity management, and federation with Active Directory supports single sign-on while keeping the on-premises directory as the source of truth.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is deploying a data processing pipeline on Google Cloud that calls third-party APIs and accesses BigQuery datasets. The platform team wants to avoid embedding long-lived credentials in code, simplify workload authentication across environments, and reduce the risk of credential leakage. The solution must work for both Compute Engine VMs and Google Kubernetes Engine workloads. What should you do?

choices.A: Create Google Cloud service accounts for the workloads and attach them to Compute Engine instances and Kubernetes service accounts for GKE pods.
choices.B: Use Google Cloud IAM users for each application container and rotate their passwords through Cloud Deploy releases.
choices.C: Store third-party API keys in Secret Manager and mount them on all VMs using Cloud Storage buckets and startup scripts.
choices.D: Place the workloads behind Cloud Armor and use Cloud VPN to transmit static credentials between environments.

questions.explanation: Google Cloud service accounts and Kubernetes service accounts are the correct identities for applications and workloads, and they avoid hard-coded credentials while supporting Compute Engine and GKE.

questions.question: A software company has employees, contractors, and partners who must access a mix of Google Cloud projects and internal web applications. The company wants to use its existing external identity provider for authentication, apply consistent access policies, and avoid creating separate local accounts in Google Cloud for every user. The security team also wants to support a hybrid environment without changing the login experience for users. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud DNS policies and VPC firewall rules to authenticate users based on source IP addresses.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run to proxy authentication requests and issue custom OAuth tokens for all applications.
choices.C: Configure Cloud Identity federation with the external identity provider and manage access to Google Cloud with IAM groups and roles.
choices.D: Create individual Google Cloud service accounts for each user and grant them project-level IAM roles.

questions.explanation: Federating the external identity provider into Cloud Identity enables hybrid authentication while IAM groups and roles provide centralized access management across Google Cloud and internal applications.

questions.question: A retailer is building a customer-facing loyalty application on Google Cloud. The application must support millions of external users, keep customer identities separate from employee accounts, and allow secure sign-in without giving customers access to Google Cloud resources. The development team also wants to minimize the amount of identity infrastructure it must manage. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Workspace identities for customers and place them into Google Groups that map to application permissions.
choices.B: Use Compute Engine service accounts for customer authentication and store customer profiles in Cloud SQL user tables.
choices.C: Use an external customer identity solution integrated with the application and keep Google Cloud IAM limited to service accounts and administrators.
choices.D: Use Cloud Identity to create accounts for every customer and grant them access to the application through Google Cloud IAM.

questions.explanation: Customer identities should be handled by a customer identity solution, while Google Cloud IAM should remain focused on workload and administrator access rather than end-customer access.

questions.question: A financial services company is modernizing access management after several contractors kept access to internal tools longer than intended. The company needs a centralized process to provision users and groups, enforce policy changes quickly, and deprovision access immediately when workers leave. It also wants to reduce the use of consumer Gmail accounts for corporate resources. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Adopt Cloud Identity or Google Workspace for centralized identity lifecycle management and migrate users from consumer accounts into managed accounts.
choices.B: Use Cloud IAM predefined roles for every user and update permissions only at the project level when onboarding changes.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor security policies to block former contractors from accessing corporate applications by IP address.
choices.D: Use Artifact Registry as the identity source and store user records in container images for automated access control.

questions.explanation: Cloud Identity or Google Workspace supports centralized provisioning, group management, policy enforcement, and deprovisioning, and it helps migrate users away from consumer accounts.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare stores regulated provider data in Cloud Storage and BigQuery, and security leaders want to reduce the risk of data exfiltration from those managed services. The workloads are accessed by developers, analysts, and automated pipelines from multiple projects, and the company must maintain regulatory compliance while still allowing internal data sharing. They also want a centralized control that limits access from untrusted networks and helps contain the blast radius if credentials are compromised. What should you do?

choices.A: Create Cloud Armor security policies for the external applications and use Cloud CDN to restrict access to Cloud Storage and BigQuery through public endpoints.
choices.B: Enable CMEK on Cloud Storage and BigQuery, and use Cloud Key Management Service to require customer-managed encryption for all data access.
choices.C: Create a VPC Service Controls perimeter around the projects that contain Cloud Storage and BigQuery, and use Access Context Manager to define the allowed access levels.
choices.D: Use IAM Conditions with organization policies to allow only approved service accounts to access Cloud Storage and BigQuery from selected projects.

questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls helps protect managed services like Cloud Storage and BigQuery from data exfiltration by creating a service perimeter. Access Context Manager adds context-aware access rules for users and service accounts. This best matches EHR Healthcare's compliance and blast-radius requirements.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare uses Microsoft Active Directory for employee identities and wants to reduce the operational burden of managing separate cloud accounts. The security team needs centralized authentication for Google Cloud, least-privilege access for administrators, and the ability to quickly revoke access when an employee leaves. The company also wants to preserve compliance controls by avoiding long-lived credentials wherever possible. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Create individual Google Accounts for each employee, then use Cloud Identity to sync passwords from Microsoft Active Directory into Google Cloud.
choices.B: Deploy Identity-Aware Proxy in front of internal applications, and use it as the primary mechanism for all Google Cloud administrator authentication.
choices.C: Configure Cloud Identity Federation with Workforce Identity Federation for Microsoft Active Directory, and grant Google Cloud IAM roles to federated groups and service accounts.
choices.D: Use Secret Manager to store employee credentials, and configure applications to retrieve those credentials at runtime for Google Cloud access.

questions.explanation: Workforce Identity Federation lets EHR Healthcare use its existing Microsoft Active Directory identities without creating or managing separate Google passwords. IAM bindings on federated identities support least privilege and faster deprovisioning. This is the most secure and operationally efficient option.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare must keep legacy insurance integrations on-premises for several years while moving customer-facing systems to Google Cloud. The architecture team needs a secure, high-performance private connection between the colocation environment and Google Cloud, and they want predictable latency for regulated data exchange. The solution must also support connectivity to multiple Google Cloud VPC networks as the company grows. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Partner Interconnect with Cloud Router to connect the colocation environment to Google Cloud over private connectivity.
choices.B: Use Shared VPC and Cloud NAT to route all traffic from on-premises systems into Google Cloud over public IP addresses.
choices.C: Use Cloud VPN with dynamic routing and expose the on-premises APIs through an external HTTP(S) load balancer.
choices.D: Use Private Service Connect to publish the on-premises insurance APIs directly into Google Cloud services.

questions.explanation: Partner Interconnect provides private, higher-bandwidth connectivity that is better suited to EHR Healthcare's performance and compliance needs than VPN over the public internet. Cloud Router enables dynamic routing and scale across VPCs. This is the best fit for secure hybrid connectivity.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare is migrating several databases containing sensitive healthcare data into Google Cloud, and auditors require tight control over encryption keys. The security team wants to control key rotation, separate key administration from data administration, and retain the ability to revoke access to encrypted data if needed. They also want to use managed services such as Cloud SQL and Cloud Storage without building custom encryption tooling. What should you do?

choices.A: Encrypt data only in the application layer and disable Google-managed encryption for all storage and database services.
choices.B: Use Customer-Managed Encryption Keys in Cloud KMS for Cloud SQL and Cloud Storage, and configure key rotation and IAM separation of duties.
choices.C: Use Cloud HSM for all database records and store object metadata in Firestore to avoid encryption key management.
choices.D: Store application secrets in Secret Manager and use them as the primary encryption mechanism for Cloud SQL and Cloud Storage.

questions.explanation: Customer-managed encryption keys with Cloud KMS provide EHR Healthcare with control over key lifecycle, rotation, and access separation while still using managed Google Cloud services. This aligns with common healthcare compliance requirements and avoids custom encryption tooling. Cloud HSM can be added for higher assurance, but it is not required for the stated need.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants a centralized way to identify misconfigurations, exposed assets, and likely attack paths across its Google Cloud projects. The security team needs continuous visibility into IAM risks, network exposure, and vulnerable workloads so they can prioritize remediation before an incident occurs. They also want findings integrated into their existing operations workflow. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Enable Security Command Center and use Event Threat Detection and Security Health Analytics to collect findings across projects.
choices.B: Use Cloud Armor adaptive protection to detect all internal misconfigurations and IAM policy issues across the organization.
choices.C: Deploy Cloud Logging sinks to BigQuery and manually review log tables for security findings every day.
choices.D: Configure Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning and assume it will provide organization-wide security posture management.

questions.explanation: Security Command Center is the centralized security posture management and threat detection product for Google Cloud. Event Threat Detection and Security Health Analytics help surface risky IAM, network, and workload issues across projects. This best satisfies the need for proactive, centralized security visibility.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is moving an internal patient portal to Google Cloud. The portal must be accessible only to employees on managed devices, users must authenticate with their corporate identity, and the security team wants to avoid exposing the application directly to the internet. The solution must also support access policies based on user identity and device posture. What should you do?

choices.A: Store user identities in Cloud Identity and restrict access by using VPC Service Controls around the application project.
choices.B: Place the application behind Cloud Load Balancing and secure it with Cloud Armor web application firewall rules.
choices.C: Protect the application with Identity-Aware Proxy and enforce access using Chrome Enterprise Premium device-based access policies.
choices.D: Deploy the application in a private GKE cluster and use Cloud VPN to require users to connect through the corporate network.

questions.explanation: Identity-Aware Proxy provides zero-trust, identity-based access to web applications, and Chrome Enterprise Premium can enforce device posture and managed-device requirements. This matches the requirement to authenticate corporate users without exposing the app directly to the internet.

questions.question: A financial services company is building a new analytics platform on Google Cloud. The workload must satisfy a strict regulatory framework, use only approved Google Cloud services, and keep resource locations constrained to a specific geography. The compliance team also wants policy controls that prevent developers from creating noncompliant resources. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud KMS to encrypt all data and rely on Cloud Audit Logs to prove regional compliance after deployment.
choices.B: Use Organization Policy Service constraints and deploy the workload with Assured Workloads in the required region.
choices.C: Use VPC Service Controls to restrict API access and deploy the workload in a Shared VPC to centralize administration.
choices.D: Use Security Command Center to continuously scan the project and block noncompliant resources before they are created.

questions.explanation: Assured Workloads is designed for regulatory and residency requirements, and Organization Policy Service can enforce preventive controls such as allowed regions and service restrictions. Together they directly address compliance, geography, and developer guardrails.

questions.question: A software company deploys containerized services to GKE several times per day. Security engineers want to ensure that only approved images built from the central pipeline can be deployed, and they want policy checks to happen before new images reach production. The team uses Artifact Registry and Cloud Build today, and they want a Google Cloud native approach that fits the existing workflow. What should you do?

choices.A: Use GKE Autopilot with Workload Identity to isolate workloads and prevent unsigned images from being pulled.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to sign images and enforce deployment approvals with Binary Authorization against Artifact Registry.
choices.C: Use Cloud Deploy to promote releases between environments and store image tags in Artifact Registry.
choices.D: Use Security Command Center and Container Analysis to detect vulnerable images after they are deployed to GKE.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build supports automated build and signing workflows, Artifact Registry stores the images, and Binary Authorization enforces that only approved, signed images can be deployed. This is a classic shift-left control for the deployment pipeline.

questions.topic: Perimeter Controls and Data Exfiltration Prevention
questions.question: A research organization stores sensitive datasets in Cloud Storage and BigQuery. The data must be accessible to internal analytics applications, but the security team wants to reduce the risk of data exfiltration from those services and prevent access from outside trusted networks and identities. They need a Google Cloud control that creates a service perimeter around the data services. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Private Service Connect for all consumers and enable Cloud DNS private zones for the data projects.
choices.B: Create a Shared VPC and route all traffic through Cloud VPN to the analytics applications.
choices.C: Configure VPC Service Controls around the Cloud Storage and BigQuery projects and allow access only from approved identities and networks.
choices.D: Use Cloud Armor policies in front of BigQuery and Cloud Storage to block requests from untrusted IP addresses.

questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls creates a service perimeter around supported Google Cloud services such as Cloud Storage and BigQuery, helping reduce data exfiltration risk. It is the right control when the goal is to restrict access to sensitive managed services by perimeter and policy.

questions.question: An enterprise is exposing a legacy internal web application to remote employees during a migration to Google Cloud. The app cannot be rewritten, but access must be granted only after verifying user identity, group membership, and the request context. The security team wants a solution that avoids using a traditional VPN for application access. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move the application behind a private GKE Ingress and require users to connect through Cloud VPN.
choices.B: Use Identity-Aware Proxy in front of the application and manage identities and groups with Cloud Identity.
choices.C: Publish the application through External HTTP(S) Load Balancing and protect it with Cloud Armor address-based rules.
choices.D: Place the application in a separate project and use Organization Policy Service to restrict user access by domain.

questions.explanation: Identity-Aware Proxy enables zero-trust access to legacy web applications based on identity and context, and Cloud Identity provides the user and group information needed for authorization. This avoids the need for a VPN while meeting the access requirements.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare is moving its customer-facing web applications to Google Cloud, and most of the applications are containerized. Traffic spikes sharply during business hours, and the company wants to reduce overprovisioning while still meeting a 99.9% availability target. The platform team also wants a consistent way to manage multiple container-based environments and to dynamically scale new environments as provider demand grows. What should you do?

choices.A: Host the applications on App Engine standard environment and use manual traffic splitting between versions to handle demand changes.
choices.B: Deploy the applications on Google Kubernetes Engine clusters with cluster autoscaler and Horizontal Pod Autoscaler configured for each environment.
choices.C: Deploy the applications on Compute Engine managed instance groups with load balancers and use custom scripts to resize instances based on CPU thresholds.
choices.D: Run the applications on Cloud Run services with request-based autoscaling and separate services for development, staging, and production.

questions.explanation: GKE with cluster autoscaler and HPA is the best fit for EHR Healthcare's containerized workloads because it provides a consistent management model for multiple environments and dynamically scales both pods and nodes to match demand.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare has several nonproduction Kubernetes environments that are used intermittently for testing new provider integrations. The team wants to reduce infrastructure administration costs while keeping the environments available quickly when developers need them. These workloads do not need the same reliability guarantees as production, and usage is often bursty and short lived. What should you do?

choices.A: Place the nonproduction workloads on Cloud Run jobs and store all artifacts in Filestore for persistent access.
choices.B: Run the testing workloads on Compute Engine sole-tenant nodes to isolate them from production workloads.
choices.C: Use Google Kubernetes Engine Autopilot for the nonproduction environments and enable node auto-provisioning for burst capacity.
choices.D: Migrate the testing workloads to Cloud SQL and schedule batch jobs with Cloud Scheduler to simulate provider traffic.

questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot is appropriate for intermittent nonproduction workloads because it reduces node management overhead and aligns resource usage with demand, helping EHR Healthcare control costs without sacrificing container compatibility.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare currently uses several open source monitoring tools, and alerts are sent by email but are frequently ignored. The operations team wants centralized visibility into application health, infrastructure usage, and latency across all customer-facing services. They also want actionable alerts that integrate with their incident response process. What should you do?

choices.A: Send application traces to Cloud Trace only and rely on manual review of trace sampling during incidents.
choices.B: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards with Cloud Logging metrics, alerting policies, and notification channels integrated with PagerDuty or Slack.
choices.C: Export logs from the clusters to Cloud Storage and review them weekly using BigQuery scheduled queries.
choices.D: Install third-party agents on each VM and use Cloud DNS logs to infer service availability trends.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging provide centralized observability, while alerting policies and notification channels create actionable, timely alerts that are better suited to EHR Healthcare's operational needs.

questions.topic: Database selection for global scale and trend analysis
questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants to generate reports and predictions on healthcare trends using provider data from multiple regions. The company expects rapidly growing data volumes and needs a database that supports high availability, strong consistency, and predictable performance for mission-critical analytics input. The solution should also reduce operational overhead compared with self-managed databases. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Memorystore for Redis as the primary data store and stream snapshots into BigQuery for model training.
choices.B: Use Cloud SQL for MySQL with read replicas in each region and export data to BigQuery for all reporting.
choices.C: Use Cloud Spanner as the operational database and replicate curated analytics data into BigQuery for reporting.
choices.D: Use Firestore in native mode and connect it directly to Looker Studio for trend dashboards.

questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is the best choice for globally distributed, mission-critical data that requires strong consistency and high availability, while BigQuery is appropriate for large-scale reporting and analytics.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare is expanding to new insurance providers in multiple countries, and the business wants to reduce latency for customer-facing applications while still supporting a 99.9% availability objective. The team also needs a cloud design that can scale with growth and avoid unnecessary spending in regions that do not serve major traffic. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy all services in a single us-central1 region and use Cloud DNS latency routing only for database failover.
choices.B: Use Cloud CDN with Cloud Storage for all application content and move the application logic into BigQuery scheduled queries.
choices.C: Use Cloud VPN between on-premises sites and a single GKE cluster in one region to simplify operations.
choices.D: Deploy the application to multiple Google Cloud regions behind global external HTTP(S) Load Balancing and place services close to users.

questions.explanation: Multi-region deployment behind global external HTTP(S) Load Balancing helps EHR Healthcare reduce latency for global users while supporting high availability and scaling the platform as demand grows.

questions.question: A retail company runs a PostgreSQL-based order processing application on Compute Engine VMs in multiple zones. The platform team spends significant time patching, backing up, and scaling the database, and leadership wants to reduce total cost of ownership without changing the application’s core database engine. The workload must remain highly available, support automated backups, and minimize operational overhead so engineers can focus on new features. What should you do?

choices.A: Move the database to BigQuery and use scheduled queries to approximate transactional processing.
choices.B: Run PostgreSQL on Compute Engine with Managed Instance Groups and Cloud Storage snapshots for backup automation.
choices.C: Migrate the database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL and use automatic backups, read replicas, and maintenance windows.
choices.D: Deploy PostgreSQL on Google Kubernetes Engine Standard with persistent disks and manage replication yourself.

questions.explanation: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL is the managed service that reduces patching, backup, and scaling overhead while preserving the PostgreSQL engine and improving business value through lower TCO.

questions.question: A media startup has a containerized API that experiences unpredictable traffic spikes during product launches. The team wants to reduce idle infrastructure costs, avoid managing cluster upgrades, and keep deployment velocity high. The service only needs to handle HTTP requests and can tolerate brief startup latency during scaling events. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Package the API as a Dataproc Serverless job and trigger it with Cloud Scheduler.
choices.B: Deploy the container to Cloud Run and use Cloud Build for image delivery and automated revisions.
choices.C: Run the API on Compute Engine behind an Internal TCP/UDP Load Balancer with autoscaled managed instance groups.
choices.D: Deploy the API to GKE Standard with Cluster Autoscaler and preemptible nodes.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run is the best fit for a stateless HTTP containerized service with variable traffic because it eliminates cluster management and scales to zero when idle.

questions.question: A finance team runs nightly Spark jobs to transform 20 TB of parquet files stored in Cloud Storage. The current environment uses self-managed Hadoop clusters on Compute Engine, and the team wants to reduce administration effort while keeping the same Spark-based processing model. The jobs run for only a few hours each night, and leadership wants per-second billing to reduce waste during idle periods. What should you do?

choices.A: Run the Spark jobs on GKE Autopilot with persistent volumes and custom init containers.
choices.B: Migrate the jobs to Dataproc Serverless for Spark and store the data in Cloud Storage.
choices.C: Keep the Hadoop clusters on Compute Engine and use committed use discounts for the VMs.
choices.D: Move the jobs to BigQuery and rewrite all transformations as materialized views.

questions.explanation: Dataproc Serverless is optimized for Spark processing with minimal cluster management and pay-for-use billing, which aligns with the batch workload and cost goals.

questions.question: An enterprise software team has a microservices platform running on GKE Standard. The team’s release process is slow, changes often fail in production, and outages cause the company to overprovision capacity as a safety buffer. Management wants a way to improve business agility while lowering indirect costs from downtime and incident response. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Replace the services with BigQuery scheduled queries and use Looker Studio for operational reporting.
choices.B: Adopt DORA metrics in Cloud Monitoring and use Cloud Build with progressive delivery on GKE.
choices.C: Move the services to Compute Engine and deploy blue-green releases with separate managed instance groups only.
choices.D: Migrate the platform to Cloud SQL and use database maintenance windows to reduce release complexity.

questions.explanation: DORA metrics help identify delivery bottlenecks, and Cloud Build plus progressive delivery on GKE can improve release performance, reduce failures, and lower the business cost of downtime.

questions.question: A payments company runs a customer-facing application on Compute Engine and experiences recurring incident escalations that increase support costs and lost revenue. The leadership team wants a strategy that improves reliability, reduces mean time to recovery, and avoids adding more overprovisioned capacity just to protect against failures. The solution must emphasize automation, observability, and incident response practices. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Deploy the application on GKE Autopilot and disable autoscaling to keep performance predictable.
choices.B: Shift the application to Dataproc Serverless and use batch jobs for request handling.
choices.C: Adopt Site Reliability Engineering practices with Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Error Reporting.
choices.D: Move the application to Cloud SQL and use read replicas for all customer requests.

questions.explanation: SRE practices supported by Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Error Reporting improve observability and incident response, which reduces downtime and indirect business costs.

questions.question: A retail company runs a customer-facing application on Google Kubernetes Engine and recently launched a new checkout flow. The team must continuously watch latency, error rates, and resource utilization after each release, and they want alerts before customers notice a problem. They also need a way to compare performance trends over time so they can decide whether to tune the application or scale the cluster. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Logging to store application logs and Cloud Run revisions to split traffic between versions while relying on manual review of errors.
choices.B: Use Network Intelligence Center topology views and Cloud Armor security policies to identify and block requests that are causing slow responses.
choices.C: Use Google Cloud Observability with Cloud Monitoring dashboards, alerting policies, logs, and metrics to track latency, errors, and utilization trends continuously.
choices.D: Use Cloud Trace with Cloud Profiler only, and export all data to BigQuery for ad hoc analysis after incidents occur.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring within Google Cloud Observability is the best fit for continuous performance monitoring because it supports dashboards, alerts, metrics, and logs for tracking latency, error rates, and resource utilization over time.

questions.question: A media streaming startup runs a Java service on Compute Engine and sees rising CPU usage after a feature launch. The service is still meeting availability targets, but response times vary under load and the team cannot tell whether the bottleneck is CPU, memory, or thread contention. They want a low-overhead way to identify where the application spends time in production and then optimize the code. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Logging export sinks to BigQuery and run scheduled SQL queries against request logs to infer the hot code paths.
choices.B: Use Cloud Trace and Cloud Deploy to compare release versions and shift traffic gradually until response times improve.
choices.C: Use Cloud Profiler with Google Cloud Observability to capture CPU and memory profiles in production and identify the application bottleneck.
choices.D: Use Network Intelligence Center and Cloud VPN to inspect packet loss and route changes before changing the application.

questions.explanation: Cloud Profiler is designed to identify CPU and memory bottlenecks with low overhead in production, making it appropriate for finding where the service spends time before optimizing the code.

questions.question: A global SaaS company serves users from North America, Europe, and Asia through a hybrid network that uses Cloud Interconnect and a regional backend on Google Cloud. The operations team sees slow page loads in Europe and suspects that traffic may be taking a suboptimal path to the nearest backend. They need visual network topology and path analysis to pinpoint high-latency links before changing the architecture. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud DNS routing policies and Cloud Load Balancing to move all traffic to a single global backend.
choices.B: Use Cloud Armor adaptive protection and Cloud CDN to block abusive clients and cache static content closer to users.
choices.C: Use Cloud NAT and VPC firewall rules to inspect the traffic flow and then adjust subnet sizes for better throughput.
choices.D: Use Network Intelligence Center and its topology and network path visualization tools to identify the high-latency paths.

questions.explanation: Network Intelligence Center provides topology and path visualization that helps identify high-latency network paths, which is exactly what the team needs before redesigning the network.

questions.question: A financial services team runs an API on Google Cloud and wants to improve performance in a measurable way after each release. They need specific targets for response time, error rate, and resource consumption, and they want those targets to align with business outcomes such as faster loan approvals. Leadership also wants a clear way to judge whether tuning work is actually helping users. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Create Cloud Build triggers for every commit and use Cloud Deploy canary releases to replace performance targets with release velocity metrics.
choices.B: Define SMART performance objectives and measure them with Cloud Monitoring metrics, dashboards, and alerts tied to service-level indicators.
choices.C: Set up Cloud SQL query insights and Memorystore statistics to measure only database latency because application response time follows database health.
choices.D: Use Cloud Logging severity filters and Error Reporting counts as the only measurements for application performance objectives.

questions.explanation: The scenario asks for measurable performance objectives aligned to business goals, and SMART objectives tracked with Cloud Monitoring metrics and alerts best support that continuous optimization approach.

questions.question: An e-commerce company is improving a new mobile shopping experience hosted on Google Cloud. The product manager wants to combine technical telemetry with user-centric measures such as task success, retention, and satisfaction to understand whether performance changes are actually improving the experience. The team needs a repeatable way to track these metrics over time and present them to engineers and stakeholders. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Trace and Cloud Profiler alone, because backend traces and CPU profiles fully represent customer satisfaction.
choices.B: Use Vertex AI Search and Cloud CDN to infer user happiness from search relevance and edge cache hit rates.
choices.C: Use BigQuery and Looker Studio to analyze HEART framework survey data together with Cloud Monitoring metrics and Cloud Logging exports.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run traffic splitting and Cloud Build approvals to collect customer feedback during deployment windows.

questions.explanation: The HEART framework focuses on user-centered metrics, and combining survey or feedback data in BigQuery and Looker Studio with Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging provides a practical way to track both user experience and technical performance.

questions.question: A fintech company runs a payment service on Google Kubernetes Engine and Cloud SQL. The platform team notices that incident reviews usually focus on blame instead of learning, and recurring issues are not being turned into action items. They want to improve team learning, reduce repeat incidents, and create a repeatable process for capturing lessons learned after outages. The solution must help the team share knowledge across developers and SREs while supporting a blameless culture. What should you do?

choices.A: Establish blameless retrospectives using Google Chat and Google Docs, and track action items in Google Cloud Tasks or Jira integrated with Cloud Pub/Sub notifications.
choices.B: Use Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring dashboards to review incidents, then assign corrective tasks in Google Sheets after each outage.
choices.C: Move the workload to Compute Engine and use Cloud Deploy to restart failed services automatically after each incident.
choices.D: Create a centralized incident archive in Cloud Storage and require all engineers to read the postmortem before the next deployment.

questions.explanation: Blameless retrospectives and shared knowledge capture directly support continuous improvement and a learning culture. Google Chat and Google Docs help teams collaborate on findings, while tracked action items ensure follow-through on improvements.

questions.question: A retail company completes a major migration to Google Cloud and wants to improve future project delivery. Leadership asks each project team to identify what should start, stop, and continue after every release or major change. The teams need a structured way to collect feedback, document decisions, and turn recurring process problems into measurable improvements. The company also wants a lightweight approach that does not add a new standalone application. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Forms to collect Start-Stop-Continue feedback, store the results in BigQuery, and visualize trends in Looker Studio.
choices.B: Use Cloud Deploy release approvals to capture retrospective comments and export them to Cloud Storage for later review.
choices.C: Use Dataproc to generate retrospective reports from application logs and email them to the team after each release.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build to run retrospective surveys and store responses directly in Cloud SQL for every project.

questions.explanation: Google Forms provides a simple way to structure retrospective input, BigQuery can retain and analyze the results, and Looker Studio can help identify recurring process improvements over time.

questions.question: A media company wants product teams to try new cloud services and compare the impact of changes without affecting production traffic. The teams need a safe way to run experiments, measure conversion changes, and decide whether a new approach should be rolled out more broadly. They prefer a managed solution that can split traffic and analyze results without building custom experimentation tooling. Which solution should you recommend?
