choices.A: Use Cloud Armor to direct test traffic to separate backend buckets and compare request counts in Cloud Logging.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run Jobs to run both versions of the application and compare their CPU utilization in Cloud Monitoring.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Experiments to track model metrics, then use Traffic Director to compare user conversion rates.
choices.D: Use Cloud CDN cache invalidation to alternate responses between two app versions and evaluate latency in Trace.

questions.explanation: This is the best available option among the choices because it uses Google Cloud services to separate traffic paths for controlled comparison, although a real A/B testing solution would typically be implemented at the application or load balancer layer. The other options focus on unrelated services or metrics.

questions.question: A software company runs many GKE clusters and Compute Engine VMs across multiple projects. FinOps wants engineering teams to identify underused resources, compare costs by service, and find opportunities to reduce monthly spend without affecting production availability. They also want periodic recommendations that can be reviewed during planning meetings. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy to schedule rolling restarts of idle workloads and measure the savings in Cloud Trace.
choices.B: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards alone to review VM utilization and manually infer cost savings from CPU graphs.
choices.C: Export Cloud Billing data to BigQuery and use Looker Studio with Recommender API findings to review idle resources and rightsizing opportunities.
choices.D: Use Cloud Storage lifecycle rules to move all logs to Coldline and assume compute costs will follow the same pattern.

questions.explanation: Exporting billing data to BigQuery enables detailed cost analysis, and Recommender API provides actionable optimization recommendations such as rightsizing and idle resource detection. Looker Studio makes it easier to review these trends with stakeholders.

questions.question: A healthcare company wants its engineering organization to stay current with new Google Cloud capabilities and continuously improve its architecture choices. Leaders want teams to learn from each other, test new services in low-risk environments, and share findings across departments. The solution must support ongoing learning and help teams evaluate whether a new service can lower operational effort or improve service quality. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Source Repositories to document service changes, then require approvals in Cloud Build before any team can experiment.
choices.B: Use Cloud Functions to send weekly training emails and store all experiment results in Cloud Firestore for ad hoc review.
choices.C: Create a shared knowledge program with Google Chat, Google Sites, and Cloud Shell tutorials, and run pilots in separate Google Cloud projects.
choices.D: Move all proof-of-concept workloads to App Engine standard and use Cloud Logging to compare their performance to production.

questions.explanation: A shared knowledge program plus isolated pilot projects supports continuous learning, experimentation, and safe evaluation of new services. Google Chat and Google Sites help spread knowledge, and separate projects limit risk during trials.

questions.question: A retail company runs several teams on Google Cloud and wants to reduce recurring incidents while also encouraging engineers to try new ideas safely. The CTO says the teams often avoid proposing changes because past failures were blamed on individuals, and the company wants a repeatable way to improve how teams work after incidents and releases. They need a practice that helps teams learn from mistakes, share knowledge, and make incremental process changes without slowing delivery. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a centralized operations runbook in Cloud Storage and require all teams to follow the same manual approval steps for every release.
choices.B: Establish blameless retrospectives after major incidents using Google Meet and track action items in Cloud Tasks or Cloud Workflows.
choices.C: Use Cloud Deploy to standardize releases and Cloud Build to enforce the same pipeline for every application change.
choices.D: Move application logs to Cloud Logging and use Cloud Monitoring alerts to notify engineers when incidents recur.

questions.explanation: Blameless retrospectives directly support continuous improvement by helping teams learn from incidents, share knowledge, and identify actionable process changes in a psychologically safe way. Google Meet can support the retrospective session, and Cloud Tasks or Cloud Workflows can help track follow-up actions.

questions.question: A media company wants its product teams to experiment with new cloud services to improve customer personalization, but leadership is concerned about risk and uncontrolled spending. The company needs an approach that encourages rapid experimentation, supports measurable learning, and lets teams compare outcomes before wider rollout. The solution must also help teams reuse datasets and iterate quickly across small pilot projects. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Vertex AI Workbench and BigQuery to run pilot experiments, and publish successful results through Looker dashboards for review.
choices.B: Use Memorystore for Redis and Cloud SQL to store experiment outputs so teams can manually compare results in spreadsheets.
choices.C: Create separate projects in Google Cloud and use Cloud Identity to grant each team broad owner permissions for faster experimentation.
choices.D: Use Cloud Composer to schedule experiments and Cloud Run jobs to execute model training in the same environment as production.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Workbench and BigQuery provide a practical environment for data-driven experimentation, while Looker can share results and enable informed decisions before broader adoption. This aligns with continuous improvement and innovation by enabling measurable pilot testing and reuse of data assets.

questions.question: A software company notices that its Google Cloud bill has grown steadily because development environments are left running after business hours and test workloads are provisioned at full size all week. Engineering managers want a way to identify idle capacity, detect waste patterns, and reduce costs without affecting production services. They also want a solution that gives them dashboards they can use in monthly review meetings. What should you do?

choices.A: Export billing data to BigQuery and build Looker dashboards to identify idle resources and recurring spend patterns.
choices.B: Move development databases to Cloud Spanner so they scale automatically and eliminate unused capacity.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor policies to rate-limit development traffic and reduce the number of active test requests.
choices.D: Place all nonproduction workloads behind Internal Load Balancing and use Cloud NAT to reduce external usage charges.

questions.explanation: Exporting billing data to BigQuery enables detailed spend analysis, and Looker provides executive-friendly dashboards to spot idle resources and cost trends. This is a common optimization pattern for finding waste and supporting regular cost reviews.

questions.question: An e-commerce company wants to improve feature prioritization for its customer portal. Product managers need to correlate application metrics, sales data, and customer behavior to decide which improvements will have the biggest business impact. The solution must support reporting across multiple datasets and allow analysts to build shared dashboards for leadership. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Centralize the data in BigQuery and use Looker to create dashboards that combine business and operational metrics.
choices.B: Use Cloud SQL for analytics and generate reports directly from application tables using scheduled queries.
choices.C: Use Pub/Sub topics to stream all records to Cloud Run services that generate weekly PDF reports.
choices.D: Store application metrics in Cloud Logging and use Cloud Monitoring dashboards to compare them with sales outcomes.

questions.explanation: BigQuery is the appropriate analytics platform for combining business and operational datasets, and Looker is designed for shared dashboards and decision support. This provides the data-driven insight needed to prioritize features based on impact.

questions.question: A financial services team has just completed a large migration to Google Cloud and wants to ensure the organization keeps improving after each release and incident. The team needs a structured way to review what worked, what should stop, and what should continue, and they want a repeatable record of follow-up actions. They also want the review process to support collaboration across engineering, operations, and product stakeholders. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy release approvals and Cloud Build triggers to require sign-off before each production change.
choices.B: Hold Start-Stop-Continue retrospectives in Google Meet and store outcomes and owners in Google Chat or Cloud Tasks.
choices.C: Create a shared folder in Cloud Storage for incident notes and use Cloud Logging queries to archive root-cause analysis documents.
choices.D: Use Cloud Monitoring uptime checks and Error Reporting to automatically generate postmortem summaries after each incident.

questions.explanation: Start-Stop-Continue retrospectives are specifically recommended for continuous improvement because they structure learning and action planning after projects or incidents. Google Meet supports collaboration, and Google Chat or Cloud Tasks can track outcomes and accountability.

questions.question: A software company runs three environments on Google Cloud. Production requires high availability and predictable performance, but development and testing are short-lived and often idle overnight. The finance team wants to reduce spend without changing the release process, and the platform team wants the environments to remain easy to provision through Infrastructure as Code. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move all environments to Google Kubernetes Engine with regional clusters and reserve the same node pool size for production, development, and testing.
choices.B: Use Compute Engine managed instance groups with premium machine types for all environments and keep them always on to maintain consistent behavior.
choices.C: Use BigQuery reservations and Cloud Storage lifecycle policies to absorb the cost of development and testing workloads.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine and Cloud Run to provision production with high-availability resources, and use Spot VMs or ephemeral Cloud Run services for development and testing.

questions.explanation: Production should use environment-appropriate high-availability resources, while development and testing should use cost-efficient ephemeral capacity. Spot VMs and Cloud Run are appropriate for short-lived, flexible workloads and help avoid paying for idle resources.

questions.question: A media analytics team is moving a batch image-processing pipeline to Google Cloud. The workload runs only when new files arrive, scales sharply during business hours, and can tolerate brief interruptions because failed jobs are retried from Cloud Storage. The team wants the lowest practical cost while still supporting bursty demand. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use GKE Standard with a fixed node pool and a Cloud SQL backend so the workers can process files whenever they arrive.
choices.B: Use Spanner for the job state and run the workers on sole-tenant Compute Engine VMs for maximum isolation.
choices.C: Run the processing service on Cloud Run and trigger it from Pub/Sub or Cloud Storage events so capacity scales with demand.
choices.D: Deploy the pipeline on a continuously running Compute Engine managed instance group with autoscaling disabled to keep performance stable.

questions.explanation: This is an event-driven workload with bursty demand, so Cloud Run is a strong fit because it scales automatically and charges based on usage. Event triggers from Pub/Sub or Cloud Storage align well with the workload pattern and minimize idle infrastructure cost.

questions.question: An online retailer runs a stateless API on Google Kubernetes Engine. Traffic spikes during promotions, but the service is underutilized for long periods overnight. The engineering lead wants to reduce cost while ensuring the application can still handle sudden increases in traffic, and the team prefers to keep the current container platform. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Place the API behind Cloud Load Balancing and use Cloud CDN to cache all dynamic responses.
choices.B: Use a larger fixed GKE node pool with more n1-standard VMs so the cluster can handle peak traffic without scaling events.
choices.C: Enable horizontal pod autoscaling for the GKE workload and cluster autoscaling for the node pool, with maximum scaling limits.
choices.D: Move the API to Cloud Run functions and store session data in Memorystore for Redis to avoid any need for Kubernetes.

questions.explanation: A stateless containerized API on GKE benefits from both pod-level and node-level autoscaling. Horizontal pod autoscaling and cluster autoscaling reduce spend during quiet periods while preserving capacity for promotion spikes.

questions.question: A fintech company has a Compute Engine-based risk-scoring service. Profiling shows the application is CPU-bound, benefits from newer processor instruction sets, and does not need a large amount of memory. The team wants to improve price-performance without changing the application architecture. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move the service to Cloud Run and store intermediate results in Cloud SQL to optimize throughput.
choices.B: Switch the service to Spanner so the application can benefit from globally consistent reads and writes.
choices.C: Benchmark the workload and migrate it to a more suitable Compute Engine machine family, such as a compute-optimized series.
choices.D: Migrate the service to a memory-optimized machine family to reduce latency during peak scoring periods.

questions.explanation: The workload is CPU-bound and should be matched to an appropriate Compute Engine machine family based on profiling and benchmarking. Compute-optimized instances can improve price-performance for CPU-heavy services without redesigning the application.

questions.question: A startup plans to deploy a new internal application on Google Cloud. The app serves employees in one geographic area, has moderate performance needs, and does not require multi-region availability. The CFO wants the team to minimize infrastructure cost, and the architects want the deployment to remain close to the users to avoid unnecessary latency. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Choose a single Google Cloud region that balances cost and proximity to the users, then deploy the app there.
choices.B: Deploy the application in every Google Cloud region and use global external HTTP(S) Load Balancing to route users to the nearest endpoint.
choices.C: Use a multi-region Spanner instance and Cloud Run services so the application can fail over anywhere with no regional preference.
choices.D: Use a sole-tenant Compute Engine cluster in the most expensive region to maximize predictable performance.

questions.explanation: When multi-region availability is not required, choosing a single region that meets cost and proximity goals is the best optimization strategy. This reduces unnecessary geographic replication and keeps the deployment aligned with the application's actual requirements.

questions.topic: Cost optimization for variable container workloads
questions.question: Cymbal Retail runs its product search and enrichment services on Google Kubernetes Engine, and traffic spikes sharply during promotions while dropping overnight and on weekdays. The platform team wants to reduce compute cost without affecting production availability, and they need an approach that works with their existing containerized applications. They also want to keep cluster operations manageable as they continue modernizing their technical stack. What should you do?

choices.A: Migrate the workloads to Cloud Run and use Cloud CDN to absorb the promotional traffic spikes.
choices.B: Move the services to Compute Engine managed instance groups with regional autoscaling and keep the Kubernetes manifests unchanged.
choices.C: Consolidate the services on Cloud SQL and use read replicas to handle the promotional traffic spikes.
choices.D: Enable GKE Cluster Autoscaler and Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, and use Spot Pods only for non-production batch workloads.

questions.explanation: GKE autoscaling lets Cymbal Retail match capacity to changing demand, which is the right cost-optimization approach for variable container workloads. Spot Pods are appropriate only for interruptible non-production batch jobs, not for production request-serving services.

questions.topic: Selecting managed databases for global retail catalog data
questions.question: Cymbal Retail is redesigning the product catalog backend because its current mix of MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server systems is expensive to operate and difficult to scale for global shoppers. The catalog data must support low-latency reads from multiple regions, preserve strong consistency for product updates, and reduce operational overhead. The architecture team wants a solution that fits the company’s modernization goals and minimizes manual database administration. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Bigtable with BigQuery federated queries for transactional catalog updates and product searches.
choices.B: Use AlloyDB for PostgreSQL with cross-region replicas and export catalog snapshots to Cloud Storage for analytics.
choices.C: Use Cloud Spanner for the catalog database and keep product assets in Cloud Storage with lifecycle policies.
choices.D: Use Cloud SQL for MySQL with read replicas in multiple regions and Cloud Load Balancing in front of the application.

questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is the best fit for globally distributed, strongly consistent catalog data with low operational overhead. It reduces administration compared with self-managed or less globally consistent database options and aligns with Cymbal Retail’s scale and modernization goals.

questions.topic: Improving search relevance with AI-powered product discovery
questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to improve product discovery on its website and mobile app because customers often browse by vague requests such as "outfit for rainy commute" or "gift for a 10-year-old who likes robotics." The team needs a managed Google Cloud solution that can index product content, support semantic search, and improve relevance without building a custom retrieval engine from scratch. They also want a path that can integrate with their conversational commerce initiative. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud SQL full-text search and Memorystore for Redis to accelerate catalog lookups for the application.
choices.B: Use BigQuery ML to train a tabular classification model and export predictions to Pub/Sub.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Vector Search with embeddings generated from product descriptions and query text.
choices.D: Use Cloud Translation API to translate product titles into more languages and store them in Firestore.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Vector Search is designed for semantic retrieval and improves product discovery for natural-language shopping requests. It is a strong fit for Cymbal Retail’s conversational commerce and search relevance goals.

questions.topic: Reducing operational overhead for file-based integrations
questions.question: Cymbal Retail still relies on SFTP file transfers and batch ETL jobs to exchange inventory and supplier data with on-premises systems, and these workflows often create delays and error-prone reprocessing. The company wants to modernize integration while reducing data-center hosting costs and improving monitoring of failed transfers. The new solution must support secure partner connectivity and event-driven processing for downstream systems. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud VPN with Compute Engine instances running cron jobs to pull files from the SFTP servers.
choices.B: Use Cloud Data Fusion to schedule nightly copy jobs directly between the on-premises databases and BigQuery.
choices.C: Replace the file exchange with Cloud Storage notifications, Pub/Sub, and Cloud Run services for event-driven processing.
choices.D: Move all batch transfers to Cloud Composer and keep the files on-premises for processing.

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage event notifications combined with Pub/Sub and Cloud Run modernize file-based integration into an event-driven design with less operational overhead. This also improves observability and reduces the need for manual batch processing.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail currently uses Grafana, Nagios, and Elastic across several environments, but the tools generate inconsistent alerts and significant operational overhead. The architecture team wants to standardize monitoring for the modernized cloud environment and reduce time spent correlating incidents across services. They need an approach that supports application metrics, logs, and alerting for both containerized services and serverless components. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy Dataproc clusters to aggregate logs and metrics from all systems into HDFS.
choices.B: Use BigQuery to ingest metrics and run scheduled queries for alerting on service health.
choices.C: Use Cloud Trace alone because distributed tracing replaces the need for logs and metrics.
choices.D: Adopt Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, and Managed Service for Prometheus for centralized telemetry and alerting.

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, and Managed Service for Prometheus provide a unified observability stack for logs, metrics, and alerting. This reduces fragmentation and operational effort while fitting Cymbal Retail’s modern container and serverless architecture.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants to speed up software delivery for its containerized customer-facing applications while reducing outages caused by frequent releases. The engineering leaders need a way to measure whether changes are improving delivery performance, and they want metrics that can be reviewed by both development and operations teams. They also want to compare teams across multiple environments and identify bottlenecks in the release process. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Logging dashboards to review release events and manually compare deployment timestamps across GKE clusters.
choices.B: Use Cloud Monitoring uptime checks and alerting policies to track release success and identify failed deployments in real time.
choices.C: Adopt DORA metrics in Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging to track change failure rate, time to detect, and time to restore across environments.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build approval gates and Artifact Registry scans to measure release quality and deployment speed.

questions.explanation: DORA metrics are the recommended way to analyze software delivery performance and identify bottlenecks. Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging can provide the operational data needed to track change failure rate, time to detect, and time to restore across environments.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare is spending heavily on engineers to patch, scale, and operate its container platforms and databases. Leadership wants to reduce infrastructure administration costs without slowing down new provider onboarding or customer-facing application updates. They need a solution that lowers operational overhead while still supporting MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server workloads. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Run the databases on Compute Engine VMs and use Cloud Load Balancing and Managed Instance Groups to improve availability.
choices.B: Use Cloud SQL for MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server, and move containerized services to GKE Autopilot to reduce management overhead.
choices.C: Place the databases on Filestore and use Cloud Run for all application components to simplify provisioning.
choices.D: Migrate the container platform to GKE Standard and keep the databases on self-managed instances to preserve control over scaling.

questions.explanation: Cloud SQL and GKE Autopilot are managed services that reduce patching, scaling, and administration effort. This aligns with the goal of reducing operational cost while preserving support for the required database engines and container workloads.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare has recurring outages when traffic spikes unexpectedly on its healthcare portal. The company wants to reduce downtime, improve recovery time, and avoid overprovisioning capacity as a safety net. The platform team needs a way to improve operational reliability across multiple Kubernetes environments and make incident response more consistent. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Managed Service for Prometheus alone to collect Kubernetes metrics and eliminate the need for incident review.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build triggers to redeploy services automatically after every code change to reduce outage duration.
choices.C: Adopt SRE practices with Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and error budgets to drive automation and incident response improvements.
choices.D: Use Cloud Trace and Cloud Profiler to diagnose application latency and tune container resource requests.

questions.explanation: SRE practices focus on automation, observability, and incident response, which directly addresses recurring outages and recovery time. Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging provide the operational visibility needed to support that approach, including the use of error budgets.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants each application team to provision and optimize its own cloud resources while keeping spending aligned to business value. The platform team wants to reduce ticket-driven requests and give teams a consistent way to inspect performance, usage, and cost signals. They also want teams to react quickly when environments need to scale for new insurance providers. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Anthos Config Management to centrally approve all changes before teams can modify their environments.
choices.B: Use Organization Policy Service and VPC Service Controls to enforce deployment boundaries for every project.
choices.C: Provide teams access to Cloud Monitoring dashboards, Cloud Logging views, and Cloud Asset Inventory for self-service optimization.
choices.D: Use Cloud Billing budgets and Pub/Sub notifications to alert finance teams when monthly spend exceeds a threshold.

questions.explanation: Self-service optimization is about giving teams the tools to observe, manage, and optimize their own resources. Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Asset Inventory provide visibility and operational data so teams can act quickly without depending on manual ticket workflows.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants to ingest and process provider data more quickly so it can generate better healthcare trend reports and predictive insights. The data engineering team expects workloads to vary widely by day and does not want to maintain a large always-on cluster. They need a Google Cloud service that can process batch analytics efficiently and reduce operational overhead. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run jobs to execute SQL transformations directly against Cloud SQL databases.
choices.B: Use Dataproc Serverless for Apache Spark jobs to process incoming provider data on demand.
choices.C: Use Dataflow streaming pipelines for all provider data and keep the jobs running continuously in each environment.
choices.D: Use BigQuery scheduled queries only and store all incoming files in Cloud Storage for manual batch processing.

questions.explanation: Dataproc Serverless is a strong fit for variable batch analytics workloads because it removes cluster management and supports Spark-based processing. This helps EHR Healthcare process provider data efficiently while reducing administration overhead.

questions.question: A retail company runs a microservices application on Google Kubernetes Engine and uses Cloud Build for CI. The engineering leaders want to reduce the number of broken builds that reach main, shorten feedback time for developers, and make test results visible across teams. They also want the process to stay fully automated and integrate with their existing source repository and artifact workflow. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Move builds to Cloud Deploy and use Cloud Run jobs to compile code before promoting releases to Google Kubernetes Engine.
choices.B: Configure Cloud Build triggers to run unit and integration tests on every pull request and publish results to BigQuery and Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
choices.C: Use Cloud Source Repositories for branch protection and rely on GKE node auto-repair to detect failing builds.
choices.D: Use Artifact Registry to store source packages and run scheduled batch tests with Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build triggers for pull requests provide continuous integration, and publishing test and build results to BigQuery and Cloud Monitoring improves visibility into failures and trends across teams.

questions.question: A software company releases a customer-facing web app several times per day and currently requires engineers to SSH into instances to deploy updates. The company wants low-risk, repeatable deployments with approval gates, canary rollout support, and an easy rollback path. The platform runs on Google Kubernetes Engine and the team wants to keep deployment logic separate from application build logic. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Artifact Registry to store container images and Cloud Scheduler to trigger rollout scripts on Compute Engine VMs.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to build images and deploy directly to GKE with kubectl after each commit.
choices.C: Use Cloud Deploy to manage release pipelines, promote artifacts from Artifact Registry, and deploy to Google Kubernetes Engine with canary stages.
choices.D: Use Config Sync and Policy Controller to enforce manifests and let developers apply updates manually from Cloud Shell.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy is designed for deployment automation, promotion, approvals, canary rollouts, and rollback while keeping release management separate from build steps.

questions.question: A fintech company operates a distributed application on Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, and Cloud SQL. Users report intermittent latency spikes, but the team cannot quickly correlate application errors with infrastructure events. They want centralized logs, distributed tracing, and metrics that can be used to detect regressions after each release. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Enable Cloud Logging, Cloud Trace, and Cloud Monitoring dashboards with alerting on service latency and error rates.
choices.B: Deploy Firebase Performance Monitoring and App Hub to replace application-level observability.
choices.C: Export application logs to BigQuery and use Looker Studio alone to detect production incidents.
choices.D: Use Dataproc to process logs in batch and store trace data in Cloud Storage for later analysis.

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging, Cloud Trace, and Cloud Monitoring provide the core observability stack for logs, traces, metrics, dashboards, and alerting across Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, and Cloud SQL-backed systems.

questions.question: A health-tech startup wants to improve software delivery quality by expanding automated tests beyond unit tests to include integration and end-to-end checks in a secure environment. The team uses Cloud Build and Artifact Registry, and it needs tests to run consistently for each change without developers provisioning separate test infrastructure. It also wants test outputs to be retained for audit and troubleshooting. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Run tests in Cloud Build using ephemeral build steps, store reports in Cloud Storage, and publish build metadata to Artifact Registry.
choices.B: Use Google Kubernetes Engine with manual test pods and keep test reports on local persistent disks attached to nodes.
choices.C: Use Cloud Tasks to schedule tests and send results only through Cloud Pub/Sub messages.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy to execute unit tests and rely on Cloud SQL read replicas for end-to-end validation.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build supports automated test execution in isolated build steps, and Cloud Storage can retain test artifacts and reports for traceability and debugging.

questions.question: A media company has multiple development teams working on shared libraries used across dozens of services in Google Cloud. Releases are slowed by dependency drift, inconsistent versioning, and difficulty tracing which artifact was used by each deployment. The company wants to make code easier to reuse and change while preserving reproducibility and traceability across pipelines. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use BigQuery to track dependency versions and Cloud Scheduler to notify teams when libraries change.
choices.B: Store shared libraries in Cloud Storage buckets and have Cloud Run services download the latest version at startup.
choices.C: Use Cloud Source Repositories as the only artifact store and publish service images directly from source branches.
choices.D: Use Artifact Registry for versioned packages and container images, and integrate it with Cloud Build to stamp immutable build metadata.

questions.explanation: Artifact Registry provides versioned package and container storage, and Cloud Build can record build provenance and metadata to improve reproducibility and traceability.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is modernizing its catalog-enrichment service so supplier data can be transformed into product attributes and descriptions before publishing to its website and mobile app. The engineering team wants every change to be version-controlled, automatically tested, and promoted through development, staging, and production with minimal downtime. The solution must support containerized workloads on Google Cloud and allow fast rollback if a release causes search or catalog issues. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Composer to orchestrate the release process, use BigQuery to store container artifacts, and use GKE Autopilot to deploy directly to production.
choices.B: Use App Engine standard environment for deployments, use Cloud Source Repositories for manual approvals, and use Cloud Run jobs for database migrations.
choices.C: Use Pub/Sub to trigger deployments, use Filestore to host release artifacts, and use Compute Engine instance groups to copy containers between environments.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build to build and test the container image, use Artifact Registry to store versioned images, and use Cloud Deploy to promote releases through GKE environments.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, and Cloud Deploy provide a standard Google Cloud CI/CD flow for containerized applications with automated testing, environment promotion, and rollback support.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to standardize how its platform teams provision networking, Kubernetes clusters, and service accounts for new catalog services. They need repeatable deployments across multiple environments, source-controlled change history, and the ability to review infrastructure changes before applying them. The solution should work well with Google Cloud resources and support automated drift detection during delivery. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Terraform configurations stored in GitHub, and run Terraform plan and apply in Cloud Build to provision Google Cloud resources.
choices.B: Use Deployment Manager templates stored in a shared drive, and apply them from Cloud Shell after each approval meeting.
choices.C: Use gcloud commands in a Cloud Build step to create resources, and store the outputs in Cloud Storage for auditing.
choices.D: Use Config Connector only, and manage all infrastructure changes by editing Kubernetes manifests directly in the cluster.

questions.explanation: Terraform with GitHub and Cloud Build gives Cymbal Retail repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure provisioning with a clear plan/apply workflow and good support for Google Cloud resources.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is exposing internal catalog and order services to its mobile app and partner integrations. The team needs centralized authentication, quota enforcement, request logging, and versioned API rollout without forcing each backend service to implement the same controls. The APIs run on GKE and Cloud Run, and the company wants a managed Google Cloud solution that can sit in front of multiple services. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Apigee API Management to publish the APIs, apply policies for authentication and quotas, and route requests to GKE and Cloud Run backends.
choices.B: Use Pub/Sub topics for each API method, and have clients read responses from BigQuery views.
choices.C: Use External HTTP(S) Load Balancing with Cloud CDN, and expose each service through separate public IP addresses.
choices.D: Use Cloud Functions as a proxy for all traffic, and store API credentials in Cloud SQL tables.

questions.explanation: Apigee is the managed API management platform for centralized auth, quotas, analytics, and versioned API governance across GKE and Cloud Run backends.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is releasing a new conversational commerce feature that uses product discovery and natural-language recommendations. The business wants to test the feature with a small portion of traffic first, compare error rates and conversion metrics, and quickly switch back if latency increases. The application is containerized and runs on GKE. The release process must minimize risk during peak shopping periods. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the new version to a separate Cloud SQL instance, and point a subset of users to it by changing DNS records.
choices.B: Use rolling updates on the GKE Deployment with no traffic splitting, and increase the pod replica count to reduce risk.
choices.C: Use Cloud Build to redeploy the service every hour, and rely on Cloud Monitoring alerts to detect failures after the fact.
choices.D: Use GKE Ingress with a service mesh such as Cloud Service Mesh or Anthos Service Mesh to split traffic gradually between stable and canary versions.

questions.explanation: Traffic splitting with GKE and Cloud Service Mesh supports controlled canary releases, allowing Cymbal Retail to compare versions and rollback quickly if issues appear.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is replacing its legacy ETL-based product feed with an event-driven pipeline that updates catalog data several times per day. The platform team needs automated releases that can be promoted across environments, integrated with approval gates, and rolled back to a previous revision if downstream systems show errors. They want to manage the deployment of the pipeline components as code and keep the release process consistent across teams. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Dataflow templates scheduled from Cloud Scheduler, and store approval records in Cloud Spanner for each release.
choices.B: Use Workflows to update the application containers directly, and use Secret Manager to track deployment history.
choices.C: Use Memorystore for Redis to coordinate release steps, and use Cloud Storage notifications to trigger manual approvals.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy to manage the promotion pipeline, use Cloud Build to build artifacts, and deploy the workload to GKE or Cloud Run with revision-based rollbacks.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy plus Cloud Build gives Cymbal Retail a managed, consistent release workflow with promotion gates and rollback support for GKE or Cloud Run workloads.

questions.question: A retail company is moving from monthly releases to on-demand deployments for a containerized web application on Google Cloud. The team needs a repeatable release process, the ability to promote the same artifact across environments, and a way to reduce deployment risk with automated approvals and rollback. They also want to keep builds separate from runtime permissions and use managed services where possible. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Build to build and test the container image, store it in Artifact Registry, and deploy to Cloud Run through Cloud Deploy with progressive delivery and approval gates.
choices.B: Use Cloud Functions to trigger deployments from Cloud Scheduler, store artifacts in Cloud Storage, and use BigQuery to track release versions.
choices.C: Use Compute Engine instance groups for each environment, package the application into VM images with Packer, and promote images by copying them between zones.
choices.D: Use App Engine flexible environment to host each release, store source code in Cloud Source Repositories, and deploy manually through gcloud commands.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, and Cloud Deploy together support a managed continuous delivery pipeline with artifact promotion, approvals, and safer releases.

questions.question: A software team has frequent merge conflicts and long integration delays because developers commit large changes to a shared branch every few days. The team wants faster feedback from automated tests, fewer integration failures, and a standard process that fits a trunk-based workflow. They also need builds to run only when code is merged to the main branch and to reuse the same pipeline for multiple services. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Dataflow to validate source code changes, then deploy the application with App Engine standard after each developer push.
choices.B: Use Cloud Deploy release pipelines to approve changes before every commit, and run tests only after production rollout in GKE.
choices.C: Use Cloud Build triggers on the main branch, run unit and integration tests in the pipeline, and publish test results to Cloud Logging and Artifact Registry.
choices.D: Use Cloud Scheduler to start nightly test jobs on Compute Engine, and store test logs in Cloud Storage for later review.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build triggers provide automated CI on trunk commits, and test results can be captured in Google Cloud logging and artifacts for repeatable feedback.

questions.question: An e-commerce company needs automated testing for a microservices application that runs on GKE. The team wants to validate each commit with unit tests, run container-based integration tests against ephemeral environments, and store test artifacts centrally for traceability. They also want the testing approach to scale across several repositories without building separate infrastructure for each team. What should you do?

choices.A: Use App Engine traffic splitting to test each commit in production, and store validation reports in BigQuery.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to run unit and integration tests in containers, deploy ephemeral test environments to GKE, and store artifacts and images in Artifact Registry.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run jobs to execute all tests manually, and trigger them from a Pub/Sub topic when developers request validation.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine managed instance groups for test runs, and copy test output to Cloud Storage after each release.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build can automate both unit and containerized integration tests, while GKE and Artifact Registry support scalable ephemeral test environments and central artifact storage.

questions.question: A financial services team must deploy a stateless API across multiple GKE clusters in different regions. The release process needs staged rollout, approvals from operations, and the ability to promote the exact same container image from test to staging to production. The team wants a managed tool that integrates with their CI system and supports deployment history. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Build to publish images to Artifact Registry and use Cloud Deploy to manage multi-target rollouts to GKE with approvals and rollout tracking.
choices.B: Use Binary Authorization to approve every container image and deploy directly to each cluster with kubectl from developer laptops.
choices.C: Use Terraform to recreate the clusters on every release and use Cloud Run service revisions as the deployment history mechanism.
choices.D: Use Anthos Config Management to store manifests and use Cloud Scheduler to copy them into each cluster on a fixed schedule.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy provides managed release orchestration, approval gates, and rollout history across multiple GKE targets, with Cloud Build and Artifact Registry supplying immutable artifacts.

questions.question: A development organization wants stronger traceability between source code, builds, and releases for several applications deployed on Google Cloud. They need a version control strategy that supports peer review, reproducible builds, and an auditable link from a commit to the artifact that reached production. They also want a managed Google Cloud service for source hosting and automated pipeline triggers. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery to track commit history, and run application builds directly in Dataflow pipelines.
choices.B: Use Cloud Source Repositories for source control, configure Cloud Build triggers from branches or tags, and store signed artifacts in Artifact Registry.
choices.C: Use Cloud Storage buckets as the source of truth for code, and deploy updates by syncing objects into GKE.
choices.D: Use Firestore to store application versions, and trigger releases with Pub/Sub messages from developers.

questions.explanation: Cloud Source Repositories, Cloud Build triggers, and Artifact Registry together support traceable source control, reproducible builds, and auditable release artifacts.

questions.question: A retail company runs several microservices on Google Kubernetes Engine and wants to reduce release risk while increasing deployment frequency. Each service must support repeatable deployments, quick rollback, and approval of production changes without blocking developers for hours. The team also needs a single pipeline that can deploy to multiple environments and track release status across services. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Terraform to recreate the GKE clusters for each release and use Cloud Logging to verify success.
choices.B: Use Cloud Deploy to orchestrate promotions to GKE environments and integrate Cloud Build for automated build and test stages.
choices.C: Use Artifact Registry to store container images and use Cloud Run jobs to deploy each release to production.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build to compile and test the images, then deploy directly to GKE with kubectl from developer workstations.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy is designed for progressive delivery and release orchestration across environments, while Cloud Build handles automated build and test stages. Together they reduce manual intervention and support controlled rollouts and rollbacks.

questions.question: A SaaS team has frequent merge conflicts because developers keep long-lived feature branches open for weeks. Leadership wants a change that improves integration speed, supports small batches, and makes it easier to release code several times per day. The solution must work with automated tests and code review in the existing Google Cloud toolchain. What should you do?

choices.A: Adopt trunk-based development using Cloud Source Repositories with short-lived branches and Cloud Build-triggered tests on each merge.
choices.B: Keep long-lived feature branches in Cloud Source Repositories and use Cloud Deploy only after quarterly code freezes.
choices.C: Use Artifact Registry to version source code and Cloud Run to merge pull requests automatically.
choices.D: Move all source code to Cloud Storage buckets and use Cloud Functions to combine changes before each release.

questions.explanation: Trunk-based development reduces merge conflicts and supports small, frequent integrations. Cloud Source Repositories and Cloud Build fit the workflow by enabling short-lived branches, automated testing, and rapid merges.

questions.question: A fintech company needs to improve software quality by running unit tests, integration tests, and security checks on every commit. The pipeline must run in Google Cloud, produce auditable test results, and fail fast before code is promoted to staging. The team also wants a managed service that can invoke custom steps without maintaining build servers. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Run jobs to run tests continuously and store the results in Cloud SQL.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to execute test steps on every commit and publish logs and artifacts to Cloud Logging and Artifact Registry.
choices.C: Use GKE Autopilot to host Jenkins and use Cloud Storage only for the source code.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy to run application tests after production rollout and store results in BigQuery.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build is a managed CI service that can run automated test stages on each commit and provide auditable logs. Artifact Registry stores build artifacts, and Cloud Logging captures the execution details for troubleshooting and compliance.

questions.question: A healthcare provider deploys application releases to Cloud Run and uses Cloud SQL for transactional data. Schema changes often break backward compatibility, and the team needs database migrations that are versioned, repeatable, and coordinated with application deployments. They also want to reduce manual DBA intervention during releases. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Build to run versioned migration scripts against Cloud SQL and gate Cloud Run deployment on migration success.
choices.B: Use Datastream to replicate schema changes from production into staging before every deployment.
choices.C: Use Cloud SQL import and export for each release and apply schema changes from developer laptops.
choices.D: Use Memorystore for Redis to store database migration history and let Cloud Deploy execute the SQL statements.

questions.explanation: Running versioned migration scripts in Cloud Build provides repeatable database change management and integrates the migration step with application deployment. Gating Cloud Run deployment on migration success helps avoid breaking changes and reduces manual intervention.

questions.question: A software organization wants strong traceability from source code to production artifacts. Developers need a managed Git repository in Google Cloud, automated builds triggered by commits, and immutable artifact storage for release packages. The company also wants to support reproducible deployments across multiple teams. How should you configure this?

choices.A: Use Cloud Storage buckets for source control, Cloud Build for artifact creation, and Cloud Run for release tagging.
choices.B: Use Artifact Registry as the Git repository and Cloud Build only for manual deployments to GKE.
choices.C: Use Cloud Source Repositories for source control, Cloud Build triggers for commits, and Artifact Registry for immutable artifacts.
choices.D: Use BigQuery to store source revisions, then use Cloud Deploy to generate Git history from release manifests.

questions.explanation: Cloud Source Repositories provides managed Git hosting, Cloud Build triggers automate builds from commits, and Artifact Registry stores immutable build outputs for traceable, reproducible releases.

questions.question: A retail company is moving its application delivery to Google Cloud and wants to reduce security defects before code is deployed. The team uses Cloud Build for CI/CD and Terraform for infrastructure, but developers sometimes introduce insecure IAM bindings and noncompliant network settings. The company needs centralized guardrails, automated checks before deployment, and a way to block misconfigured infrastructure code. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Cloud Armor to filter requests to the application and use Binary Authorization to allow only signed container images.
choices.B: Use Policy Controller with Open Policy Agent to enforce infrastructure-as-code policies in Cloud Build and combine it with Organization Policy Service constraints.
choices.C: Use Security Command Center to scan deployed resources daily and use Cloud Monitoring alerts to notify developers about misconfigurations.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy to promote releases through environments and add post-deployment manual approval steps for every change.

questions.explanation: Policy Controller and OPA can validate infrastructure code in the CI/CD pipeline before deployment, and Organization Policy Service provides centralized preventive guardrails for resource configuration.

questions.question: A media company runs containerized workloads on Google Kubernetes Engine and wants to ensure only approved application versions are released to production. The company requires that images be verified before deployment, release promotion must be automated, and deployments should fail if an untrusted artifact is used. The platform team also wants to keep the release process inside Google Cloud services. What should you do?

choices.A: Use GKE node auto-repair and Cloud Monitoring uptime checks to detect bad releases and roll them back manually.
choices.B: Use Cloud Deploy to manage the release pipeline and Binary Authorization to allow only verified container images to be deployed to GKE.
choices.C: Use Artifact Registry to store images and use Cloud Run jobs to scan the images after they are deployed to production.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build triggers to build images and Cloud Armor policies to block traffic to any release that is not approved.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy automates promotion of releases, and Binary Authorization enforces that only trusted and verified container images can be deployed to GKE.

questions.question: A financial services team provisions new Google Cloud projects with Terraform and wants to prevent developers from creating resources in unsupported regions. They also need to block the use of external IP addresses on certain workloads and apply the same restrictions across all new projects in the organization. The team wants these rules enforced before resources are created. What should you do?

choices.A: Use IAM Conditions on every project to allow only approved engineers to create resources in the correct region.
choices.B: Use VPC Service Controls to create perimeters around all projects and use Cloud NAT to hide external addresses.
choices.C: Use Security Command Center findings to alert on violations and use Cloud Functions to delete unsupported resources after deployment.
choices.D: Use Organization Policy Service constraints to restrict resource locations and external IP usage across the organization.

questions.explanation: Organization Policy Service provides centralized preventive controls that can restrict allowed regions and disallow external IP usage before resources are created.

questions.topic: Secure code review and vulnerability scanning
questions.question: A healthcare company stores source code in GitHub and builds container images in Cloud Build before deploying to GKE. The security team wants known vulnerabilities to be detected as early as possible, and they want releases to stop if critical issues are found in application code or container dependencies. They also want the checks to run as part of the normal build process. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Security Command Center to scan the source repository and use VPC Flow Logs to identify vulnerable packages.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run service revisions to validate the code after deployment and use Cloud Scheduler to run weekly dependency reports.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor to block requests to vulnerable services and use Cloud Logging sinks to export code findings to BigQuery.
choices.D: Use Artifact Analysis with Cloud Build to scan container images during the pipeline and use Approvals in Cloud Deploy for manual review.

questions.explanation: Artifact Analysis can scan container images for vulnerabilities during the build pipeline, and Cloud Deploy approvals can add a release gate for high-risk changes.

questions.question: An enterprise software team must ensure that every production deployment follows an approved change process. They use Cloud Build for builds and Cloud Deploy for releases, but auditors require that production promotion cannot happen unless a designated approver reviews the release. The company also wants the same release controls for multiple microservices deployed to GKE. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Binary Authorization to approve container images and use Artifact Registry tags to mark production-ready builds.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build triggers to deploy directly to GKE and use Cloud Monitoring dashboards to track which releases were approved.
choices.C: Use IAM role bindings to restrict developers from accessing production clusters and use Cloud Scheduler to delay deployments until business hours.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy release approvals to require human sign-off before production promotion and manage the rollout to GKE through delivery pipelines.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy supports approval gates that enforce an approved release process before promoting changes to production, including deployments to GKE.

questions.question: A retail company is moving a set of legacy web applications from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud. The team wants to reduce operational overhead, avoid replatforming all services at once, and keep the migration low-risk for business-critical workloads. They need an approach that lets them move applications in stages, validate each wave, and make it easier to modernize components later. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Cloud VMware Engine to lift and shift all applications into a managed VMware environment, then defer modernization until after the migration is complete.
choices.B: Use Migrate to Virtual Machines and Migrate to Containers to move workloads in phases, then place them behind an External Application Load Balancer for controlled cutover.
choices.C: Deploy the applications directly on Compute Engine with Persistent Disk and skip a structured migration plan so the team can move faster.
choices.D: Rebuild all applications on App Engine standard environment before migrating data, because this eliminates the need for wave planning.

questions.explanation: Staged migration with Migrate to Virtual Machines and Migrate to Containers supports lower-risk incremental moves and creates a path to modernization. The load balancer can help with traffic management during cutover.

questions.question: A software company is migrating a containerized internal platform from another cloud provider to Google Cloud. The platform must support repeatable deployments, minimize configuration drift, and allow the platform team to standardize releases across multiple environments. The team also wants a managed service that integrates with their existing source repository and CI system. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the platform on GKE and use Cloud Build triggers with Artifact Registry to build and promote container images through environments.
choices.B: Deploy the platform on Compute Engine instance groups and use Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL to centralize release metadata.
choices.C: Use App Engine flexible environment and Cloud Scheduler to copy release artifacts between projects on a fixed schedule.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run for Anthos and Cloud Source Repositories for every service so that application code and infrastructure are managed together.

questions.explanation: GKE with Cloud Build and Artifact Registry provides a repeatable, automated container deployment pipeline with strong support for standardizing releases and reducing drift.

questions.question: A financial services firm is migrating hundreds of Google Cloud resources into a new landing zone. The platform team wants every project, VPC, firewall rule, and service account to be created consistently from code and reviewed through pull requests. They also need a way to manage the deployment order of dependent resources across multiple teams. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Config Connector to create resources from Kubernetes manifests and rely on Cloud Run jobs to sequence dependencies.
choices.B: Use Cloud Deployment Manager templates with Cloud Storage buckets for state files, and apply changes manually after each review.
choices.C: Use gcloud CLI scripts stored in Cloud Source Repositories and execute them from Cloud Scheduler for each environment.
choices.D: Use Terraform with Cloud Build and remote state in Cloud Storage to provision the landing zone from version-controlled modules.

questions.explanation: Terraform is the standard IaC choice for multi-team landing zones, and Cloud Build plus Cloud Storage remote state supports reviewable, repeatable provisioning with dependency management.

questions.question: An insurance company is migrating an on-premises application stack to Google Cloud and wants to verify that the new environment behaves the same as the old one before full cutover. The team needs automated tests for application health, traffic handling, and configuration parity across migration waves. They also want to capture metrics and logs during validation to support go/no-go decisions. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy to validate VM images and Cloud Armor to compare application responses between source and target environments.
choices.B: Use Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring only after production cutover, then validate behavior with ad hoc SQL queries in BigQuery.
choices.C: Use Dataflow to replay migration traffic into Pub/Sub and verify readiness through batch reports in Looker.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build test steps, Cloud Monitoring uptime checks, and Cloud Logging to automate pre-cutover validation of the migrated stack.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build can run automated validation steps, while Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging provide the observability needed to assess readiness and compare behavior before cutover.

questions.question: A media company must migrate a 300 TB dataset from an on-premises environment to Google Cloud before deploying a new analytics platform. The WAN link is limited, the transfer window is short, and the team wants to minimize application downtime during final synchronization. They need a managed transfer option that can handle large volumes and support incremental updates. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to move the data directly into BigQuery and avoid staging in Cloud Storage.
choices.B: Use Cloud VPN and gcloud storage cp for all transfers because the dataset can be copied in the background.
choices.C: Use Transfer Appliance for the initial bulk load and then use Storage Transfer Service for incremental object synchronization.
choices.D: Use Filestore as a temporary landing zone and rsync the data into Cloud Storage during a maintenance window.

questions.explanation: Transfer Appliance is suited to very large initial transfers, and Storage Transfer Service can handle ongoing synchronization to reduce downtime during cutover.

questions.question: A retail company is redesigning its software delivery process to reduce merge conflicts and speed up releases. Its development teams currently maintain long-lived feature branches, which causes painful integrations and delayed testing. The company wants developers to commit changes more frequently, keep release risk low, and preserve traceability in source control. It also needs an approach that works well with automated builds and short feedback loops. What should you do?

choices.A: Store each developer's changes in individual Cloud Storage buckets and promote code to production with Compute Engine startup scripts.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to package code from feature branches and merge them manually into a shared integration branch only after quarterly stabilization.
choices.C: Move all development to separate long-lived release branches in Cloud Source Repositories and deploy only from tagged branches with Cloud Deploy.
choices.D: Adopt trunk-based development using Cloud Source Repositories or GitHub, and trigger Cloud Build on every commit to run tests and create build artifacts.

questions.explanation: Trunk-based development reduces merge conflicts and improves feedback speed by keeping branches short-lived and integrating frequently. Cloud Build supports automated verification on each commit.

questions.question: A media company wants to standardize application releases across development, test, and production environments. The release process must reduce manual intervention, support repeatable deployments, and allow controlled promotion after validation. The team uses container images and wants a Google Cloud-native deployment workflow with clear rollout visibility. Leadership also wants a simple path to pause or promote releases when a quality gate passes. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Compute Engine instance groups with startup scripts and update each environment by copying images directly between buckets in Cloud Storage.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to build container images, store them in Artifact Registry, and manage staged promotions with Cloud Deploy.
choices.C: Use Pub/Sub to send release messages and have each application instance pull its own version from Secret Manager at startup.
choices.D: Use Google Kubernetes Engine only for production and deploy to lower environments with ad hoc kubectl commands from Cloud Shell.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, and Cloud Deploy provide an automated, repeatable delivery pipeline with controlled promotion between stages.

questions.question: A fintech startup needs a continuous integration system that validates code on every commit, runs unit tests quickly, and produces deployable artifacts. The team wants to catch integration problems early and keep the pipeline consistent across multiple repositories. It also wants a managed Google Cloud service that can connect to source control and trigger builds automatically. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Build triggers connected to your source repository, and configure the pipeline to run tests and publish artifacts to Artifact Registry.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run jobs to execute tests after production deployment and store the results in BigQuery for later analysis.
choices.C: Use Cloud Composer to orchestrate source control merges and manually approve each commit before tests begin.
choices.D: Use Google Kubernetes Engine namespaces as the primary CI system and run builds interactively from each developer workstation.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build is a managed CI service that can trigger on commits, run tests, and publish artifacts consistently across repositories.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is moving to a modern delivery model and wants strong reproducibility and traceability for application changes. The organization requires a single source of truth for code, audit-friendly history, and support for peer review before merges. It also wants the repository strategy to integrate cleanly with automated builds and security scans. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Source Repositories or a connected GitHub repository with protected branches and Cloud Build triggers for every merge request.
choices.B: Store application source in Cloud Storage and use scheduled Dataflow jobs to synchronize changes into production every night.
choices.C: Use Firebase Hosting as the system of record for source code and deploy approved versions with Cloud Run revisions.
choices.D: Keep code in individual developer projects in Cloud Shell and use Memorystore to distribute changes to downstream environments.

questions.explanation: A Git-based repository with protected branches provides traceability and reproducibility, and Cloud Build integrates directly for automated validation.

questions.question: An e-commerce company wants to improve software quality by adding automated tests throughout its delivery lifecycle. The team needs its pipeline to run fast unit tests on every commit, execute more expensive integration tests before release, and keep test artifacts available for investigation. The solution should use Google Cloud services and fit naturally into an automated CI/CD workflow. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Build to run unit and integration test steps, store build outputs in Artifact Registry, and publish logs to Cloud Logging.
choices.B: Use Google Kubernetes Engine to run tests manually from a dedicated testing cluster and archive results in Cloud Storage only.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run services to execute tests only after users report defects and store defect data in Firestore.
choices.D: Use Dataproc jobs to validate application code and rely on BigQuery scheduled queries to determine whether the build passed.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build is well suited to automated test execution in a CI pipeline, with Artifact Registry for artifacts and Cloud Logging for diagnostics.

questions.question: A financial services company is moving several microservices to Google Cloud and wants to reduce security defects before code reaches production. The team uses Terraform for infrastructure, Cloud Build for CI/CD, and Cloud Run for deployment. They need centralized guardrails that prevent noncompliant infrastructure changes from being deployed, while still allowing developers to move quickly. They also want policy checks to run automatically on each pull request and build. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Policy Controller with Open Policy Agent to validate Terraform and Kubernetes manifests in Cloud Build, and use Organization Policy Service constraints for centralized preventive guardrails.
choices.B: Use Security Command Center and Cloud Logging alerts to detect misconfigurations after deployment, and remediate them with Cloud Run jobs.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor and Binary Authorization to inspect all infrastructure code changes, and enforce release gates through Google Kubernetes Engine admission controls.
choices.D: Use IAM Conditions and VPC Service Controls to block deployment access, and rely on Cloud Deploy approvals to validate every Terraform change before release.

questions.explanation: Policy Controller and OPA can enforce infrastructure-as-code policy checks in the CI/CD pipeline before deployment, while Organization Policy Service provides centralized preventive constraints at the resource hierarchy level.

questions.question: A retail company deploys containerized services to Google Kubernetes Engine and wants to ensure only trusted images are released. Their developers build images in Cloud Build, and the security team wants deployment-time enforcement based on image provenance and vulnerability status. The solution must integrate with existing GKE deployment workflows and prevent unverified images from being deployed. What should you do?

choices.A: Enable Binary Authorization for GKE and configure Cloud Build to produce signed container images that are verified before deployment.
choices.B: Use Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning only, and allow GKE to deploy any image while Cloud Monitoring alerts on risky packages.
choices.C: Use Cloud Deploy to approve releases manually and store images in Container Registry so signed attestations are unnecessary.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls around Artifact Registry and enforce deployment access with IAM roles on the GKE cluster.

questions.explanation: Binary Authorization enforces trusted-image deployment in GKE using attestations or signatures, and Cloud Build can create signed artifacts as part of the release pipeline.

questions.topic: Automated provisioning and policy as code
questions.question: A healthcare startup provisions projects, networks, and service accounts through Terraform and wants to prevent insecure configurations before they are applied. The security team needs the same policy checks to work for both GKE manifests and Terraform plans. They also want the checks to run in Cloud Build and block merges that violate organizational rules. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Policy Controller with OPA to enforce policy-as-code on Terraform and Kubernetes manifests in Cloud Build, and gate merges on policy test results.
choices.B: Use Cloud Asset Inventory to review resource changes, and require manual approvals in Cloud Deploy for all infrastructure updates.
choices.C: Use Organization Policy Service only, because it can validate every Terraform and Kubernetes resource during commit time.
choices.D: Use Security Command Center to scan the Terraform state after deployment, then use Cloud Functions to auto-remediate any violations.

questions.explanation: Policy Controller with OPA is designed for policy-as-code enforcement in CI/CD, letting teams validate Terraform and Kubernetes configurations before deployment and block noncompliant changes early.

questions.topic: Scan for known vulnerabilities before application deployment
questions.question: A media company builds Java and Python services in Cloud Build and packages them into container images stored in Artifact Registry. The security team wants to stop vulnerable builds before release and requires an automated check for known vulnerabilities in application dependencies and container images. The solution should work with existing build pipelines and provide actionable results early in the release process. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Security Command Center posture management to inspect source code repositories directly and approve builds after production deployment.
choices.B: Enable Artifact Analysis vulnerability scanning and configure Cloud Build to fail the pipeline when critical findings are detected in images or packages.
choices.C: Use Cloud Armor threat intelligence policies to block builds that contain vulnerable libraries, and rely on Cloud Run revisions for release control.
choices.D: Use VPC Service Controls to isolate Artifact Registry and Google Cloud Storage, then allow all builds to continue without vulnerability checks.

questions.explanation: Artifact Analysis can scan container images and packages for known vulnerabilities, and Cloud Build can be configured to stop the release when severe findings are present.

questions.topic: Monitor application code for known vulnerabilities
questions.question: A software company uses GitHub repositories, Cloud Build, and GKE to deliver services on Google Cloud. The platform team wants developers to detect code-level security issues early, including vulnerable dependencies and misconfigurations, and they want findings surfaced in the development workflow rather than after production. They need a Google Cloud-native approach that supports security testing during the development lifecycle. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud DNS policies to prevent vulnerable packages from being downloaded, and use IAM Conditions to approve code merges.
choices.B: Use Security Command Center to block all repository access and rely on Cloud Deploy to review source code changes.
choices.C: Use Cloud Logging sinks to export application logs to BigQuery and scan them after deployment for dependency issues.
choices.D: Use CodeQL or Dependency scanning in the CI pipeline with Cloud Build, and feed results into Security Command Center for centralized visibility.

questions.explanation: Code scanning and dependency scanning in the CI pipeline help identify vulnerabilities early, and Security Command Center can aggregate findings for centralized security visibility.

questions.question: A retail company is planning to migrate 120 virtual machines and several small web applications from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud. The team has limited experience with cloud migrations, must minimize downtime during cutover, and needs a clear understanding of dependencies before they build the target environment. They also want to compare the current environment with the future state and create a phased migration approach that reduces risk. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery and Dataflow to analyze server logs, then copy the application servers directly into Cloud Storage before cutover.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build and Artifact Registry to containerize all applications first, then redeploy them to GKE without performing a migration assessment.
choices.C: Use Migrate to Virtual Machines and Migration Center to assess the workloads, inventory dependencies, and plan a phased migration to Compute Engine.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run and Pub/Sub to re-architect the applications immediately, then migrate the databases after the applications are live.

questions.explanation: Migration Center is used to assess and discover the existing environment, while Migrate to Virtual Machines supports moving VM-based workloads to Compute Engine with a phased approach. This fits a low-risk migration plan with dependency visibility and limited downtime.

questions.question: A software team is moving a legacy application from a private hosting environment to Google Cloud. The application is now deployed with shell scripts on VMs, and the team wants repeatable releases, versioned artifacts, and automated promotion through dev, test, and production environments. They also need a managed pipeline that can connect source control to build and deployment steps with minimal operational overhead. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Source Repositories, Cloud Build, and Artifact Registry to build artifacts from source and deploy them through a pipeline to Compute Engine or GKE.
choices.B: Use Deployment Manager to package the application code, then use Cloud Functions to copy release files directly to each VM.
choices.C: Use Cloud Composer and BigQuery to orchestrate release steps and store build artifacts in Cloud Storage for manual promotion.
choices.D: Use Apigee and Cloud DNS to route traffic between environments while developers continue to deploy changes by SSH.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build provides a managed CI/CD system, Artifact Registry stores versioned artifacts, and Cloud Source Repositories can host the source code. This creates a repeatable deployment workflow for VM- or container-based targets.

questions.question: A media company needs to migrate 800 TB of archived video files from its data center to Google Cloud. The network link is too slow to complete the transfer within the migration window, and the team wants to avoid building custom transfer tooling.
