They need a secure, scalable way to send the data to Google Cloud and then load it into durable cloud storage for later processing. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Storage Transfer Service to pull the data directly from the on-premises file server over the existing internet connection.
choices.B: Use Transfer Appliance to move the datasets to Google Cloud, then import the data into Cloud Storage buckets.
choices.C: Use Pub/Sub and Dataflow to stream the archive files continuously into Cloud Storage without changing the source system.
choices.D: Use Filestore to stage the files in Google Cloud, then use Cloud SQL exports to ingest the archived video assets.

questions.explanation: Transfer Appliance is designed for very large offline data transfers when network bandwidth is insufficient. After arrival, the data can be imported into Cloud Storage for durable storage and later use.

questions.question: KnightMotives Automotive operates customer-facing vehicle configuration and dealer ordering systems that have become unreliable during peak sales periods. The company needs a consistent way to track incidents, assign responders, and maintain clear escalation paths because outages affect both dealers and customers. Teams are distributed across regions, and incident data is currently scattered across chat tools and email. They also want a repeatable process for root-cause analysis and post-incident follow-up. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring alerting policies only, and have teams coordinate incident updates in Google Chat.
choices.B: Create a Cloud Run service that receives alerts and stores incident notes in Cloud SQL for manual triage by the on-call team.
choices.C: Move the ordering application to App Engine standard and rely on automatic scaling to prevent incidents from occurring.
choices.D: Adopt Google Cloud’s incident management practices with Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and a centralized incident response runbook in Cloud Storage.

questions.explanation: A centralized incident process supported by Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging gives KnightMotives visibility, alerting, and operational context, while a runbook in Cloud Storage helps standardize response and postmortems.

questions.question: KnightMotives Automotive is modernizing its dealer ordering platform and wants better visibility into request latency, error rates, and service dependencies across multiple microservices. Leadership wants operators to quickly identify whether issues are caused by the web tier, APIs, or backend data services. The platform runs on Google Kubernetes Engine and includes services deployed in multiple regions. They need actionable dashboards and tracing without adding significant operational overhead. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy the services on Compute Engine with a shared persistent disk so that performance issues are easier to isolate.
choices.B: Use Cloud Armor policies to block slow requests and rely on load balancer logs for all application visibility.
choices.C: Export container logs to BigQuery and use Looker Studio dashboards as the primary source of service health.
choices.D: Instrument the application with Cloud Trace and Cloud Monitoring, and build dashboards for latency, availability, and service-to-service dependencies.

questions.explanation: Cloud Trace and Cloud Monitoring provide distributed tracing, metrics, and dashboards that help KnightMotives understand application performance and isolate failures across services.

questions.question: KnightMotives Automotive wants to reduce risk when releasing updates to its customer-facing vehicle build-and-order application. The business requires gradual rollout, fast rollback if errors increase, and a deployment approach that works across development, test, and production environments. The application is containerized and deployed in Google Cloud. Engineers want a managed approach that also supports automated traffic splitting. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Artifact Registry to store images and manually update each environment with kubectl from a developer workstation.
choices.B: Use Config Connector to create all services in production first and then copy the configuration to lower environments.
choices.C: Use Cloud Deploy to promote releases through environments and manage progressive delivery to Cloud Run or Google Kubernetes Engine.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build to package the container image and deploy it directly to Compute Engine instances using startup scripts.

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy is designed for managed release orchestration and progressive delivery, which fits KnightMotives’ need for controlled rollouts and quick rollback.

questions.question: KnightMotives Automotive is launching AI-enabled features for in-vehicle experiences and wants to validate how its systems behave during regional failures and traffic spikes before global rollout. The platform must remain available for dealers and customers, and leadership wants evidence that recovery objectives are being met. The solution should help engineers test failover scenarios in a controlled way using Google Cloud services. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud CDN to cache responses and assume the application will recover automatically during a regional outage.
choices.B: Create additional Cloud SQL replicas in every region and promote them manually during a failure test.
choices.C: Use Dataproc to replay production logs and measure recovery after an outage has already happened.
choices.D: Run controlled failure tests with Chaos Engineering patterns on Google Kubernetes Engine and validate failover using Cloud Monitoring.

questions.explanation: Controlled chaos testing on GKE, combined with Cloud Monitoring, helps KnightMotives validate resilience, failover behavior, and recovery objectives before production incidents occur.

questions.question: KnightMotives Automotive is seeing recurring incidents when its dealer portal exceeds memory thresholds during promotion launches. The operations team wants an automated response that can restart unhealthy workloads, notify responders, and record the event for later analysis. They want to minimize human intervention while preserving an audit trail. The workload runs on Google Cloud and already emits metrics and logs. What should you do?

choices.A: Send all logs to Pub/Sub and have a Cloud Run job periodically check for high memory usage.
choices.B: Create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy that triggers a Cloud Functions workflow to notify responders and restart the workload through Google Kubernetes Engine.
choices.C: Use Cloud DNS failover policies so that traffic automatically shifts away from the workload when memory rises.
choices.D: Move the application state to Memorystore for Redis and rely on cache eviction to clear memory pressure.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring alerts combined with Cloud Functions provide event-driven automated response, while GKE actions and logging support recovery and auditability.

questions.question: A retail company is preparing to launch a new customer-facing checkout service on Google Cloud. The team must define measurable uptime and latency targets before go-live, provide reporting for internal stakeholders, and use metrics that can be tracked over time. They also want a managed Google Cloud service that can help evaluate service health against those targets without building a custom dashboarding stack. What should you do?

choices.A: Create uptime checks in Cloud Monitoring and use Cloud Logging to alert on request failures, then write the service commitments in a shared document.
choices.B: Use Google Cloud Armor policies and BigQuery exports from Cloud Logging to produce weekly availability reports.
choices.C: Deploy Cloud Load Balancing with Cloud CDN and rely on autoscaling metrics in Compute Engine to infer service availability.
choices.D: Define SLOs using Cloud Monitoring service level objectives and service level indicators, then publish the service commitments as SLAs for stakeholders.

questions.explanation: SLOs and SLAs are the core governance tools for operational readiness. Cloud Monitoring and SLIs provide the managed measurement layer needed to define and track the targets over time.

questions.question: A media streaming company runs a microservices application on Google Kubernetes Engine and Compute Engine. After several incidents, the operations team wants real-time visibility into latency, errors, and saturation across services, plus centralized logs for faster troubleshooting. The team also wants to correlate metrics and logs without introducing a separate monitoring platform. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards and alerting together with Cloud Logging and Cloud Trace for centralized observability.
choices.B: Export all logs to BigQuery and use Looker Studio for operational dashboards and alerting.
choices.C: Use Cloud DNS health checks and Cloud Load Balancing logs to estimate application performance.
choices.D: Configure Memorystore and Cloud Storage lifecycle policies to retain operational data for incident review.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace are the core Google Cloud observability services for metrics, logs, and traces, and they work together for real-time operational insight.

questions.question: A gaming startup expects a major traffic spike during a product launch. The platform team needs to validate that the application can meet response-time goals under peak load before release, and they want the testing approach to reflect the deployed cloud architecture. The workload runs behind HTTP(S) Load Balancing and uses managed instance groups on Compute Engine. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Profiler to identify slow code paths and then rely on Cloud CDN to absorb the launch traffic.
choices.B: Use Cloud Monitoring alerting to watch CPU utilization during launch and scale after users report slowdowns.
choices.C: Use Cloud Build to run unit tests, then deploy directly to production with a canary rollout in Cloud Deploy.
choices.D: Use Cloud Load Balancing and Compute Engine with a third-party load testing tool such as Locust or JMeter to validate performance before release.

questions.explanation: Performance testing should be conducted before go-live using a load-testing approach that reflects the real deployment topology. Cloud Load Balancing and Compute Engine are the relevant target environment for this validation.

questions.question: A financial services team operates a batch-processing application on Google Cloud that peaks at the end of each business day. They want to avoid overprovisioning but still guarantee enough compute and storage capacity for predictable spikes. The team needs a Google Cloud-native approach to forecast demand and align infrastructure sizing with expected load patterns. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Armor rate limiting to reduce incoming requests during peak hours and keep resource usage low.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run with minimum instances and let automatic scaling absorb all peak demand without forecasting.
choices.C: Use Cloud VPN and Cloud Interconnect to improve throughput so the application can process more jobs without capacity changes.
choices.D: Use Cloud Monitoring metrics and historical utilization trends to forecast load, then adjust Compute Engine managed instance group sizing and Cloud Storage capacity plans.

questions.explanation: Operational readiness includes proactive capacity planning. Historical metrics in Cloud Monitoring can be used to forecast demand and right-size Compute Engine and Cloud Storage capacity.

questions.question: A healthcare organization is moving a patient portal to Google Cloud and wants to be confident it can be operated safely after launch. The team needs clear responsibilities for on-call support, standardized incident response, and a repeatable way to prepare for day-2 operations. They also want to align the operational model with reporting and governance. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards and Cloud Logging sinks as the primary mechanism for assigning operational responsibilities.
choices.B: Establish a CloudOps operating model with defined workforce roles, processes, tooling, and governance, and support it with Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging.
choices.C: Use Cloud Build triggers to automate deployments and rely on Artifact Registry permissions to define support ownership.
choices.D: Use Cloud Scheduler and Pub/Sub to distribute incident notifications and defer operational planning until after launch.

questions.explanation: Operational readiness in CloudOps includes workforce, processes, tooling, and governance. A defined CloudOps operating model supported by observability services is the best fit for day-2 operations.

questions.question: A media company runs a global video streaming application on Google Cloud and has recently launched a new recommendation service. The team needs to detect rising latency, error rates, and CPU saturation before users notice degraded playback, and they want a solution that works across logs, metrics, and traces. They also want a single place to set alerts and create dashboards for engineering and operations teams. What should you do?

choices.A: Deploy Cloud Run jobs to periodically probe the service and store the results in Cloud Storage for manual review.
choices.B: Use Cloud Load Balancing health checks and export VM metrics to BigQuery for offline analysis after incidents occur.
choices.C: Send application logs to Pub/Sub and use Dataflow to compute performance trends without configuring alerts.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Observability with Cloud Monitoring alerting, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace to track latency, error rates, and resource utilization in real time.

questions.explanation: Google Cloud Observability provides integrated monitoring across metrics, logs, and traces, and Cloud Monitoring alerts can notify the team before users are impacted.

questions.question: A financial services company migrated its trading platform to Google Cloud and now sees intermittent latency between services in different regions. The platform uses VPC networks, Cloud Load Balancing, and GKE, and the network team needs a visual way to identify high-latency paths and topology issues. They want to reduce the time spent correlating packet loss symptoms across logs from multiple projects. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Place the services behind Apigee and use API analytics to estimate network latency.
choices.B: Use Cloud Armor policies to block traffic from all regions except the primary production region.
choices.C: Enable Network Intelligence Center to analyze topology, connectivity, and network path performance across the VPC environment.
choices.D: Export VPC Flow Logs to BigQuery and build custom SQL reports for network path analysis.

questions.explanation: Network Intelligence Center is designed for network visualization and troubleshooting, including identifying high-latency paths and connectivity issues.

questions.question: An online retailer is refactoring its checkout application on Google Cloud and wants to continuously improve performance after each release. The product team needs measurable targets for response time, error rate, and throughput, and they want those targets to guide operational reviews and engineering priorities. The company also wants a repeatable way to decide whether a component needs optimization or redesign. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy to promote each release automatically and rely on rollback to handle any performance regression.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run and Artifact Registry to rebuild the checkout service with more frequent releases.
choices.C: Define SMART performance objectives and track them with Cloud Monitoring dashboards and alert policies for the checkout service.
choices.D: Store application KPIs in Firestore and review them manually in monthly meetings without setting thresholds.

questions.explanation: SMART goals paired with Cloud Monitoring dashboards and alerting create measurable performance targets and support continuous improvement.

questions.question: A SaaS provider runs a microservices application on GKE and notices that some services have increased response times after a feature launch. The team wants to identify CPU-heavy functions, memory pressure, and inefficient code paths without changing the application architecture. They need observability data that can help engineers pinpoint bottlenecks and reduce resource waste. How should you configure this?

choices.A: Enable Google Cloud Observability with Cloud Profiler and Cloud Monitoring to identify CPU, memory, and network consumption hotspots.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run request logs and Cloud SQL insights to infer the bottleneck from database query timings only.
choices.C: Mirror traffic with Traffic Director and analyze the mirrored requests in Cloud Storage.
choices.D: Move the workload to Compute Engine and use OS Login to manually inspect process performance on each VM.

questions.explanation: Cloud Profiler and Cloud Monitoring within Google Cloud Observability help identify resource hotspots and bottlenecks for continuous performance tuning.

questions.question: A gaming company wants to improve the user experience of its backend services on Google Cloud by combining technical performance data with product feedback. The engineering leaders want quantitative and qualitative indicators of user happiness, task success, and retention, alongside latency and error metrics. They plan to use the results in quarterly reviews to decide which services need redesign. What should you do?

choices.A: Collect feedback in Cloud Tasks and use Cloud Scheduler to send periodic reminders to users.
choices.B: Use Pub/Sub and Dataflow to aggregate logs into daily summaries without tracking user satisfaction.
choices.C: Instrument the application with Google Cloud Observability and measure user experience using HEART-style metrics stored in BigQuery.
choices.D: Use Looker Studio with Cloud SQL to report only server-side uptime and ignore user feedback data.

questions.explanation: Combining Google Cloud Observability with HEART-style metrics in BigQuery supports continuous improvement using both operational and user-centered signals.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is moving its customer-facing storefront to Google Cloud and wants to validate that the application can survive a regional outage with minimal downtime. The architecture uses a global external HTTP(S) load balancer, a GKE-based application tier in two regions, and Cloud SQL for transactional data replication. Operations must periodically test failover without breaking production traffic, and they need clear metrics on RTO and RPO during each test. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy to promote a new release to the secondary region first, then rely on Artifact Registry replication to provide regional failover for the storefront.
choices.B: Use Cloud Monitoring uptime checks and Cloud Logging alerts to observe the production environment, then manually switch DNS records to the secondary region when an outage occurs.
choices.C: Use a staging environment with the same global external HTTP(S) load balancer pattern, run controlled regional failover tests on the GKE services and Cloud SQL replicas, and record results in Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run jobs to replay traffic against the primary region and use Cloud Storage object versioning to recover application state after the test.

questions.explanation: A staging environment that mirrors production is the right place to test regional failover safely. Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging provide the visibility needed to measure RTO, RPO, and application behavior during the test.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail operates several microservices for product search, cart management, and order creation on Google Cloud. The team currently uses a mix of Grafana, Nagios, and Elastic, but incident response is slow because logs and metrics are not correlated across services. They want a unified approach for alerting, dashboards, and root-cause analysis across GKE and Cloud SQL, with minimal operational overhead. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Migrate all services to Compute Engine instance groups and use Pub/Sub to archive logs before sending notifications.
choices.B: Deploy Ops Agent on every virtual machine and store all application logs in BigQuery for manual querying during incidents.
choices.C: Configure Cloud Monitoring dashboards, Cloud Logging log-based metrics, and Cloud Trace to correlate service latency, error rates, and request paths across the application.
choices.D: Use Cloud Armor security policies and Identity-Aware Proxy to identify failing microservices and suppress alerts from noisy endpoints.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace together provide a unified observability stack for metrics, logs, and request tracing, which improves incident response and root-cause analysis.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail releases frequent updates to its customer-facing web application and wants to reduce the risk of production incidents. The application runs on GKE and uses a backend API on Cloud Run, and the company needs a standardized way to promote builds from test to production with rollback support. Releases must be observable, and failed deployments must be reverted quickly without affecting unrelated services. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Dataflow pipelines to validate release artifacts and use Secret Manager to overwrite the previous deployment when a rollback is needed.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to create container images, store them in Artifact Registry, and manage progressive promotion and rollback with Cloud Deploy to GKE and Cloud Run targets.
choices.C: Use Spanner change streams to synchronize application versions across environments and rely on Cloud Scheduler to trigger production deployments at off-peak hours.
choices.D: Use Migrate to Containers to rebuild the storefront after each release and rely on Cloud DNS to shift users back if a release fails.

questions.explanation: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, and Cloud Deploy provide a controlled CI/CD release process with promotion and rollback for GKE and Cloud Run, which fits the requirement for safer deployments.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to regularly validate that its product catalog and checkout services can tolerate component failures without impacting customers. The platform includes a multi-zone GKE cluster, Cloud SQL, and several internal APIs, and the operations team wants to simulate failures in a controlled way. They need a repeatable process that can test service health checks, autoscaling behavior, and recovery procedures before each major release. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Compute Engine instance templates to recreate the cluster nodes, then disable the external HTTP(S) load balancer during testing to confirm recovery.
choices.B: Use Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus with custom alerts to watch incidents, but rely on manual shutdown of production pods for failure simulation.
choices.C: Use Cloud Storage object lifecycle rules to force data rehydration and use Cloud CDN cache invalidation to simulate service failure scenarios.
choices.D: Use controlled chaos testing in a staging environment by terminating GKE pods or nodes, temporarily disabling dependent services, and monitoring recovery with Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging.

questions.explanation: Controlled chaos testing in staging is the best fit for validating resilience and recovery procedures. Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging let the team observe health checks, scaling, and recovery behavior during the test.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail has adopted SRE practices for its storefront and order processing systems on Google Cloud. Leadership wants availability targets for critical user journeys, including search, cart, and checkout, and they need alerts that page the on-call team when customer impact exceeds acceptable thresholds. The team also wants to measure service-level indicators directly from request traffic rather than from infrastructure-only metrics. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud Armor rate limiting and Security Command Center findings to page operators whenever requests exceed a configured threshold.
choices.B: Define Cloud DNS health checks for each service and send notifications from Cloud Functions when records stop resolving.
choices.C: Create Cloud Monitoring dashboards with service-level objectives, use Cloud Logging or Managed Service for Prometheus to derive SLIs from request latency and error rates, and configure alerting policies for SLO burn rate.
choices.D: Export application logs to BigQuery and use Looker Studio reports to notify the on-call team when daily traffic is lower than expected.

questions.explanation: SLOs, SLIs, and burn-rate alerting in Cloud Monitoring provide an SRE-aligned approach to measuring customer impact from request traffic and triggering timely alerts.

questions.question: A media company is launching a customer-facing streaming platform on Google Cloud. The platform must provide 99.9% monthly availability, response time under 200 ms for key API calls, and clear reporting for executives and customers. The operations team wants measurable targets that can be tracked over time with alerts when service quality begins to degrade. They also need a practical way to connect these commitments to their day-2 operations model. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a Cloud Run service and rely on Cloud Logging queries to judge whether the platform is meeting expectations.
choices.B: Use BigQuery and Looker Studio to build executive dashboards that estimate availability after incidents occur.
choices.C: Use Cloud Load Balancing with Cloud CDN and define availability goals only in a service document shared with customers.
choices.D: Define SLOs and SLIs in Cloud Monitoring for availability and latency, then configure alerting policies and SLO reporting for the streaming APIs.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring is the right tool for defining SLIs, tracking SLOs, and creating alerts and reports for service health and performance. This directly supports operational readiness and ongoing CloudOps management.

questions.question: A fintech company runs a payment processing application on Google Kubernetes Engine across multiple zones. The platform team needs real-time visibility into request latency, error rates, and infrastructure health so they can detect degradation before customers notice it. They also want centralized logs, metrics, and traces for both application and cluster components. The solution must support ongoing operations without adding custom monitoring infrastructure. What should you do?

choices.A: Send all application logs to Pub/Sub and analyze them only with Dataflow jobs for incident detection.
choices.B: Install Cloud Operations for GKE, including Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace, to collect metrics, logs, and traces centrally.
choices.C: Deploy a managed instance group behind a TCP proxy load balancer and export only VM metrics to Cloud Storage.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy to release new versions and review deployment status in the Google Cloud console.

questions.explanation: Cloud Operations for GKE provides native observability for GKE workloads, including metrics, logs, and traces. This gives the team centralized, real-time insight into application and cluster health.

questions.question: An e-commerce company expects traffic to triple during a seasonal promotion. The application runs on Compute Engine and Cloud SQL, and the team wants to validate whether the system can meet latency and throughput targets before launch. They need to simulate realistic user load, identify bottlenecks, and use the results to adjust capacity plans. The solution should fit a CloudOps process for pre-production readiness. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Create additional Compute Engine instances manually and verify capacity by watching CPU utilization in the console.
choices.B: Use Cloud Load Balancing with Cloud Monitoring dashboards and run load tests against the application to measure latency, error rates, and saturation before go-live.
choices.C: Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger synthetic requests and inspect the results in Cloud Logging after the promotion starts.
choices.D: Use Cloud Build to run unit tests, then increase Cloud SQL storage size to reduce response times.

questions.explanation: Performance testing should be done before launch using realistic load against the production-like environment, with results analyzed through Cloud Monitoring. This supports capacity planning and operational readiness.

questions.question: A logistics company has a fleet-management application on Google Cloud that experiences predictable spikes every weekday morning. The team wants to prevent performance issues while keeping costs controlled, and it prefers an approach that scales resources ahead of demand. They already collect request volume and CPU metrics in Google Cloud. The goal is to improve reliability during peak periods without overprovisioning all day. What should you do?

choices.A: Move the application to App Engine standard and rely on default instance scaling only.
choices.B: Use Cloud Storage lifecycle policies to preserve request logs and estimate future demand from object counts.
choices.C: Configure Cloud CDN to cache API responses and use the cached traffic pattern to size the backend.
choices.D: Use Compute Engine managed instance groups with autoscaling based on Cloud Monitoring metrics and scheduled scaling for the morning peak.

questions.explanation: Managed instance groups with autoscaling and scheduled scaling are appropriate for recurring demand spikes. Using Cloud Monitoring metrics helps align capacity with known usage patterns while controlling cost.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is preparing to move a patient portal to Google Cloud. The platform team must clearly define who owns incidents, who responds to alerts, and how service performance is reported after launch. They also need to ensure that the support model is ready before the migration cutover and that critical services have documented operating procedures. The organization wants this preparation to be part of its CloudOps framework. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Asset Inventory and IAM to document all resources and let each application team decide its own incident response process.
choices.B: Create a Cloud Build pipeline that deploys the portal and stores operational runbooks in Artifact Registry.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI to summarize postmortems and determine response ownership from historical tickets.
choices.D: Establish a CloudOps operating model with defined roles, escalation paths, runbooks, SLO reporting in Cloud Monitoring, and governance reviews.

questions.explanation: Operational readiness includes workforce, processes, tooling, and governance. Defining roles, escalation paths, runbooks, and SLO reporting is the best way to prepare for go-live and day-2 operations.

questions.question: A media streaming company runs a user-facing service on Google Cloud and has seen intermittent slowdowns after recent feature releases. The team wants to continuously detect latency, error-rate, and resource-utilization regressions without waiting for customer complaints. They need a solution that supports logs, metrics, tracing, and alerting across services so they can identify bottlenecks quickly and tune performance over time. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Cloud Observability with Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace to collect KPIs and create alerts for latency, throughput, error rates, and utilization.
choices.B: Use Cloud Logging sinks to BigQuery and run scheduled queries to identify slow requests, then use Cloud Run revision traffic splitting to mitigate regressions.
choices.C: Use Cloud Deploy to create canary releases and rely on Artifact Registry metadata to infer application performance trends.
choices.D: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards and uptime checks only, because visual alerts are sufficient to detect all application performance issues.

questions.explanation: Continuous performance improvement requires centralized observability. Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace provide metrics, logs, and tracing so the team can detect regressions, troubleshoot bottlenecks, and alert on KPI thresholds.

questions.question: An e-commerce company uses multiple VPCs and hybrid connectivity to serve customers in several regions. Users in one geography report higher checkout latency, but application logs do not show errors. The network team needs to visualize the topology, identify high-latency paths, and determine whether the issue is in the cloud network or the path to a partner system. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Use Cloud DNS policies and Cloud NAT logging to change egress routing for all traffic, then review the results in Cloud Monitoring.
choices.B: Use Network Intelligence Center to analyze the network topology and latency, then use Cloud Logging to correlate network events with application timestamps.
choices.C: Use VPC Service Controls and Access Context Manager to isolate the checkout service, then measure latency with BigQuery exports.
choices.D: Use Cloud Armor adaptive protection and reCAPTCHA Enterprise to inspect requests, then block traffic from the affected region.

questions.explanation: Network Intelligence Center is designed to visualize network topology and help identify high-latency paths. Cloud Logging can complement the analysis by correlating network and application timing.

questions.question: A SaaS provider has a Java application on Google Cloud that is consuming unexpected CPU and memory during peak traffic. The team already has metrics, but they need deeper insight into which code paths are inefficient so they can improve performance and reduce infrastructure cost. They want a managed approach that helps uncover CPU hotspots and memory usage patterns over time. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards and alerting policies to raise the VM size whenever CPU utilization exceeds the threshold.
choices.B: Use Cloud Profiler to continuously profile the application and identify CPU and memory bottlenecks in production.
choices.C: Use Cloud Logging log-based metrics to estimate hot functions and then store the results in Firestore for review.
choices.D: Use Cloud Run request concurrency controls and autoscaling policies to reduce CPU use, then review cost reports in Cloud Billing.

questions.explanation: Cloud Profiler is intended for continuously analyzing runtime CPU and memory behavior in production. It helps identify inefficient code paths and resource bottlenecks without requiring manual code instrumentation.

questions.question: A fintech startup is defining an operations program for its payment API on Google Cloud. Leadership wants measurable performance objectives that align with business goals, such as checkout success rate, request latency, and resource efficiency. The team must track progress over time and use the data to decide when parts of the architecture need redesign. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Define quotas in Service Usage, use Cloud DNS routing policies, and monitor certificate expirations in Certificate Manager.
choices.B: Define SMART performance goals, track KPIs such as latency, throughput, and error rates in Cloud Monitoring, and review trends for redesign decisions.
choices.C: Define deployment windows in Cloud Deploy, use Binary Authorization, and compare container image sizes in Artifact Registry.
choices.D: Define folder-level IAM policies, use Cloud KMS key rotation, and review access logs to assess whether performance is improving.

questions.explanation: The guidance is to set clear, measurable performance objectives and monitor KPIs such as latency, throughput, error rates, and resource utilization. Cloud Monitoring is the right service for tracking those trends over time.

questions.question: A consumer app team wants to improve user experience after launch, but they have already instrumented their system with standard cloud metrics. Product managers want a structured way to combine quantitative usage data with qualitative user feedback so they can prioritize performance improvements over the next quarter. They also want a repeatable framework that helps teams discuss adoption, retention, and task success. What should you do?

choices.A: Use the HEART framework with feedback collected from product surveys and operational metrics in Google Cloud Observability to track happiness, engagement, adoption, retention, and task success.
choices.B: Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards and Cloud Trace spans only, because technical telemetry is enough to measure user satisfaction.
choices.C: Use BigQuery ML to classify user complaints, then use Cloud Scheduler to email the results to developers weekly.
choices.D: Use Cloud Deploy canary analysis and Cloud Build test reports to score user happiness from release pipelines.

questions.explanation: The HEART framework is explicitly recommended for measuring user feedback across happiness, engagement, adoption, retention, and task success. Combining that with observability data supports continuous improvement decisions.

questions.question: A media company is redesigning its video-streaming platform on Google Cloud after several costly overinvestments in high availability. The business wants the service to stay highly available during peak events, but the team has a limited budget and needs room to release features quickly. The platform has two critical user-facing services and several supporting batch jobs, and the company has defined RTO and RPO targets for outages. The architecture team wants a reliability target that reflects user expectations without aiming for perfect uptime. What should you do?

choices.A: Set a 100% uptime goal for all Google Cloud workloads and deploy the platform across multiple regions with Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud SQL cross-region replicas, and Cloud Storage dual-region buckets.
choices.B: Reduce reliability spending by moving the platform to a single Compute Engine zone with Managed Instance Groups and Cloud SQL, then use Cloud Monitoring alerts to compensate for the lower availability.
choices.C: Define a realistic uptime target such as 99.9% or 99.99% for the critical user-facing services, use RTO and RPO to prioritize recovery design, and accept lower reliability targets for batch jobs on Google Kubernetes Engine and Cloud Scheduler.
choices.D: Set the same 99.99% uptime target for every component and use Cloud Run, Memorystore for Redis, and BigQuery so that all services receive equal reliability investment.

questions.explanation: A realistic reliability target should be based on user expectations, business value, and recovery objectives. Critical services deserve stronger reliability design, while less critical components can tolerate more failure. This approach balances reliability and cost and avoids pursuing unnecessary 100% uptime.

questions.question: A retail startup runs an online checkout service on Google Cloud and has recently introduced frequent feature releases. Users are satisfied with the current response times, but the platform team wants a formal way to decide when to slow down releases and focus on reliability work. Leadership also wants the team to reserve budget for innovation instead of constantly overengineering uptime. The team needs an approach that acknowledges that some failures are inevitable and helps balance release velocity with stability. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deploy canary releases and Cloud Build approvals to enforce a fixed two-week release freeze for every change, regardless of current service health.
choices.B: Replicate the checkout service in multiple projects with VPC Service Controls and Cloud Key Management Service to remove all risk of outages before releasing any new features.
choices.C: Move the checkout service to App Engine standard and Cloud Firestore to eliminate the need for release controls and ensure the service can always accept traffic.
choices.D: Use Cloud Monitoring uptime checks and custom dashboards to track an error budget for the checkout service, then pause feature releases and focus on reliability improvements when the budget is exhausted.

questions.explanation: Error budgets provide a practical way to accept some failure while protecting user happiness. When the budget is consumed, the team should slow down release velocity and invest in reliability improvements. Cloud Monitoring is the appropriate service for measuring and alerting on service health and uptime objectives.

questions.question: A healthcare analytics provider runs a patient portal, an API backend, and several reporting pipelines on Google Cloud. The portal must remain highly available during clinic hours, but the reporting pipelines can tolerate occasional delays as long as patient records are eventually processed. The company has a limited reliability budget and wants to focus redundancy on the parts of the system that affect users the most. They also want to avoid applying the same expensive redundancy pattern to every component. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Place all services in a single regional Managed Instance Group and use Cloud Monitoring alerting to detect issues before they affect users.
choices.B: Store all application data in Cloud Storage Nearline and use Pub/Sub as the primary serving layer so every component gets the same reliability design.
choices.C: Prioritize high availability for the patient portal and API backend with regional Cloud Load Balancing and GKE multi-zone clusters, while keeping reporting pipelines on Cloud Run Jobs or Cloud Composer with lower availability requirements.
choices.D: Use Cloud DNS with failover routing and create active-active deployments for every pipeline, API, and portal component across three regions.

questions.explanation: The key is to prioritize reliability for the most critical components and accept more failure tolerance for less critical ones. User-facing services should receive stronger availability design, while batch or reporting workloads can have lower resilience requirements. This aligns spending with business impact.

questions.question: A financial services company is moving its claims-processing application to Google Cloud and must document recovery objectives for an audit. The application can tolerate a short interruption, but data loss beyond a few minutes would violate internal policy. The team is considering several backup and failover options and wants the design to reflect the true business cost of outages. They need a solution that uses recovery time objective and recovery point objective to choose an appropriate reliability level within budget. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Storage Archive for all application data and rely on Cloud Monitoring alerts to restore the application manually after a regional outage.
choices.B: Define the target RTO and RPO first, then select a recovery design such as Cloud SQL cross-region replicas, Cloud Storage versioning, and Backup and DR Service to meet those objectives within budget.
choices.C: Implement Cloud Armor and Identity-Aware Proxy to protect the application from attacks, since security controls also determine the acceptable RTO and RPO.
choices.D: Deploy the application on Cloud Run only and use Pub/Sub retention to avoid the need to define formal recovery objectives.

questions.explanation: RTO and RPO should drive the recovery architecture, not the other way around. Once those objectives are known, the team can choose appropriate backup, replication, and failover services that meet the business requirement without overbuilding.

questions.question: A SaaS company is considering whether to add a third regional deployment for its collaboration platform on Google Cloud. The current two-region design already meets most user needs, but the platform team wants to know whether the added reliability is worth the extra cost. The product manager says users are happy today unless there is a major regional failure, and finance wants the team to avoid spending money on reliability that does not noticeably improve the experience. The team needs a method to compare incremental reliability gains against cost and risk. Which solution should you recommend?

choices.A: Add a third region by default and use Cloud Load Balancing, Spanner, and Cloud Storage dual-region buckets to maximize uptime regardless of cost.
choices.B: Keep the current two-region architecture, measure customer impact with Cloud Monitoring and logging, and perform a cost-benefit analysis before adding more redundancy or failover capacity.
choices.C: Move the platform to one region with a larger GKE cluster and use Horizontal Pod Autoscaling to make the design cheaper and more reliable at the same time.
choices.D: Use Cloud CDN and Cloud Armor to absorb outages from the application tier so the company does not need to evaluate the cost of higher reliability.

questions.explanation: Reliability should be improved only when the incremental benefit justifies the additional cost and complexity. A cost-benefit analysis helps decide whether extra regions or failover capacity are worthwhile based on actual user impact and business risk.

questions.question: A retail company runs a microservices application on GKE and Compute Engine across two regions. The operations team needs to detect potential failures before customers are impacted, and they want to track request latency, error rate, and service dependency issues. They also need a single place to analyze logs, metrics, and traces, and they want to minimize manual instrumentation work while using native Google Cloud services. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace to collect service metrics, logs, and traces, then create custom metrics and alerting policies for key SLIs.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run, Cloud Tasks, and Cloud Load Balancing to route traffic away from unhealthy services and rely on built-in autoscaling.
choices.C: Use BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and Pub/Sub to archive application events and run scheduled queries to identify failures after they occur.
choices.D: Use Cloud CDN, Cloud Armor, and VPC Flow Logs to capture traffic patterns and block requests that correlate with failures.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, and Cloud Trace provide the observability stack needed to proactively detect potential failures using metrics, logs, and traces. Custom metrics and alerting policies let the team track SLIs such as latency and error rate and respond before customers are affected.

questions.topic: Logging cost optimization and alert prioritization
questions.question: A media platform uses Cloud Run and GKE for its production workloads and has enabled extensive logging for troubleshooting. The platform's SRE team notices that logging costs are rising quickly, and many alerts are firing for low-priority events that do not require immediate response. They want to keep the logs needed for incident response, reduce storage costs, and focus alerts on the most important reliability signals. What should you do?

choices.A: Export all logs to BigQuery, increase log retention in Cloud Logging, and create more alerting policies for every available metric.
choices.B: Enable VPC Flow Logs on all subnets, use Cloud Armor policies for alerting, and store the results in Cloud Storage for long-term analysis.
choices.C: Move application logs to Pub/Sub, process them with Dataflow, and send summarized reports to Looker Studio for daily review.
choices.D: Configure exclusion filters on Cloud Logging log sinks to drop low-value logs, keep critical logs in Cloud Logging, and tune Cloud Monitoring alert thresholds to focus on key metrics.

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging exclusion filters reduce log storage costs by preventing low-value logs from being stored, while Cloud Monitoring alert thresholds can be tuned to prioritize critical metrics and reduce alert fatigue. This matches the need to preserve useful incident-response data and focus on the most important signals.

questions.topic: Zero trust network access and segmentation
questions.question: A company is moving sensitive workloads to Google Cloud and wants to reduce reliance on the network perimeter. Users must access internal applications only after identity and device posture are verified, and different application tiers must be isolated while keeping centralized network management. Which two Google Cloud services or products should the architect recommend? (Choose two)

choices.A: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)
choices.B: Shared VPC
choices.C: Cloud DNS
choices.D: Cloud Router

questions.explanation: IAP enforces access based on identity and context rather than network location, which aligns with zero trust. Shared VPC is used to segment workloads while maintaining centralized network management.

questions.topic: Protecting services and preventing data exfiltration
questions.question: A regulated organization needs to protect internet-facing applications from DDoS and web attacks, and also prevent data exfiltration from sensitive Google Cloud services. The security team wants Google-managed controls that enforce policy close to the edge and around sensitive resources. Which two services should the architect propose? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Google Cloud Armor
choices.B: VPC Service Controls
choices.C: Cloud VPN
choices.D: Cloud Load Balancing

questions.explanation: Cloud Armor provides DDoS and web application protection at the edge. VPC Service Controls helps create security perimeters around sensitive data and services to reduce the risk of data exfiltration.

questions.topic: Zero trust network connectivity and outbound control
questions.question: A company has GKE workloads that must securely access Google Cloud APIs and also make controlled outbound connections to third-party services. The solution must avoid exposing traffic to the public internet where possible and support explicit egress control from GKE. Which two Google Cloud services or products should be used? (Choose two)

choices.A: Private Service Connect
choices.B: Cloud Service Mesh egress gateways
choices.C: Cloud VPN
choices.D: Cloud Run

questions.explanation: Private Service Connect provides private access to Google Cloud services and APIs. Cloud Service Mesh egress gateways help secure and control outbound access from GKE and related workloads.

questions.question: A media company needs to modernize a latency-sensitive application. The frontend must run close to users at the edge, while the backend processing should remain in a regional Google Cloud environment. The team wants a hybrid architecture pattern that distributes application components to the environment best suited for each function. Which two Google Cloud products should you use? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Cloud CDN
choices.B: Cloud Run
choices.C: Cloud Armor
choices.D: Google Kubernetes Engine

questions.explanation: A distributed hybrid pattern fits when different application components run in different environments based on their function. Cloud Run can host backend services or APIs in Google Cloud, and Google Kubernetes Engine can run containerized application tiers that need regional deployment and orchestration. The other options are important services, but they are not the best fit for placing distinct application components in a distributed hybrid architecture.

questions.question: A financial services company wants to replicate an existing on-premises application environment into Google Cloud for development, testing, and disaster recovery. The solution must use a redundant architecture pattern and allow the same application stack to run in multiple environments. Which two Google Cloud products should you use? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Compute Engine
choices.B: Cloud Functions
choices.C: Google Kubernetes Engine
choices.D: BigQuery

questions.explanation: Redundant architecture patterns deploy the same applications and components in multiple environments to increase resiliency or replicate an environment for testing. Compute Engine is suitable for lifting and shifting server-based workloads, and Google Kubernetes Engine is suitable for running the same containerized application stack in another environment. Cloud Functions, BigQuery, and Pub/Sub are useful services, but they do not directly represent a redundant full application environment in this scenario.

questions.question: An enterprise wants to build a global customer-facing application that can tolerate regional failures and still provide consistent access across multiple locations. The architecture must span more than one Google Cloud region and support a broad failure domain for the application. Which two Google Cloud products should you choose to help implement this design? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Cloud Load Balancing
choices.B: Cloud Storage
choices.C: Cloud SQL
choices.D: Cloud VPN

questions.explanation: A global or multi-regional deployment archetype is used when the application must operate across broader failure domains and remain available during regional issues. Cloud Load Balancing helps distribute traffic globally, and Cloud Storage supports multi-region or dual-region data placement for durable access across locations. Cloud SQL, Cloud VPN, and Cloud Armor are relevant services, but they do not by themselves provide the broad multi-region deployment foundation described here.

questions.topic: GKE best practices - large clusters and scalability
questions.question: A retail company is designing a Google Kubernetes Engine platform that must support rapid growth during seasonal peaks, with hundreds of services and strict requirements to avoid hitting scalability limits in a single cluster. The team wants to follow GKE best practices for planning a large environment. Which two actions should they take? (Choose two)

choices.A: Use multiple Google Kubernetes Engine clusters, each scoped to a business domain or environment, instead of placing all workloads in one cluster
choices.B: Place all workloads in one Google Kubernetes Engine Autopilot cluster to simplify management and maximize pod density
choices.C: Plan the cluster topology and node pool strategy using Google Cloud Load Balancing and Google Kubernetes Engine quotas to reduce scaling bottlenecks
choices.D: Use Cloud Run for Anthos to run the entire multi-service platform without Kubernetes cluster planning
choices.E: Disable horizontal autoscaling and rely only on manual node upgrades in Google Kubernetes Engine Standard

questions.explanation: For large GKE environments, a common best practice is to avoid overloading a single cluster and instead use multiple clusters aligned to domains or environments. It is also important to plan topology, node pools, and quota limits so the platform can scale reliably without bottlenecks.

questions.topic: GKE best practices - hardening and RBAC
questions.question: A financial services team is deploying sensitive workloads on Google Kubernetes Engine. They need to reduce the attack surface, enforce least privilege for administrators and developers, and improve cluster security posture. Which two actions should they take? (Choose two)

choices.A: Use Google Kubernetes Engine role-based access control (RBAC) to grant users and service accounts only the permissions they need
choices.B: Expose the Kubernetes API server publicly and allow broad access so troubleshooting is easier
choices.C: Enable Google Kubernetes Engine security features such as private clusters and Workload Identity to harden access to the cluster and Google Cloud services
choices.D: Store all application secrets in local Docker images so they are available during startup
choices.E: Use Compute Engine instance metadata broadly from every Pod instead of identity-based access controls

questions.explanation: GKE security best practices emphasize least privilege and hardening. RBAC is used to control Kubernetes permissions, while features like private clusters and Workload Identity help reduce exposure and improve identity management.

questions.topic: GKE best practices - upgrades and CI/CD
questions.question: An e-commerce platform runs critical services on Google Kubernetes Engine Standard. The operations team wants to make cluster upgrades smooth and predictable while also reducing manual deployment mistakes during application releases. Which two practices should they adopt? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Use Google Kubernetes Engine upgrade strategies and schedule cluster upgrades during maintenance windows to minimize disruption
choices.B: Manually patch nodes one by one outside of Google Kubernetes Engine maintenance controls to avoid version skew
choices.C: Implement Cloud Build and Cloud Deploy for a managed CI/CD pipeline to automate application delivery to Google Kubernetes Engine
choices.D: Deploy application changes directly from Cloud Shell to production Pods to shorten release time
choices.E: Turn off release automation and use kubectl apply from a developer laptop for every deployment

questions.explanation: Operational best practices for GKE include planning and controlling cluster upgrades to reduce disruption, and using a CI/CD system to automate safe, repeatable application releases. Cloud Build and Cloud Deploy are appropriate Google Cloud services for this workflow.

questions.topic: Resource redundancy across zones and regions
questions.question: A fintech application on Google Cloud must remain available during a zone outage and should avoid any single point of failure for its web and application tiers. The team wants a design that automatically keeps serving traffic if one zone becomes unavailable. Which two actions best support this goal? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Deploy the application on a regional Managed Instance Group across multiple zones
choices.B: Place all application VMs in a single zone but use Cloud Scheduler for retries
choices.C: Use an external Application Load Balancer in front of the application tier
choices.D: Store all user sessions on a single Compute Engine VM with a persistent disk
choices.E: Use Cloud CDN only, without any backend redundancy

questions.explanation: A regional Managed Instance Group replicates instances across zones and supports automatic failover within the region. An external Application Load Balancer can route traffic to healthy backends and help maintain availability when a zone fails.

questions.question: A media company runs a critical service on Google Cloud and wants to quickly detect regional service issues and validate that failover will work before a real outage occurs. They need operational tools aligned with a redundancy strategy. Which two choices best meet this need? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Use Google Cloud Service Health dashboard to monitor Google Cloud service incidents by region
choices.B: Use Personalized Service Health to view incidents relevant to the project
choices.C: Use Cloud Storage lifecycle rules to trigger alerts on zone failures
choices.D: Use BigQuery reservations to detect unhealthy backends
choices.E: Use Cloud Pub/Sub subscriptions to simulate an outage without changing architecture

questions.explanation: The Service Health dashboard shows the status of Google Cloud services across regions, and Personalized Service Health provides project-relevant incidents. These tools help detect issues promptly so the team can respond quickly in a redundancy design.

questions.topic: Testing failover scenarios for redundant systems
questions.question: A retail platform is deployed across multiple zones and must prove that traffic can fail over correctly during a zone outage. The team wants to regularly test its redundancy plan with minimal redesign. Which two Google Cloud actions should they use? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Simulate a zone outage for a regional Managed Instance Group
choices.B: Simulate a zone failure in a GKE regional cluster
choices.C: Convert the application to a single-zone unmanaged instance group
choices.D: Disable health checks on the Cloud Load Balancer to avoid false positives
choices.E: Move the application state to a single zonal Persistent Disk

questions.explanation: Both simulations are explicitly recommended ways to test failover behavior in redundant architectures. They validate whether the system can continue operating when a zone becomes unavailable.

questions.topic: Defense-in-depth controls for an enterprise foundation
questions.question: A platform team is building a new Google Cloud landing zone for regulated workloads. They need to prevent risky resource configurations before deployment and also detect malicious behavior after resources are created. Which two capabilities best match the enterprise foundations blueprint? (Choose two)

choices.A: Organization Policy Service constraints
choices.B: Cloud Build policy-as-code checks in the CI/CD pipeline
choices.C: Cloud DNS private zones
choices.D: Cloud NAT

questions.explanation: The blueprint uses prevention controls such as Organization Policy Service constraints and IaC/policy-as-code validation in Cloud Build. These help block risky configurations before deployment, which is central to the defense-in-depth model described in the foundation.

questions.topic: Enterprise foundation resource management and deployment model
questions.question: An organization wants to manage Google Cloud foundation resources in a repeatable way with code review and approval, while ensuring changes are deployed through a controlled automation process. Which two services should be used? (Choose 2)

choices.A: Terraform
choices.B: Cloud Build
choices.C: Cloud Asset Inventory
choices.D: Cloud Scheduler

questions.explanation: The blueprint recommends a GitOps model where declarative infrastructure is written in Terraform and deployed through Cloud Build as the CI/CD automation tool. Together, they support reviewable, controlled provisioning of foundation resources.

questions.question: A security operations team needs centralized visibility across the organization and wants to integrate Google Cloud with the company’s existing identity provider for user lifecycle and single sign-on. Which two blueprint components should they rely on? (Choose two)

choices.A: Cloud Logging aggregated log sinks
choices.B: Cloud Identity synchronization with an external identity provider
choices.C: Cloud Spanner multi-region instances
choices.D: Cloud Load Balancing

questions.explanation: The blueprint calls for aggregated log sinks in Cloud Logging to centralize security and audit logs, and Cloud Identity synchronized from the existing identity provider for account lifecycle management and SSO. These support operational visibility and centralized access control.

questions.question: A logistics company is building a Cloud Bigtable solution to store billions of package scan events from thousands of depots worldwide. The application must support very fast lookups for the latest scans by depot, avoid write hotspots during peak sorting periods, and return recent events first for operators. The team expects the workload to grow quickly and wants a schema that supports efficient reads without changing the storage layer later. What should you do?

choices.A: Use a randomly generated UUID as the row key and partition the data into separate BigQuery tables by depot.
choices.B: Store the events in Cloud SQL with an indexed depot ID column and use read replicas for faster operator queries.
choices.C: Use an auto-incrementing scan ID as the row key and store each scan attribute in a separate Cloud Bigtable column family.
choices.D: Use a row key composed of depot ID followed by a reverse timestamp, and store scan attributes in a small number of column families in Cloud Bigtable.

questions.explanation: A Bigtable schema should avoid monotonically increasing keys and should match common access patterns. A depot ID plus reverse timestamp supports recent-first reads while spreading writes across key space, and keeping column families small is a good Bigtable design practice.

questions.question: A manufacturing company is collecting sensor readings from factory equipment every second and storing them in Cloud Bigtable for near-real-time dashboards and historical analysis. Operators usually query the most recent readings for a single machine or a small group of machines, and the team wants to minimize scan latency. They also need to keep the schema narrow because each reading contains only a few attributes. What should you do?

choices.A: Use a row key built from machine ID and reverse timestamp, and keep the sensor measurements in one or two column families in Cloud Bigtable.
choices.B: Use a row key built from reverse timestamp only, and create a separate Bigtable table for each machine type.
choices.C: Use Cloud Spanner with a composite primary key on machine ID and timestamp to support all dashboard queries.
choices.D: Use BigQuery partitioned by ingestion time and cluster by machine ID for all operational dashboard reads.

questions.explanation: For time-series data in Bigtable, a row key based on the entity identifier plus reverse timestamp supports efficient retrieval of the newest readings and distributes data well. Keeping the schema narrow with a small number of column families aligns with Bigtable best practices.

questions.question: A fintech startup is using Cloud Bigtable to store transaction events for fraud monitoring. The current design uses a timestamp as the row key, and traffic has increased sharply during market open, causing latency spikes in one cluster. The team wants to preserve low-latency reads for recent transactions and reduce hotspotting without introducing a relational database. What should you do?

choices.A: Change the row key to include a transaction time bucket and account identifier, then use Cloud Bigtable replication across clusters for availability and read locality.
choices.B: Move the workload to Cloud SQL and add a secondary index on transaction time for faster fraud queries.
choices.C: Keep the timestamp row key and add more Bigtable column families so the data is spread across additional storage paths.
choices.D: Migrate the hot dataset to Cloud Memorystore for Redis and use it as the primary system of record.

questions.explanation: Monotonically increasing row keys can create hotspots in Bigtable. Adding a time bucket with an identifier helps distribute writes, and multi-cluster replication improves availability and read locality for a low-latency operational workload.

questions.question: A media analytics team is designing Cloud Bigtable to store viewer interaction records for recommendation features. Each record contains a small set of frequently queried attributes and a larger set of optional metadata that is rarely accessed. The team wants to optimize read performance for the common queries and keep the schema manageable as new metadata fields are added. What should you do?

choices.A: Write all records to BigQuery and let analysts query the optional metadata from nested repeated fields.
choices.B: Place frequently accessed attributes in one Cloud Bigtable column family and rarely used metadata in another small number of column families.
choices.C: Store all fields in a single JSON column in Cloud SQL to simplify later application changes.
choices.D: Create a separate Bigtable column family for each metadata field so schema changes can be isolated independently.

questions.explanation: Bigtable performs best when related columns are grouped into a small number of column families. Separating hot attributes from rarely used metadata helps optimize read performance while keeping the schema flexible.

questions.question: A sports analytics company uses Cloud Bigtable to store player tracking data for an application that shows the most recent movements for a specific player and the last 10 minutes of activity for a team. The team has identified that the dominant access patterns are player-level and team-level reads, and they want a schema that minimizes full-table scans. The data must support billions of rows and be easy to query by these common patterns. What should you do?

choices.A: Store the tracking records in Cloud Firestore and use collection group queries for recent activity.
choices.B: Create row keys that start with the player ID or team ID, followed by a reverse timestamp, and store the tracking points in Cloud Bigtable.
choices.C: Use Cloud Spanner with separate tables for players and teams, and join them in the application for each request.
choices.D: Use a globally sorted timestamp row key and rely on Bigtable filters to locate player and team data at query time.

questions.explanation: Bigtable row keys should match the most common query patterns. Using player or team identifiers with reverse timestamps allows efficient point and range reads without scanning unrelated rows.

questions.question: A global e-commerce company is storing operational feature data in Cloud Bigtable for personalization and needs high availability across regions. Their application reads data close to each user, and the engineering team wants to minimize schema-related operational complexity as new features are added over time. They expect mostly single-row updates and do not need cross-row consistency. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery replicated datasets and update feature values with scheduled batch jobs.
choices.B: Use Cloud Bigtable multi-cluster replication and keep the schema narrow with a small set of column families for the feature store.
choices.C: Use Cloud SQL cross-region replicas and map each feature to a separate normalized table.
choices.D: Use Cloud Firestore in multi-region mode and model each feature as a top-level collection.

questions.explanation: Bigtable replication across clusters provides high availability and read locality for global workloads. A narrow schema with a small number of column families is well suited to feature-store style access patterns and single-row updates.

questions.question: A media company is giving an external auditor read-only access to several Cloud Storage buckets that contain financial records. The auditor must have access for exactly 30 days, and the access must be limited to buckets used for the audit project only. The security team wants to avoid custom scripts that revoke access later and wants the policy to be easy to review in IAM. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant the auditor roles/storage.admin on the buckets and use Organization Policy to prevent changes after the audit ends.
choices.B: Create a dedicated service account for the auditor, grant it Storage Object Viewer, and use Cloud Scheduler with Cloud Functions to remove the binding after 30 days.
choices.C: Grant the auditor Storage Object Viewer on the project and use Cloud Storage lifecycle rules to expire the objects after 30 days.
choices.D: Add an IAM binding for Storage Object Viewer with a CEL condition that expires after 30 days and matches only the audit bucket names.

questions.explanation: IAM Conditions are the right fit because they can enforce time-based and resource-based access directly in the IAM binding using CEL, without custom revocation automation.

questions.question: A healthcare provider has a partner team that needs temporary access to only BigQuery datasets tagged for the migration project. The access should be allowed only during the migration window, and the security team wants a single policy that is easy to audit. They already use Organization Policy constraints for other controls and want this requirement to fit into the existing layered security approach. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery dataset ACLs and Cloud Scheduler to update permissions every day during the migration.
choices.B: Grant BigQuery Data Viewer with an IAM condition that checks the dataset tag and an expiration timestamp in CEL.
choices.C: Create a separate project for the partner team, migrate the datasets there, and grant BigQuery Data Viewer at the project level.
choices.D: Use Cloud Identity groups for the partner team and remove them from the group when the migration window ends.

questions.explanation: An IAM condition can restrict access to tagged resources and limit it to a time window, while still working cleanly with Organization Policy as part of layered controls.

questions.question: A retail company has an SRE team that needs permission to view Cloud Logging logs for only one production application during a two-week incident review. The team should not be able to access logs from other applications or retain the access after the review period. The security manager wants the solution to use native Google Cloud controls rather than a custom access-removal workflow. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Asset Inventory to generate a report of log access and manually revoke the role after the incident review.
choices.B: Grant Logging Viewer at the organization level and restrict log queries by using Cloud Logging log scopes.
choices.C: Create a new Cloud Logging sink for the application and grant the SRE team Viewer access to the destination project.
choices.D: Create a role binding for Logging Viewer with an IAM condition that limits access to the specific log resource name and expires after two weeks.

questions.explanation: IAM Conditions allow you to scope the binding to a specific resource and enforce a time limit, which is the most direct native approach for temporary, limited log access.

questions.question: A software company uses labels to distinguish production and nonproduction Compute Engine instances. A contractor must be allowed to start and stop only the nonproduction instances for the next 14 days, and the security team does not want the contractor to access production resources even if the role is granted at the project level. The solution must be enforced in IAM and be straightforward for auditors to understand. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant Compute Instance Admin (v1) on the project and use VPC Service Controls to isolate production instances.
choices.B: Grant compute.instanceAdmin on the project with an IAM condition that allows only instances with the nonproduction label and expires after 14 days.
choices.C: Create a separate folder for nonproduction instances and move the contractor accounts there temporarily.
choices.D: Grant the contractor a custom role with start and stop permissions and remove the custom role after 14 days.

questions.explanation: A conditional IAM binding can filter access by resource attributes such as labels and also enforce an expiration date, which matches the requirement precisely.

questions.question: An insurance company wants internal developers to deploy artifacts only to Cloud Run services in the staging environment, and only for a limited release window each month. The platform team already assigns roles through IAM and wants the policy to be enforced consistently without adding deployment-time scripts. The deployment targets are identifiable by resource names and environment tags. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant the developers Artifact Registry Reader so they can deploy to staging services only.
choices.B: Create a separate Cloud Run service account for staging and rotate its keys after each release window.
choices.C: Create an IAM binding for Cloud Run Developer with a condition that matches staging resource names or tags and limits access to the release window.
choices.D: Grant the developers Cloud Run Admin on the project and use Cloud Build triggers to block production deployments.

questions.explanation: IAM Conditions are ideal for restricting access to specific resources and time windows, and they avoid the complexity of custom deployment scripts or key rotation schemes.

questions.question: A consulting firm needs to let a vendor support engineer read only selected Secret Manager secrets for a specific customer engagement. The access should stop automatically at the end of the engagement, and the security team wants the secrets to remain in the same project with no extra manual cleanup. They also want a policy design that can be combined with Organization Policy restrictions later. What should you do?

choices.A: Grant Secret Manager Secret Accessor with an IAM condition that restricts the secrets by name and expires at the end of the engagement.
choices.B: Move the secrets into a new project and grant the vendor engineer Owner temporarily.
choices.C: Create a temporary service account for the vendor engineer and delete it after the engagement ends.
choices.D: Use Cloud Scheduler to disable the Secret Manager API at the end of the engagement.

questions.explanation: A conditional IAM binding can limit access to specific secrets and automatically expire the permission, which fits the stated operational and security requirements.

questions.question: A retail company wants to add a customer-support chatbot in a few weeks and has no machine learning team available to train a model from scratch. The solution must support natural language responses, minimize infrastructure management, and allow the team to start with a pre-trained foundation model. They also want a Google Cloud-managed service that can be evaluated before committing to custom model development. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery ML to train a text model directly from the customer conversation data stored in BigQuery.
choices.B: Use Vertex AI Pipelines to build a custom training workflow and deploy the resulting model to Vertex AI Endpoints.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Model Garden to deploy a pre-trained Gemini model and integrate it with the application through Vertex AI APIs.
choices.D: Use AutoML Natural Language to train a custom classification model on labeled support tickets.

questions.explanation: The company needs a pre-trained foundation model with minimal setup and no custom training requirement, which fits Vertex AI Model Garden. Vertex AI Endpoints, AutoML, and BigQuery ML would be better if the goal were custom model training rather than quickly deploying an existing model.

questions.question: A logistics company has a demand-forecasting model that must return predictions in under 100 milliseconds for a customer-facing application. Traffic fluctuates throughout the day, and the platform team wants Google Cloud to handle scaling without building custom serving infrastructure. They also need to keep the model available continuously rather than running scheduled scoring jobs. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Vertex AI Pipelines to schedule model retraining and publish the model artifacts to Cloud Storage.
choices.B: Use BigQuery ML to run prediction queries directly against the forecast tables in BigQuery.
