choices.C: Deploy the model to a Vertex AI Endpoint and enable autoscaling for online predictions.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI Batch Prediction to generate forecast files every hour and load them into BigQuery.

questions.explanation: Low-latency, continuously available predictions with variable traffic are best served by Vertex AI Endpoints with autoscaling. Batch prediction is better for offline workloads, while Pipelines and BigQuery ML do not satisfy the real-time serving requirement as directly.

questions.question: A healthcare analytics team needs a repeatable machine learning workflow that includes data validation, feature engineering, training, evaluation, and deployment approvals. They must be able to rerun the same process for each new dataset and keep a clear record of each step for audit purposes. The team wants a managed orchestration service that supports reproducible ML pipelines. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery ML to run all preprocessing, training, and deployment logic inside SQL scripts.
choices.B: Use Vertex AI Pipelines with Kubeflow components to orchestrate the end-to-end ML workflow.
choices.C: Use Colab Enterprise notebooks to run each pipeline step interactively and manually deploy models to Vertex AI.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI Feature Store to centralize features and let analysts manually retrain models from notebooks.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Pipelines is designed for repeatable, auditable ML workflows with orchestration across validation, training, evaluation, and deployment. Feature Store, Colab Enterprise, and BigQuery ML address parts of the workflow but do not provide full managed pipeline orchestration.

questions.question: An e-commerce company has a fraud detection model that is giving inconsistent results between training and production. The data science team discovered that some features are computed differently in the offline training environment than in the online serving system. They need a centralized service to store and serve the same features consistently for both training and inference. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Vertex AI Pipelines to rebuild the feature values separately for every training job.
choices.B: Use BigQuery ML so training and prediction both happen inside BigQuery with SQL.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Model Garden to deploy a fraud detection foundation model and skip feature engineering.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI Feature Store to manage shared features for both offline training and online serving.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Feature Store is intended to keep feature definitions and values consistent across training and serving, reducing training-serving skew. Model Garden, BigQuery ML, and Pipelines do not directly solve centralized feature consistency.

questions.question: A manufacturing company wants to classify product images for quality inspection, but its team has limited machine learning expertise and labeled examples are available. The business wants a managed service that can train a custom image model without requiring the engineers to design architectures or tune algorithms manually. They also want to deploy the model for future use in their production line. What should you do?

choices.A: Use BigQuery ML to train an image classification model from image metadata stored in SQL tables.
choices.B: Use Vertex AI AutoML Vision to train and deploy a custom image classification model.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Pipelines to hand-code a TensorFlow training workflow and deploy it on Compute Engine.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI Model Garden to select a general-purpose image model and perform only prompt-based inference.

questions.explanation: AutoML Vision is appropriate when a team needs a managed custom image model with limited ML expertise. Model Garden is for pre-trained models, BigQuery ML is not suited to this image use case, and Pipelines is for orchestrating workflows rather than replacing model training expertise.

questions.question: A finance team stores all of its transaction data in BigQuery and wants to build a simple churn prediction model without moving data into a separate ML platform. The analysts are comfortable with SQL and prefer to manage the model using queries rather than a notebook-based workflow. They do not need advanced model serving features or custom training infrastructure. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Vertex AI Endpoints to deploy a custom model trained in Colab Enterprise.
choices.B: Use BigQuery ML to train and score the model directly in BigQuery with SQL.
choices.C: Use Vertex AI Model Garden to deploy a foundation model and fine-tune it on the transaction data.
choices.D: Use Vertex AI Feature Store to export the transaction features and then train a model in Vertex AI Pipelines.

questions.explanation: BigQuery ML is the best fit when data is already in BigQuery and the team wants a straightforward model with minimal data movement. Vertex AI services are better for more advanced ML workflows, custom serving, or foundation-model use cases.

questions.topic: Private Service Connect vs VPC Peering
questions.question: A media company is migrating a partner-facing analytics platform to Google Cloud. The platform must let external partners connect privately to a published service without exposing the producer VPC, and several partners use overlapping IP ranges that cannot be renumbered in the near term. The solution must avoid broad network visibility across environments and should support adding more partners later with minimal routing changes. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Shared VPC to place the producer service and partner workloads in the same centralized network so they can communicate through internal IP addresses.
choices.B: Publish the service through Private Service Connect and have each partner create PSC endpoints in their own VPCs to reach the service privately.
choices.C: Deploy Cloud VPN tunnels between the producer VPC and each partner environment so the partners can reach the service over private connectivity.
choices.D: Create VPC Network Peering between the producer VPC and each partner VPC so traffic can use internal IP addresses across the private network.

questions.explanation: Private Service Connect is the right choice because the partners have overlapping IP ranges and only need private access to a specific service, not full network visibility. VPC Network Peering requires non-overlapping ranges and exposes broader network reachability. Shared VPC is for resources within one organization, and Cloud VPN is unnecessary for this service-consumption pattern.

questions.topic: Private Service Connect vs VPC Peering
questions.question: A healthcare provider runs an internal application in one VPC and a billing system in a second VPC owned by the same organization. Both VPCs have distinct, non-overlapping CIDR ranges, and each team needs full private network access to the other system for multiple internal services. The network architects want a simple design that allows direct communication without creating individual service endpoints. What should you do?

choices.A: Configure VPC Network Peering between the two VPC networks so both sides can use internal IP addresses across the private connection.
choices.B: Create a Shared VPC and move both systems into the same host project so the applications can use the same network.
choices.C: Use Private Service Connect endpoints for each application service so the teams can exchange traffic through consumer endpoints.
choices.D: Set up Cloud Interconnect between the two VPC networks so the systems can communicate privately at layer 3.

questions.explanation: VPC Network Peering fits because the two VPCs need broad internal connectivity and their IP ranges do not overlap. Private Service Connect is better for consuming specific services rather than full VPC-to-VPC access. Shared VPC applies to centralized networking within one organization and requires a different landing-zone design, while Cloud Interconnect is for on-premises connectivity.

questions.topic: Private Service Connect vs VPC Peering
questions.question: A fintech company has three isolated application VPCs that must all access a shared fraud-detection API. The application VPCs use overlapping RFC1918 address ranges because they were created by separate acquisition teams, and the security team does not want any of those VPCs to see each other’s full address space. The company wants a private design that can be extended to more VPCs later without redesigning routes. What should you do?

choices.A: Publish the fraud-detection API with Private Service Connect and let each application VPC create its own private endpoint.
choices.B: Connect the VPCs with VPC Network Peering and advertise the API subnet to each environment through custom routes.
choices.C: Place the API in a Shared VPC and migrate all application teams into the same host project to eliminate the overlap.
choices.D: Expose the fraud-detection API through Cloud Load Balancing and require public internet access with Cloud Armor protection.

questions.explanation: Private Service Connect is appropriate because the VPCs have overlapping IP ranges and only need private access to a shared service. VPC Network Peering cannot work with overlapping ranges and would expose broader routing than desired. Shared VPC is an organizational model, not a solution for separately managed overlapping networks, and public load balancing does not meet the private-access requirement.

questions.topic: Private Service Connect vs VPC Peering
questions.question: A software vendor hosts a managed SaaS backend in Google Cloud and wants customer environments to reach the service privately. Each customer has a separate VPC, and several customers use the same CIDR blocks, so direct VPC-to-VPC routing is not possible. The vendor wants customers to connect only to the SaaS service, not to any other backend subnets or internal systems. What should you do?

choices.A: Create a Shared VPC and attach all customer projects to it so the backend is reachable through shared networking.
choices.B: Use Private Service Connect so each customer VPC can consume the SaaS backend through a private endpoint.
choices.C: Use VPC Network Peering from each customer VPC to the SaaS VPC so internal IP traffic can flow between all networks.
choices.D: Deploy Cloud VPN gateways for each customer so the SaaS backend can be reached over encrypted tunnels.

questions.explanation: Private Service Connect is designed for exposing a specific service privately to multiple consumer VPCs, including cases with overlapping IP ranges. VPC Network Peering would require non-overlapping CIDRs and would expose more of the network than the vendor wants. Shared VPC is not suitable for separate customer environments, and Cloud VPN is a broader connectivity pattern than needed here.

questions.topic: Private Service Connect vs VPC Peering
questions.question: A retail company is designing connectivity between a payment processing VPC and an inventory VPC in the same Google Cloud organization. The VPCs have non-overlapping IP ranges, and both teams need bidirectional access to many internal services, not just a single published endpoint. The architects also want the simplest architecture that supports normal private routing without creating consumer-side endpoints. What should you do?

choices.A: Configure Private Service Connect endpoints in each VPC and publish all internal services as service attachments.
choices.B: Implement Shared VPC for the two teams so both applications can use a common network and avoid routing complexity.
choices.C: Use Cloud Router with Cloud NAT so each VPC can reach the other privately through translated internal addresses.
choices.D: Set up VPC Network Peering between the two VPCs so they can communicate using internal IP addresses.

questions.explanation: VPC Network Peering is the best fit when two VPCs need broad internal connectivity and their IP ranges do not overlap. Private Service Connect is better for publishing and consuming specific services, not general VPC-to-VPC communication. Cloud NAT is for outbound internet access, and Shared VPC is a centralized network model within one organization rather than a direct inter-VPC connectivity mechanism.

questions.question: A software company is standardizing its infrastructure as code practices for a new platform team. The team must provision Google Cloud resources in development, staging, and production, and the same templates will later be used to create equivalent networking and compute resources in AWS for a second region. The engineers already use a shared Git-based workflow and want a tool that can manage resources across multiple cloud providers from one configuration. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Cloud Deployment Manager with YAML templates because it is a native Google Cloud IaC tool and can be extended to cover AWS resources.
choices.B: Use Cloud Build to run gcloud commands in a pipeline because it can provision resources in both Google Cloud and AWS without an IaC tool.
choices.C: Use Terraform with Google Cloud official modules and a remote state backend in Cloud Storage so the team can manage Google Cloud and AWS resources from the same HCL configuration.
choices.D: Use Config Connector to declare the infrastructure because it can manage Google Cloud and AWS resources from Kubernetes manifests.

questions.explanation: Terraform is the best choice when the same infrastructure definition must span multiple cloud providers. Deployment Manager is Google Cloud only, and Config Connector is Kubernetes-native rather than a general multi-cloud IaC tool.

questions.question: A startup is building a repeatable environment for a single Google Cloud application. The platform team wants to define VPC networks, instance templates, and managed instance groups using a Google Cloud-native templating system. They do not need to manage any non-Google Cloud resources, and they prefer a solution that is free to use. The team also wants to reuse Jinja2-based templates that were created by an earlier group. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Deployment Pipelines with gcloud CLI scripts because scripts are the recommended native template system for Google Cloud resources.
choices.B: Use Anthos Config Management because it is designed for Google Cloud-only resources and directly replaces Jinja2 templating.
choices.C: Adopt Terraform with HashiCorp Cloud Platform state management because it is the only Google Cloud-native option that supports Jinja2 templates.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Deployment Manager with YAML and Jinja2 templates to provision only Google Cloud resources from a native IaC workflow.

questions.explanation: Deployment Manager fits a Google Cloud-only environment and supports YAML and Jinja2 templating. Terraform would also work, but the question emphasizes Google Cloud-native templates and a single-cloud scope.

questions.question: An enterprise platform team manages infrastructure for applications deployed on Google Cloud, AWS, and Microsoft Azure. They need version-controlled templates, consistent naming conventions, and the ability to enforce the same network and IAM baseline in every environment. The team already has Terraform expertise and wants to minimize the number of tools used for IaC. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Google Cloud Deployment Manager because it provides simpler templates for multi-cloud environments and can target AWS and Azure through providers.
choices.B: Use Config Connector because it gives a single configuration model for Google Cloud, AWS, and Azure resources.
choices.C: Use Terraform with Google Cloud provider modules and remote state in Cloud Storage to manage the shared baseline across all three cloud providers.
choices.D: Use Google Cloud Blueprints with Deployment Manager templates because blueprints can manage infrastructure across multiple clouds.

questions.explanation: Terraform is the preferred IaC tool for multi-cloud standardization and for teams that already use Terraform. Deployment Manager is limited to Google Cloud resources, while Config Connector is not a general multi-cloud solution.

questions.question: A company is modernizing its platform automation and wants to reduce the long-term risk of using an IaC product that may not be actively invested in by the vendor. The team currently manages only Google Cloud resources, but the roadmap includes possible expansion to another public cloud within the next year. They want an approach that is widely adopted, supports stateful deployments, and can grow without rewriting all infrastructure code. What should you do?

choices.A: Keep using Google Cloud Deployment Manager because it is the most future-proof option for Google Cloud and will automatically extend to other providers.
choices.B: Replace IaC with Cloud Build deployment scripts because scripts avoid the need to choose between Deployment Manager and Terraform.
choices.C: Move to Terraform and store state in Cloud Storage so the team can keep a single IaC workflow as the environment expands to additional cloud providers.
choices.D: Adopt Google Cloud Config Connector because it is the standard way to future-proof non-Kubernetes infrastructure on Google Cloud.

questions.explanation: Terraform is the better strategic choice when future multi-cloud expansion is likely and the team wants a broadly adopted tool with remote state management. Deployment Manager is Google Cloud only and is being gradually deprecated.

questions.topic: Cloud Run traffic splitting and revisions
questions.question: A retail company is migrating an internal order API to Cloud Run. The team wants each release to create an immutable deployment artifact, send only 10% of traffic to the new version at first, and be able to instantly send all traffic back to the last stable version if error rates rise. They also want to keep the application simple without introducing Kubernetes management overhead. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Cloud Functions with multiple function versions and a global external HTTP(S) load balancer to shift a percentage of traffic during testing.
choices.B: Deploy the service to Cloud Run and use traffic splitting between revisions to route 90% of requests to the stable revision and 10% to the new revision, then shift traffic gradually after validation.
choices.C: Deploy the service on Compute Engine managed instance groups and use Cloud Load Balancing with backend weights to simulate canary releases.
choices.D: Deploy the application on GKE Autopilot and use a Google Cloud load balancer to distribute 10% of traffic to a separate Kubernetes Deployment for the new version.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run creates a new revision for each deployment, and traffic splitting between revisions is the native way to perform canary releases and quick rollbacks without Kubernetes complexity.

questions.question: A media startup runs a customer-facing API on Cloud Run. A new deployment is causing elevated 5xx responses, and the operations team needs the fastest possible rollback while preserving the prior working container image and configuration. The service must remain fully managed and support future canary releases. What should you do?

choices.A: Delete the faulty Cloud Run service and create a new Cloud Run service that points to the previous Docker image.
choices.B: Redeploy the previous container image to Cloud Run and recreate the service so all users return to the stable version.
choices.C: Update the Cloud Run service to route 100% of traffic back to the previous revision and keep the failed revision available for analysis.
choices.D: Move the workload to GKE and use a rollback of the Kubernetes Deployment to restore the prior version.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run revisions are immutable, and the quickest rollback is to change traffic allocation so 100% of traffic returns to the previous healthy revision.

questions.topic: Cloud Run minimum instances and traffic management
questions.question: A fintech company exposes a low-latency HTTP service on Cloud Run. The service has unpredictable traffic spikes, but p95 latency must stay consistently low for the first request after periods of idle time. The team also wants to canary a new revision with 5% of traffic before full rollout. What should you do?

choices.A: Increase the Cloud Run maximum instances setting only, because autoscaling will keep the service warm during idle periods and enable canary testing.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run jobs with a scheduled trigger so requests are processed continuously and latency stays low during deployment changes.
choices.C: Configure Cloud Run minimum instances for the service and use revision traffic splitting to send 5% of requests to the new revision.
choices.D: Deploy the service to GKE and set a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler to maintain warm pods while using a service mesh for traffic shifting.

questions.explanation: Minimum instances help avoid cold starts for latency-sensitive Cloud Run services, and revision traffic splitting supports gradual canary rollout.

questions.topic: Cloud Run jobs versus services and revision rollout
questions.question: A logistics team is building a workload that receives HTTP requests from partners and must also run nightly batch processing to reconcile shipments. The HTTP portion needs gradual release management with immediate rollback, while the batch process should run to completion without serving traffic. The team wants to keep the platform simple and managed by Google Cloud. What should you do?

choices.A: Use App Engine standard for the HTTP API and Cloud Run jobs for batch processing to avoid revision management.
choices.B: Use Cloud Run services for the HTTP API with revision-based traffic splitting, and use Cloud Run jobs for the nightly batch processing.
choices.C: Use Cloud Run jobs for both the HTTP API and the batch processing so that all workload types share the same execution model.
choices.D: Use GKE for the HTTP API and Cloud Functions for the batch process because both support autoscaling and versioned releases.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run services are designed for HTTP request handling and support revisions and traffic splitting, while Cloud Run jobs are intended for run-to-completion batch workloads.

questions.question: A gaming company runs a Compute Engine fleet that processes match telemetry every few minutes and rebuilds derived aggregates from the original source data if a node fails. The workload needs the lowest possible latency and highest IOPS during processing, but all intermediate data can be discarded when a VM is stopped or restarted. The team does not need the disk to be used as a boot disk, and they want the simplest design for temporary scratch space. What should you do?

choices.A: Attach Local SSD to the Compute Engine instances and use it for temporary processing data and caches.
choices.B: Use pd-ssd Persistent Disk and create snapshots after each processing run to protect the intermediate data.
choices.C: Use Hyperdisk Balanced to provision exact performance while preserving data after maintenance events.
choices.D: Create a larger Persistent Disk and use it for the processing data so that performance scales with capacity.

questions.explanation: Local SSD is the best choice when the workload needs the highest IOPS and lowest latency and can tolerate data loss on stop, delete, or maintenance. It is intended for scratch space, caches, and other temporary data, not durable storage.

questions.question: A financial services company runs an on-premises migration of a PostgreSQL database on Compute Engine. The database must survive VM restarts and host maintenance, and the storage team wants to expand capacity without taking the VM down. The workload is write-heavy, but the team can tolerate somewhat lower peak IOPS than Local SSD as long as data remains durable. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Local SSD for the database volume because it provides the lowest latency for write-heavy workloads.
choices.B: Use pd-ssd Persistent Disk and resize the disk online when the database needs more capacity.
choices.C: Use pd-standard Persistent Disk because it is the least expensive option for durable database storage.
choices.D: Use Local SSD with a Cloud Storage bucket for periodic exports to preserve the data.

questions.explanation: Persistent Disk is the correct choice for durable database storage because it survives VM restarts and maintenance, and it can be resized without stopping the VM. Pd-ssd offers solid performance while maintaining durability, making it more appropriate than Local SSD for this database.

questions.question: A media company uses Compute Engine to transcode large video files. The transcoding nodes need very high temporary write performance for intermediate frames, but the final outputs are stored in Cloud Storage after the job completes. If a worker VM is reclaimed during maintenance, the job can simply be restarted from the original input. The team wants to keep costs lower than using the largest Persistent Disk sizes just to gain performance. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Hyperdisk Extreme as the boot disk and store all intermediate frames on it for maximum performance.
choices.B: Use pd-balanced Persistent Disk and increase the disk size to improve throughput for the transcode job.
choices.C: Use Filestore Enterprise for the intermediate frames so the workers can share the same scratch space.
choices.D: Use Local SSD for the intermediate frames and write the final video outputs to Cloud Storage.

questions.explanation: Local SSD fits temporary, high-performance scratch data used during compute-intensive processing. Since the intermediate frames can be discarded and the final outputs are stored elsewhere, durability is not required for the scratch space.

questions.question: A healthcare analytics team runs batch jobs on Compute Engine that need fast local staging storage, but the results must be retained even if a worker VM is deleted or the host undergoes maintenance. The team also wants the option to provision specific IOPS and throughput independently of capacity for future workloads. The current design must keep the staging area durable enough for retries while avoiding unnecessary overprovisioning. What should you do?

choices.A: Use Hyperdisk to provision the required performance and capacity separately for the staging data.
choices.B: Use Local SSD because it delivers the highest IOPS and then replicate the staged data to another VM.
choices.C: Use pd-ssd Persistent Disk because it is the only disk type that supports durable staging data.
choices.D: Use pd-standard Persistent Disk and size the disk very large to gain more throughput.

questions.explanation: Hyperdisk is the best choice when the requirement is to decouple performance from capacity and provision exact IOPS and throughput. It provides durable storage, unlike Local SSD, while avoiding the need to oversize a Persistent Disk just to get more performance.
